title: PyTorch issue 20888
body:
## 🐛 Bug

Shuffling a huge tensor by reindexing into it results in all of the values past a certain point being set to 0 when the reindexing is done in GPU memory.

@colesbury  This likely relates to https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/20562 as the issue is also with reindexing and also shows up at the same point in the tensor.  Hopefully this helps with narrowing the other issue.

I initially thought this was limited to longtensors, but if you use a floattensor with 2x values you run into the exact same issue in the exact same place. 

## To Reproduce

Steps to reproduce the behavior:

1. Create a huge longtensor (>536870700 values) of random values.  I went with 15Mx45
2. Load it into gpu memory
3. Reindex to shuffle the values

Data past ~536870700 will be all 0.  The data is zero'd mid tensor.

Here's a notebook that reproduces the issue:
https://github.com/EvenOldridge/HugeTensor/blob/master/Huge%20Tensor%20Bug%20-%20Data%20Loss.ipynb

## Expected behavior

Data should not be lost when reindexing

## Environment

- PyTorch Version (e.g., 1.0): 1.01
- OS (e.g., Linux): Linux (Ubuntu 16.04)
- How you installed PyTorch (conda, pip, source): pip
- Python version: 3.6
- CUDA/cuDNN version: 10.0
- GPU models and configuration: TeslaV100 32 Gig (on a DGX-1)
comments:
[ngimel]: Slight correction - data is not zero'd, data is untouched. 
```
import torch                                                                                                                                 
features = torch.empty(15000000, 45, device="cuda", dtype=torch.long).random_(0, 2**22)                                                                                  
ind=torch.randperm(len(features))                                                                                                            
tmp = torch.ones(15000000,50,device="cuda", dtype=torch.long)
del tmp
features3=features[ind]                                                                                                                      
print( features3[0])
print(features3[14999999] )
```
will print ones for the last row. 
Turns out OffsetCalculator by default will produce uint32_t offsets that are insufficient to address tensors that are more than 2**32 bytes (offsets are calculated in bytes). We should probably fix indexing type depending on the tensor size (splitting tensor into parts and looping over them is probably not practical for indexing, but I don't have a good mental picture of how indexing works, it still might be possible).

---

[colesbury]: I'll look at this today. @ngimel do you have any insight into #20562?

---

[ngimel]: No. Isolated repro would help, I think. It might be that some data dependent operations are unhappy with the wrong results produced by indexing, so may be fixing this will automatically fix #20562.

---

[EvenOldridge]: Thank you both so much for such a quick fix!  You rock!

title: PyTorch issue 21261
body:
## 🐛 Bug

In `tools/pyi/gen_pyi.py` there is no type hint provided for `Tensor.contiguous()` (`Tensor.is_contiguous()` is there however).  As a result uses of `.contiguous()` cause Mypy linting errors.  

In the case I had the line (the context and details of the tensors doesn't matter):

```
re_batched = seq_first.contiguous().view(expanded_tokens.size(1), -1).transpose(0, 1)
```

gives the linting error:

```
error: "Tensor" has no attribute "contiguous"; maybe "is_contiguous"?
```

## To Reproduce

Any use of `Tensor.contiguous` will produce this when linted with MyPy

## Expected behavior

No error should be reported

## Environment

PyTorch Version (e.g., 1.0): 1.1.0
OS (e.g., Linux): MacOS
How you installed PyTorch (conda, pip, source): conda
Build command you used (if compiling from source): N/A
Python version: 3.6
CUDA/cuDNN version: N/A
GPU models and configuration: None
Any other relevant information: None
comments:
[ezyang]: `contiguous` is marked `SKIP_PYTHON_BINDINGS` so it needs to get a handwritten declaration in `unsorted_function_hints.update`. @sdraper-CS do you think you could submit a patch? Add me as reviewer.

---

[sdraper-CS]: @ezyang  I would, but it seems someone beat me to it!  Next time I'll construct a pull request directly if the fix is obvious.  Thanks for the prompt update though

---

[shihongzhi]: @sdraper-CS   I seen this issue,  then fixed this bug last week. Thank you for your bug report.

title: PyTorch issue 21344
body:
@kuttas @pietern and I had a discussion on how to make DDP failure recoverable. The process involves the following steps:

```python
m = SomeModel()
dist.init_process_group()
ddp = DistributedDataParallel(m)
# got error
dist.destroy_process_group()
dist.init_process_group()
del ddp
ddp = DistributedDataParallel(m)
```

This does not work in today's DDP. Currently, to get better performance, DDP assigns the [original module](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/fa4ca4e70e98fbe944d1435929ea4fd17c7bed7d/torch/nn/parallel/distributed.py#L316) to the first module replica instead of creating a new one. Then, it creates a new `Reducer` to [add post hooks](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/fa4ca4e70e98fbe944d1435929ea4fd17c7bed7d/torch/csrc/distributed/c10d/reducer.cpp#L108-L112) to sync params. However, because every reconstructed DDP instance wraps the same original module, all their reducers will add hooks to the same set of variables. Hence, after 10 recoveries, each param (variable) in the original module will have 11 hooks introduced by 11 different reducers, where only the last one is still alive. We thought about several potential solutions:

#### Solution 1: Force Module Replication

Force DDP to create new module replicas instead of using the original module. In this way, those variables in the replicas will die together with the DDP instance. But it will make the DDP slower. Maybe make it an option?

#### Solution 2: Delete Hooks in Destructor

I feel the best way would be deleting those hooks from model variables when destructing a `Reducer`, but I didn't find a clean way to do that. The [`add_post_hook`](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/fa4ca4e70e98fbe944d1435929ea4fd17c7bed7d/torch/csrc/autograd/function.h#L250) function takes unique parameters, and we can get those hooks through [`post_hooks`](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/fa4ca4e70e98fbe944d1435929ea4fd17c7bed7d/torch/csrc/autograd/function.h#L254). Directly looping through the the hooks vector and find the target to delete seems to be too hackish.

#### Solution 3: Create New Variables (?)

Not sure if this can work. Instead of creating replica (as in Solution 1), let DDP create a new variable for every parameter in the original module. All DDP forward and backward pass will use those new variables. I think this won't work if the application only wraps part of the model using DDP, because there will be two disjoint autograd graphs (?)


@soumith @gchanan @ezyang  thoughts?
comments:
[mdlockyer]: I recently posted about this in the discussion forum [here](https://discuss.pytorch.org/t/safely-removing-a-module-from-ddp/45753). This issue actually both answers my question and confirms what I suspected was the case when either removing the module, or in this case, trying to redo the DDP wrapping. The idea of removing the hooks during destruction of the reducer would make DDP much more flexible as well. It would not only solve the situation described in this issue, but would also allow for a module to be removed from DDP with something like:

    model = ddp_model.module
    del ddp

I'm currently working on an architecture that would benefit greatly from the ability to easily remove the module from DDP. Right now there is a lot of extra complexity in my codebase just to deal with this.

---

[kuttas]: @MichaelDylan77  what are you doing to work around it currently? Re-create the inner model and transfer the parameters over?

The usage pattern you have above is similar to the use case I have. I need to be able to re-create the DDP module-wrapper with a new set of worker machines and keep training.

---

[mdlockyer]: That's exactly right. I have to re-instantiate the model and copy params. It's a pain right now. It's nice to know someone else has a use case in which they would need to extract the model from DDP 👍. It seemed like an edge case.

---

[mrshenli]: ## Trying Solution 2

#21534 seems addressed the problem but in quite a dirty way. A better solution might need to satisfy the following requirements:

1. As mentioned by @pietern, the hook deletion function should be implemented in `torch/csrc/autograd/function.h`, as it owns the data. 
2. We should not slow down existing use cases of `add_post_hook` and `post_hooks()`.

I initially thought about using an `OrderedDict` to store named hooks, as what we did for params, buffers, and children in `nn/Module.h`, but that would violate the second requirement.

~Another possibility is that, instead of using the default deleter, we create a special deleter for the hook unique ptr in DDP, e.g., `ReducerHookDeleter`, that wraps the default deleter.  The `add_post_hook` and `post_hooks()` APIs would stay the same, then we add one `delete_post_hook<DeleterType>()` API to `torch/csrc/autograd/function.h`, which loops over all post hooks, and only delete the ones with matching deleter type, i.e., `ReducerHookDeleter`. This would be slow, but is OK, as we only need this on failures, where timeout delay will dominate. Any comments?~ Should be able to directly check hook pointer type.

---

[pietern]: @MichaelDylan77 Could you give the code in #21591 a try and see if it works for you?

---

[mdlockyer]: @pietern I’ll test it out this afternoon!

---

[mdlockyer]: Everything seems to work fine on my end. No obvious issues. Memory usage looks relatively constant after removing and re-wrapping the model in DDP

---

[pietern]: @MichaelDylan77 Thanks for confirming it works for you!

---

[knagrecha]: Same issue is now coming up for FSDP - but I suspect it'll be a harder fix there due to how FSDP recursively injects itself. Can't just pull it out in this way.

title: PyTorch issue 21821
body:
#21785 showed that `at::svd()` is not stream-safe.  `at::svd()` uses `magma_dgesdd()`.

According to  http://icl.cs.utk.edu/magma/forum/viewtopic.php?f=2&t=1080, the old way of making MAGMA functions stream-safe is:

```
lock()
magmablasSetKernelStream( s1 )
magma_dgemm( ... )
unlock() 
```

The new way is using a `magma_queue_t`, but not all functions have it.  In particular `magma_dgesdd()` doesn't appear to have this parameter.


I'm not sure if either of these methods can be integrated into `pytorch`.  Meanwhile, the functions should probably raise an error if a non-default stream is detected.
comments:
[vishwakftw]: Another related thread: http://icl.cs.utk.edu/magma/forum/viewtopic.php?f=2&t=1034

---

[ngimel]: The problem is not so much with a stream safety, as with magma not setting a stream at all (e.g. the failing test is not multithreaded, so the locks in the old way that are supposed to provide stream safety under multithreaded operation are irrelevant). Rather than throwing an error on encountering non-default stream, I think it is better to synchronize default stream (on which magma would run its kernels) with the current pytorch stream. It is unfortunate that it seems to be impossible to set streams to magma kernels directly.

---

[vishwakftw]: For all of the batched kernels in MAGMA, a `magma_queue_t` object is taken as an argument, which is constructed using the current stream, cuBLAS and cuSparse handles. However, all MAGMA functions don’t support batching.

---

[ezyang]: Comments from @skrah:

> Scoped the issue and tried Natalia's suggestion, so far without success. Documentation for old-style Magma stream safety is poor. Issue seems magically fixed in some cases after port of batched_svd.

---

[skrah]: Yes, I just want to explain the "magically fixed" part as briefly as possible. I'm not working on `pytorch` this week so there may be some inaccuracies.

I could not replicate #21638 (i.e. the problematic test passed with cuda-memcheck and the stream tests re-enabled).

I'm not sure if @vishwakftw has taken extra steps to ensure stream safety in the `svd` port, but I didn't notice anything explicit.

So perhaps the memory allocation in

https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/896cd1c51071a13a465e3c54ef7c49abf4f1d0c0/aten/src/ATen/native/cuda/BatchLinearAlgebra.cu#L1347

provides some extra safety (I suspect that it performs an implicit synchronization) that makes the issue harder to trigger or indeed eliminates the issue.

---

[vishwakftw]: To echo @skrah, I didn't take care of any stream safety issues during the port, so just could have been the fact that multiple SVD calls were replaced by a single one.

title: PyTorch issue 21935
body:
The output of Maxpool has an unexpected size for some corner cases.

Repro:
Maxpool1d(kernel_size=3, stride=2, padding=0, dilation=2)
input=torch.randn(1,1,4)

If I use the L_out formula from the documentation (https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/nn.html?highlight=maxpool#torch.nn.MaxPool1d) to compute the output size, I get {1,1,0}.
However, the output generated has a size of {1, 1, 1}.

And with ONNX, the same operation results in an output of shape {1,1,0}
(https://github.com/microsoft/onnxruntime/issues/1224).

Since this case is not mentioned in the documentation, I was wondering if this is a special case that is handled separately by design (to avoid returning an output with a dim of 0), or if it is a bug.
comments:
[jignparm]: Any updates on this? It seems like the PyTorch implementation doesn't match the documentation for Maxpool1d, at least in the expected output shape computation.

---

[ailzhang]: @skrah @xmnlab can you take a look? Thanks!

---

[skrah]: Yes, it does not match. This is the implementation of the shape calculation:

```
template<typename T>
static inline T pooling_output_shape(
        T inputSize, T kernelSize, T pad, T stride, T dilation, bool ceil_mode) {
    T outputSize = ((inputSize + 2 * pad - dilation * (kernelSize - 1) - 1 + (ceil_mode ? stride - 1 : 0)) / stride + 1);
    if (pad) {
        // ensure that the last pooling starts inside the image
        // needed to avoid problems in ceil mode
        if ((outputSize - 1) * stride >= inputSize + pad)
          --outputSize;
    }
    return outputSize;
}
```

Looking at it very briefly, the most likely thing is that the right floor bracket in the documentation should exclude the `+ 1`.

But I'd need to see the original algorithm to be sure.

---

[skrah]: It must be a documentation issue:  Looking at c19af59, the original code had the `+ 1` outside of `ceil()` and `floor()`.

---

[jignparm]: >It must be a documentation issue: Looking at c19af59, the original code had the + 1 outside of ceil() and floor().

Could this be a bug in the implementation (instead of documentation)? The **+1** being outside or inside the `floor()` function should still result in same value since.
```
           floor(x + 1) = floor(x) + 1
```



For example, in issue  microsoft/onnxruntime#1224 , the  tensor size, kernel etc. variables are **i=4 ,k=3 d=2, s=2, p=0**

If the **+1** is **inside** the size computed is:

```
  outsize = floor ( (i + 2p - d*(k - 1) - 1) / s  +1 )
          = floor ( (4 + 0 - 2 *(3 - 1) - 1) / 2  +1 )
          = floor ( (4        - 2 *2    - 1) / 2  +1 )
          = floor ( (                   - 1) / 2  +1 )
          = floor (                         -0.5  +1 ) 
          = floor ( 0.5 )
          = 0
```

If the **+1** is **outside** the function then:

```
  outsize = floor ( (i + 2p - d*(k - 1) - 1) / s ) + 1 
          = floor ( (4 + 0 - 2 *(3 - 1) - 1) / 2  ) +1 
          = floor ( (4        - 2 *2    - 1) / 2  ) +1 
          = floor ( (                   - 1) / 2  ) +1 
          = floor (                         -0.5  ) +1  
          = floor (  -0.5 ) +1
          = -1 + 1
          = 0
```
In either case, the size evaluates to 0.

---

[skrah]: @jignparm Thanks, I replied too hastily. :)

So, revisiting c19af59:  Integer division in `C++` truncates toward zero, which gives unexpected results if the intermediate result in `pooling_output_shape()` before the division is negative:

```
(gdb) p -1 / 2
$12 = 0
```

Contrast with Python, which does not:

```
>>> -1 // 2
-1
```

So the question is if this was deliberate or not, perhaps @f0k can say more.  Removing the doc label.

---

[jignparm]: The implementation should allow for the output size to be 0 if the kernel is larger than input.

For example, if ** i=2, p=0, d=1, s=2, k=3**, the kernel is 1 pixel larger than the input.

The formula says output size =  **(2 - 3 )/ 2 + 1** =  **-1/2 + 1**

However, in  the C++ implementation above, because of truncation (and not flooring) we get output size = 1. In Python we get 0.

If k=4, then the C++ behavior becomes correct again, and we get output size = 0. It seems like a corner case in the C++ implementation. The  floor function seems correct to use, versus rounding towards zero.

---

[f0k]: > So the question is if this was deliberate or not, perhaps @f0k can say more.

Oh, good catch @lara-hdr. I'm quite sure I was not aware of different truncation rules between Python and C++ when I wrote this, my goal was to have the implementation match the documentation, and I relied on the existing test suite to tell me whether all edge cases were handled correctly. So this is a bug in the implementation and the test suite.

Note that only C++11 and above are guaranteed to truncate to zero, but that's [what PyTorch uses](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/master/CMakeLists.txt#L15), so we can rely on that.

> The floor function seems correct to use, versus rounding towards zero.

Good, since we have to stick with integer arithmetic, we will need to check whether the size is negative before dividing by the stride and apply a correction.

Side question: What do we want to happen if the kernel is much larger than the input? Follow the equation in the documentation to give a negative output size, or give an output size of zero?

---

[skrah]: > What do we want to happen if the kernel is much larger than the input?

It should raise an error in `shape_check()`:

```
>>> m = MaxPool1d(kernel_size=2**31-1, stride=2, padding=0, dilation=2, ceil_mode=True)
>>> m(input)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/home/stefan/rel2/lib/python3.7/site-packages/torch/nn/modules/module.py", line 494, in __call__
    result = self.forward(*input, **kwargs)
  File "/home/stefan/rel2/lib/python3.7/site-packages/torch/nn/modules/pooling.py", line 75, in forward
    self.return_indices)
  File "/home/stefan/rel2/lib/python3.7/site-packages/torch/_jit_internal.py", line 129, in fn
    return if_false(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/home/stefan/rel2/lib/python3.7/site-packages/torch/nn/functional.py", line 458, in _max_pool1d
    input, kernel_size, stride, padding, dilation, ceil_mode)
RuntimeError: Given input size: (1x1x4). Calculated output size: (1x1x-2147483643). Output size is too small
```

---

[skrah]: @jignparm However, in your example with `k=4`, the shape check (which is separate code) raises:

```
>>> input = torch.randn(1,1,2)
>>> m = MaxPool1d(kernel_size=4, stride=2, padding=0, dilation=1)
>>> m(input)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/home/conda/miniconda3/lib/python3.7/site-packages/torch/nn/modules/module.py", line 493, in __call__
    result = self.forward(*input, **kwargs)
  File "/home/conda/miniconda3/lib/python3.7/site-packages/torch/nn/modules/pooling.py", line 75, in forward
    self.return_indices)
  File "/home/conda/miniconda3/lib/python3.7/site-packages/torch/_jit_internal.py", line 133, in fn
    return if_false(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/home/conda/miniconda3/lib/python3.7/site-packages/torch/nn/functional.py", line 461, in _max_pool1d
    input, kernel_size, stride, padding, dilation, ceil_mode)
RuntimeError: Given input size: (1x1x2). Calculated output size: (1x1x0). Output size is too small at /opt/conda/conda-bld/pytorch-nightly_1557205623994/work/aten/src/THNN/generic/SpatialDilatedMaxPooling.c:51
```

---

[f0k]: >> What do we want to happen if the kernel is much larger than the input?
>
> It should raise an error in shape_check():

Ok, for that it's enough if we return an output size of zero:
https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/31e1e63/aten/src/ATen/native/Pool.h#L64-L69

I'm asking because then we could just return 0 if the nominator is <= zero before dividing by the stride, instead of implementing [a properly flooring integer division](https://godbolt.org/#g:!((g:!((g:!((h:codeEditor,i:(j:1,lang:c%2B%2B,source:'%23include+%3Cstdio.h%3E%0A%23include+%3Cstdlib.h%3E%0A%23include+%3Cstring.h%3E%0A%0Aint64_t+floor_div_a(int64_t+a,+int64_t+b)%0A%7B%0A++++//+requires+stdlib+div()%0A++++ldiv_t+r+%3D+div(a,+b)%3B%0A++++if+(r.rem+!!%3D+0+%26%26+((a+%3C+0)+%5E+(b+%3C+0)))%0A++++++++r.quot--%3B%0A++++return+r.quot%3B%0A%7D%0A%0Aint64_t+floor_div_b(int64_t+a,+int64_t+b)+%7B%0A++++//+portable+implementation%0A++++int64_t+d+%3D+a+/+b%3B%0A++++int64_t+r+%3D+a+%25+b%3B%0A++++if+(r)+%7B%0A++++++++return+d+-+((a+%3C+0)+%5E+(b+%3C+0))%3B%0A++++%7D+else+%7B%0A++++++++return+d%3B%0A++++%7D%0A%7D%0A%0Aint64_t+floor_div_c(int64_t+a,+int64_t+b)+%7B%0A++++//+only+valid+for+%3E%3D+C99+/+C%2B%2B11%0A++++return+(a+%3E%3D+0)+%3F+(a+/+b)+:+-((-a)+/+b)%3B%0A%7D%0A'),l:'5',n:'0',o:'C%2B%2B+source+%231',t:'0')),k:48.35509138381202,l:'4',n:'0',o:'',s:0,t:'0'),(g:!((h:compiler,i:(compiler:g74,filters:(___0:(),b:'0',binary:'1',commentOnly:'0',demangle:'0',directives:'0',execute:'1',intel:'0',jquery:'3.2.1',length:1,libraryCode:'1',prevObject:(___0:(),length:1,prevObject:(___0:(jQuery321065988541133087141:(display:'')),length:1)),trim:'0'),lang:c%2B%2B,libs:!(),options:'-O3+-xc%2B%2B+-std%3Dc%2B%2B11',source:1),l:'5',n:'0',o:'x86-64+gcc+7.4+(Editor+%231,+Compiler+%231)+C%2B%2B',t:'0')),header:(),k:51.644908616188,l:'4',n:'0',o:'',s:0,t:'0')),l:'2',n:'0',o:'',t:'0')),version:4).

---

[f0k]: > I'm asking because then we could just return 0 if the nominator is <= zero before dividing by the stride, instead of implementing a properly flooring integer division.

Just checked what the convolution shape computation does, turns out they use a `div_rtn` function, which is the second of the implementations I linked to: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/master/aten/src/ATen/div_rtn.h
So we should just reuse it here, and extend the test suite accordingly.

---

[jignparm]: >Side question: What do we want to happen if the kernel is much larger than the input? Follow the equation in the documentation to give a negative output size, or give an output size of zero?

From the the case of K=4 that @skrah mentioned above,  it looks like the prevailing pattern is to  raise an exception. A negative value would be confusing -- so 0 or else raising exception seems most logical.,

---

[f0k]: The exception is not raised by the shape computation code, but outside, and [the exception message includes the computed shape](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/31e1e63/aten/src/ATen/native/Pool.h#L64-L69). Not sure if it would make a difference for anything else but the message. But I'd suggest to be consistent with the convolution, since it's the same problem.

title: PyTorch issue 22301
body:
## 🐛 Bug

Ambiguous torch.Tensor int vs float comparison

## To Reproduce
input: torch.Tensor([0]).int() < 0.5
output: tensor([0], dtype=torch.uint8)

## Expected behavior
Expected numpy-like behavior:
input: torch.Tensor([0]).int() < 0.5
output: tensor([1])

- PyTorch 1.0.1
- Ubuntu 18.04
comments:
[zou3519]: cc @nairbv: I think type promotion things might be able to fix this?

---

[nairbv]: Thanks @azakhtyamov for pointing this out.

Yes @zou3519 this is related to https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/9515. 

In this particular case the `<` operator casts the operand (0.5) to an int before performing the comparison. Essentially this means it's checking `< 0` instead of `< 0.5`.

The relevant code is in the generated LegacyTHFunctionsCPU.cpp.

Similarly `torch.IntTensor([2]) * 1.9` results in `tensor([2], dtype=torch.int32)` instead of `3`, because the 1.9 is cast to an int tensor `1`.

---

[OneMoreSecond]: The promotion from float to int Tensor is really confusing.

---

[nairbv]: I think we've only fixed for one comparison operator so-far so re-opening.
@ifedan

---

[ifedan]: Fixed in https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/26981

```python
import numpy as np
```

```python
b
```

title: PyTorch issue 22833
body:
## 🐛 Bug

The following script shows the issue.

## To Reproduce

```
import torch

def f(z):
    return (torch.ones(2) * z ** 2 - z).sum()

torch.set_default_tensor_type(torch.cuda.FloatTensor)
jf = torch.jit.trace(f, torch.tensor(1.))

z = torch.tensor(1., requires_grad=True)
y = jf(z)
print(torch.autograd.grad(y, (z,))[0])

z = torch.tensor(1., requires_grad=True)
y = f(z)
print(torch.autograd.grad(y, (z,))[0])
```
gives
```
tensor(6.)
tensor(2.)
```

## Expected behavior

Get the same result for both jit and non-jit version: `torch.tensor(2.)`

## Environment

 - PyTorch Version (e.g., 1.0): 1.1.0
 - OS (e.g., Linux): Linux
 - How you installed PyTorch (`conda`, `pip`, source): conda
 - Python version: 3.6
 - CUDA/cuDNN version: 10.0
 - GPU models and configuration: RTX 2070
comments:
[eellison]: @ailzhang or @wanchaol can you take a look?

---

[t-vi]: So @Chillee tracked this down and it comes from the `_grad_sum_to_size` commutation business.
TLDR: While `AutogradAdd` is never broadcasting and so the sizes of the operands will be the same, `_grad_sum_to_size does` does not in general commute with `AutogradAdd` because after the commutation the previously non-broadcasting `AutogradAdd` becomes broadcasting and the smaller argument is erroneously broadcasted and `sum_to_size`ed. I should have seen this.

title: PyTorch issue 24137
body:
## 🐛 Bug

In version 1.2.0, NCCL backend doesn't support `torch.bool` datatype. Broadcasting a tensor of this type throws error "RuntimeError: Unsupported data type for NCCL process group".

## To Reproduce

Steps to reproduce the behavior:

Create a file test.py with following contents:
```py
import torch
import argparse
from torch import distributed as dist


parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
parser.add_argument("--local_rank", type=int)

args = parser.parse_args()

torch.distributed.init_process_group("nccl")

local_rank = args.local_rank

device = torch.device(local_rank)

if local_rank == 0:
    element = False
else:
    element = True


def broadcast_scalar(scalar, src=0, device="cpu"):
    scalar_tensor = torch.tensor(scalar).to(device)
    with torch.no_grad():
        scalar_tensor = dist.broadcast(scalar_tensor, src)
    return scalar_tensor.item()


broadcast_scalar(element, src=0, device=device)
```

Run it with following command:
`python -u -m torch.distributed.launch --nproc_per_node 2 test.py`

This has been tested on 2 GPUs.

## Expected behavior

NCCL backend should support the bool datatype. 
Current workaround fix: Change the datatype to int by doing `.long()` before broadcasting.
## Environment

```
Is debug build: No
CUDA used to build PyTorch: 10.0.130

OS: Ubuntu 18.04.1 LTS
GCC version: (Ubuntu 7.4.0-1ubuntu1~18.04.1) 7.4.0
CMake version: version 3.10.2

Python version: 3.6
Is CUDA available: Yes
CUDA runtime version: 9.0.176
GPU models and configuration:
GPU 0: Quadro GP100
GPU 1: Quadro GP100

Nvidia driver version: 410.79
cuDNN version: Could not collect

Versions of relevant libraries:
[pip] numpy==1.16.3
[pip] torch==1.2.0
[pip] torchtext==0.3.1
[pip] torchvision==0.2.2
[conda] torch                     1.2.0                     <pip>
[conda] torchtext                 0.3.1                     <pip>
[conda] torchvision               0.2.2                     <pip>
```
comments:
[williamFalcon]: And you get a warning saying .uint8 isn't supported so we should switch to .bool... but then NCCL doesn't support it.

---

[soumith]: cc: @mrshenli @pietern we should fix this with upcast + transfer + downcast

---

[rohan-varma]: @soumith, I'm working on this bug. Could you explain what you mean by "upcast + transfer + downcast"? It appears that the error is coming from here: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/master/torch/lib/c10d/ProcessGroupNCCL.cpp#L45-L60, and seems to happen because `ncclDataType_t` doesn't have a bool type

---

[soumith]: @rohan-varma i mean that we should cast the buffer from bool to uint8, then all reduce, then cast it on the other side to bool again

---

[pietern]: Things to keep in mind:
* Can only use `uint8_t` with up to 255 processes in the process group. We rely on every process contributing an integer equal to 1 if the equivalent boolean entry is set. With 256 processes we would overflow an 8-bit unsigned integer and get the wrong result. The change should either 1) assert that the process group size is small enough, or implement a separate code path that uses a 16-bit unsigned integer for larger process groups.
* Semantics of the different reduction ops (each of which _could_ use SUM as the underlying reduction):
  * `ReduceOp.SUM` -- boolean OR, so the boolean output is `output != 0`
  * `ReduceOp.PRODUCT` -- boolean AND, so the boolean output is `output == pg->size`
  * `ReduceOp.MIN` -- boolean AND (see above)
  * `ReduceOp.MAX` -- boolean OR (see above)

---

[ekmb]: @rohan-varma Is it fixed?

---

[mrcslws]: I would be really happy to see this fixed. We have lots of modules that store masks in buffers. Because of this issue, these modules are forced to use `float16` or `float32` mask buffers rather than `bool` buffers.

---

[sajadn]: On pytorch 1.5.1 I still have problem with this. Is it going to be fixed?

---

[rohan-varma]: Hi @sajadn, this was landed ~1 month ago so it should be part of the next release, PT 1.7. Until then, you can try out the nightly build (see instructions at https://pytorch.org/) where this is fixed.

title: PyTorch issue 24200
body:
## 🐛 Bug

Calling `torch.tensor` on a Python `list` of `np.float32` objects leaks memory.

## To Reproduce

```
import numpy as np
import torch

def mem():
	import os, psutil
	return psutil.Process(os.getpid()).memory_info().rss // 1024

a = np.zeros(10000, dtype = np.float32) # <-- 1. dtype is important
a = list(a) # <-- 2. has to be a python list

mem_base = mem()
for _ in range(10):
	torch.tensor(a) # <-- 3. must not pass `dtype=torch.float32` here
	print(mem() - mem_base)

# output on my machine:
# 1416
# 2884
# 3940
# 4996
# 6316
# 7372
# 8428
# 9484
# 10804
# 11860
```

## Expected behavior

The number being printed (memory usage) should be more or less constant.

## Environment

 - PyTorch Version: 1.0.0, 1.1.0, 1.2.0
 - OS (e.g., Linux): Linux
 - How you installed PyTorch (`conda`, `pip`, source): pip
 - Python version: 3.6.8

## Additional context

On pytorch==0.4.1, `torch.tensor(a)` throws:
```
RuntimeError: Could not infer dtype of numpy.float32
```

Also, this might be the underlying bug in [#17499](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/17499).

### How this shows up in real code

This shows up in real code if you have a 1d array in a dataset:

```
class Dataset:
    def __init__(self, X, y):
        self.X = x; self.y = y
    
    def __getitem__(self, i):
        return self.X[i], self.y[i] # <-- self.y[i] is a np.float32
    
    def __len__(self):
        return self.X.shape[0]
```

`self.y[i]` is a `np.float32`, and in 1.2.0, a batch of these get wrapped in a `list` by `_MapDatasetFetcher`, and `default_collate` calls `torch.as_tensor` on it.

title: PyTorch issue 24504
body:
Reproduction:
```
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

inputs = torch.randn(1, 1, 5, 5)
print(F.pad(inputs, [4,0,3,0], mode='circular').shape) # (1,1,5,5) (Wrong)
print(F.pad(inputs, [1,0,0,0], mode='circular').shape) # (1,1,5,5) (Wrong)
print(F.pad(inputs, [1,1,0,0], mode='circular').shape) # (1,1,5,7) (Correct)
print(F.pad(inputs, [2,1,0,0], mode='circular').shape) # (1,1,5,8) (Correct)
```

Correct results:
```
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

inputs = torch.randn(1, 1, 5, 5)
print(F.pad(inputs, [4,0,3,0], mode='circular').shape) # (1,1,8,9)
print(F.pad(inputs, [1,0,0,0], mode='circular').shape) # (1,1,5,6)
print(F.pad(inputs, [1,1,0,0], mode='circular').shape) # (1,1,5,7)
print(F.pad(inputs, [2,1,0,0], mode='circular').shape) # (1,1,5,8)
```



cc @SsnL
comments:
[roberthoenig]: I just hit the same bug.

---

[roberthoenig]: As a workaround, you can define your own 1D circular padding like so:
```python
def padding1d_circular(input, pad):
    return torch.cat([input[:, :, -pad[0]:], input,
                      input[:, :, 0:pad[1]]], dim=2)
```

---

[pdlive215]: The implementation of circular padding is already a bit of a workaround. It's implemented in Python rather than in `torch/csrc`, and it's not defined in `nn.modules.padding`. I have submitted a PR to fix this specific issue, but I think it would make sense going forward to add a proper C++ implementation of circular padding defined in `nn.modules.padding` (and then to remove the workaround Python implementation).

---

[mruberry]: Running this on nightly I get:

```
torch.Size([1, 1, 8, 9])
torch.Size([1, 1, 5, 6])
torch.Size([1, 1, 5, 7])
torch.Size([1, 1, 5, 8])
```

I think this was fixed by https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/39273 by @cth7. 

I think, however, that the C++ implementation of circular padding still has this bug. See https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/44459.

title: PyTorch issue 24802
body:
`torch.norm` currently have the following inconsistent behavior where for matrix inputs and `ord` is a numerical value, **vector** norm is computed instead:
```
            =====  ============================  ==========================
            ord    matrix norm                   vector norm
            =====  ============================  ==========================
            None   Frobenius norm                2-norm
            'fro'  Frobenius norm                --
            'nuc'  nuclear norm                  --
            Other  as vec norm when dim is None  sum(abs(x)**ord)**(1./ord)
            =====  ============================  ==========================
```
(Even though the last row reads `when dim is None`, same behavior happens when `dim` is a 2-tuple).


However, the matrix Lpq norms are useful. And users are justified in expecting that `mat.norm(2)` computes the L2 norm (max e-value) since `mat.norm('fro')` computes the matrix Frobenius norm. If we follow the [NumPy interface](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/numpy/reference/generated/numpy.linalg.norm.html), the following behavior should be less surprising and error prune:

```

    =====  ============================  ==========================
    ord    norm for matrices             norm for vectors
    =====  ============================  ==========================
    None   Frobenius norm                2-norm
    'fro'  Frobenius norm                --
    'nuc'  nuclear norm                  --
    inf    max(sum(abs(x), axis=1))      max(abs(x))
    -inf   min(sum(abs(x), axis=1))      min(abs(x))
    0      --                            sum(x != 0)
    1      max(sum(abs(x), axis=0))      as below
    -1     min(sum(abs(x), axis=0))      as below
    2      2-norm (largest sing. value)  as below
    -2     smallest singular value       as below
    other  --                            sum(abs(x)**ord)**(1./ord)
    =====  ============================  ==========================
```

cc @ezyang @gchanan @zou3519 @SsnL @vishwakftw @jianyuh
comments:
[vadimkantorov]: Related about the spectral matrix norm: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/12760

---

[vishwakftw]: How does NumPy handle batching?

For instance, a 4 x 3 matrix can also be interpreted as 4 size-3 vectors.

---

[vishwakftw]: So, I did some trials and this is what I found:
- If inputs are 2-D and axis is not specified, then the input is considered to be a matrix
- If inputs are 2-D and an axis is specified, then the input is considered to be a batch of vectors whose corresponding norms are output
- If inputs are > 2-D, then an axis argument is mandatory when `ord` is not `None`. Furthermore, you will have to provide one axis for vector inputs, and 2 axes for matrix inputs.

---

[ezyang]: It does seem like this may merit a BC-breaking change. Some things we should check before doing so: (1) when was this consistency introduced, (2) is the inconsistent behavior tested for in our test suite, or is this actually totally untested.

This seems like a case where we can easily give a deprecation warning, since the forward compatible way of batching (specify an axis explicitly) works today,

---

[VitalyFedyunin]: We need to separate 'needs discussion' label from the triage process.

---

[ailzhang]: ~Any objections if we fix the documentation first for 1.3? Now it says we give matrix norm in some cases, which is not true.~
[Edit: ] Nvm the documentation matches our behavior which "redefines" matrices norm to be same as vec norm for non-fro and non-nuc norms. https://pytorch.org/docs/master/torch.html#torch.norm 
I'd strong prefer a BC breaking change. Assigning myself for now, will try to find some time to fix later if no one beats me to it.

---

[kurtamohler]: @ailzhang , are you still planning on doing this? If not, I would be happy to take over.

---

[ailzhang]: Hey @kurtamohler , feel free to take it over! Please let us know if you need any help! Thanks!

---

[kurtamohler]: Cool, thanks!

---

[kurtamohler]: I don't fully understand what numpy's norm is supposed to do when `ord=2` in the matrix norm case. The documentation says `2-norm (largest sing. value)`, which is unclear to me. "largest sing. value" would seem to just mean `max(x, axis=(0,1))`, but that's not the result I get:
```
>>> numpy.linalg.norm(numpy.array([[1,2,3],[4,5,6]]), ord=2, axis=(0,1))
9.50803200069572
```

---

[vishwakftw]: Largest singular value is the largest value of the diagonal elements of S, where X = USV^{T} (SVD).

---

[kurtamohler]: Got it, thanks @vishwakftw!

---

[kurtamohler]: I think I might need to introduce a new function `at::matrix_norm`. At first, my approach was just to change `at::norm` to calculate a matrix norm if `dim.size() == 2`. But the problem is that `at::frobenius_norm` calls `at::norm`, expecting it to act like a vector norm even if `dim.size() > 1`.

An alternative solution could be to add an optional bool argument to `at::norm` to allow the caller to force the vector norm to be used, no mater what `dim.size()` is, but to me it seems less confusing to just make a new function. If anyone sees an issue with that, please let me know!

---

[ailzhang]: cc: @mruberry here: do we have some preferred way to handle this change? Thanks!

---

[mruberry]: In the near-ish future we'll have torch.linalg.norm analogous to NumPy's numpy.linalg.norm (https://numpy.org/doc/1.18/reference/generated/numpy.linalg.norm.html?highlight=norm#numpy.linalg.norm) and want to deprecate this function. Once we have torch.linalg.norm like that, would you still want to add a separate function?

---

[vadimkantorov]: on side note: will all other linear algebra functions like lu, qr, eig, svd, solvers, cholesky etc be moved in linalg? (like they are in numpy / scipy)

---

[kurtamohler]: @mruberry, the separate function would only be in the C++ `at` namespace. I'm still planning on keeping everything within the Python `torch.norm` function so it has the same interface as `numpy.linalg.norm`. The decision whether or not to add an `at::matrix_norm` function should not affect the plan to move `torch.norm` to `torch.linalg.norm`.

However, I may have just thought of a simpler solution to the `at::frobenius_norm` problem. I might be able to squeeze the reduction dims into one dimension before calling `at::norm`. I'll go see if that works.

---

[mruberry]: > on side note: will all other linear algebra functions like lu, qr, eig, svd, solvers, cholesky etc be moved in linalg? (like they are in numpy / scipy)

Yes. We're going for naming consistency.

---

[vadimkantorov]: Are instance methods going to disappear as well? It's going to be a bit strange to have `tensor.norm(...)` that is synonym `torch.linalg.norm(...)`. Are fft functions are also getting moved to `fft` namespace? I have mixed feelings, PyTorch API is more concise :)

---

[mruberry]: Yes. fft functions are also being moved to the fft namespace. No, we don't plan to add a namespace to methods on torch.Tensor.

---

[kurtamohler]: I have a working implementation, but some other tests are failing. Currently working on fixing them.

---

[kurtamohler]: Most of the failures I've seen in `test_torch.py` so far are related to `torch.dist`, which calls `at::norm` underneath. Similar to the `at::frobenius_norm` issue, I had to flatten the input to `at::norm` so that the vector norm would be used, which fixed almost everything. But now I'm seeing one remaining error in `test_torch.py` because a 1-dim vector norm seems to introduce a little more precision error than a 2-dim vector norm in this case. In other words, reducing the 2 dimensions separately gives a more precise result than flattening to 1 dimension and then reducing.
```
$ python test/test_torch.py TestTorchDeviceTypeCUDA.test_triangular_solve_batched_cuda_float64
F
======================================================================
FAIL: test_triangular_solve_batched_cuda_float64 (__main__.TestTorchDeviceTypeCUDA)
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/work/kurtamohler/development/pytorch-matrix-norm/torch/testing/_internal/common_utils.py", line 769, in wrapper
    method(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/work/kurtamohler/development/pytorch-matrix-norm/torch/testing/_internal/common_device_type.py", line 241, in instantiated_test
    result = test(self, device_arg, dtype)
  File "/work/kurtamohler/development/pytorch-matrix-norm/torch/testing/_internal/common_device_type.py", line 411, in dep_fn
    return fn(slf, device, *args, **kwargs)
  File "/work/kurtamohler/development/pytorch-matrix-norm/torch/testing/_internal/common_device_type.py", line 411, in dep_fn
    return fn(slf, device, *args, **kwargs)
  File "test/test_torch.py", line 10156, in test_triangular_solve_batched
    triangular_solve_batch_helper((batchsize, 5, 5), (batchsize, 5, 10),
  File "test/test_torch.py", line 10153, in triangular_solve_batch_helper
    self.assertLessEqual(b.dist(torch.matmul(A, x_act)), 3e-12)  # Correctness check
AssertionError: tensor(5.0628e-12, device='cuda:0', dtype=torch.float64) not less than or equal to 3e-12
```

The difference is still fairly minimal, 5.0628e-12 vs 3e-12. So would it be acceptable for me to just increase the correctness threshold in this test to 6e-12, or should we re-enable a way to calculate n-dim vector norms in `at::norm`? If we want to enable n-dim vector norms, then I'll likely have to create the `at::matrix_norm` function I mentioned before.

---

[kurtamohler]: Does anyone have any thoughts on my previous comment? To me, it seems like it would be best if I create the `at::matrix_norm` function. That way, `at::norm` can retain its original behavior, we will have less precision error in `torch.dist`, and `at::norm` callers won't have the extra overhead of flattening to 1-d when they want to calculate a vector norm for an n-d tensor.

---

[vadimkantorov]: > Yes. fft functions are also being moved to the fft namespace. No, we don't plan to add a namespace to methods on torch.Tensor.

One other thing to note is that splitting functions in namespaces may create discrepancy between Python functions and TorchCpp functions (which is not end of the world, so maybe not a big problem)

---

[ezyang]: @kurtamohler I'm not sure I have a good answer for you. Precision in reductions has always been a sensitive matter; for example, look at https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/39516 where we added a tree based sum for floats to reduce numerical stability. The more numerically stable the reduction is, the better.

That being said, it seems like the old "two reductions" strategy seems like it only accidentally is more precise, e.g., you could come up with dimension sizes where it is no benefit. And one could maybe apply the same technique as #39516 to get back numerical stability. The counterpoint, however, is that maybe users EXPECT a 2-dim vector norm to have numerics similar to what would happen if you did one reduction first, then another reduction.

cc @peterbell10 if you have any comments

---

[peterbell10]: I have checked and reduced dimensions can already be coalesced by `TensorIterator`. So, e.g. a `dim=None` reduction over a contiguous tensor would do a single large reduction anyway and thus be less accurate than successive reductions over each dim. So, from that perspective I think this is not an issue.

I would add that `flatten`ing could add a fair bit of overhead. Having a C++ function which always does vector norm would probably be better from that point of view.

---

[kurtamohler]: I've created the `norm_matrix` function, but right now it's callable from Python with `torch.norm_matrix`. I'd like to disable that somehow, and only have it callable from `torch._VF`. I added the function schema in `native_functions.yaml`, is there a simple way for me to disable it from generating `torch.norm_matrix`? Or do I need to remove it from `native_functions.yaml` and then manually make it available in `torch._VF`?

---

[ailzhang]: @kurtamohler I think https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/master/tools/autograd/gen_python_functions.py#L53 might be helpful to stop exposing it in python

---

[kurtamohler]: > @kurtamohler I think https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/master/tools/autograd/gen_python_functions.py#L53 might be helpful to stop exposing it in python

That does remove `torch.norm_matrix`, but it also removes `torch._VF.norm_matrix`. But maybe I can then add it manually to `tools/autograd/templates/python_variable_methods.cpp`. Or maybe it would be better to modify codegen so that `norm_matrix` gets added to `torch/csrc/autograd/generated/python_variable_methods.cpp` but not `torch/csrc/autograd/generated/python_torch_functions.cpp`. I could make a new list called something like `SKIP_TORCH_BINDINGS`, which would be similar to `SKIP_PYTHON_BINDINGS`.

Although I'm wondering, if I keep `torch._VF.norm_matrix`, does that mean that `norm_matrix` will be callable from a Tensor, like `torch.rand(2,2).norm_matrix()`? Because I don't want that.

---

[kurtamohler]: Ok I did some experimenting. Removing `norm_matrix` from `python_torch_methods.cpp` while keeping it in `python_variable_methods.cpp` ends up removing `torch.norm_matrix` like I wanted, but it also removes `torch._VF.norm_matrix` and it's still callable from a Tensor object, like `torch.rand(2,2).norm_matrix()`. So it seems like I will need to remove `norm_matrix` completely from autogen, but I'll need to somehow make it available to be called from `torch.functional.norm`. Although, if accomplishing that is going to be a problem, we could consider just letting `norm_matrix` be callable from Python, just like `frobenius_norm` and `nuclear_norm` are.

---

[ezyang]: Can't you just put a leading underscore on the name?

---

[kurtamohler]: The above commit accomplishes the main goals of this issue, adding matrix norm, and making `torch.norm`'s interface consistent with numpy. I've added tests to cover the changed behavior.

There are a few things I still have to do: create backward formula for matrix norm, update jit for `torch.norm`, and fix any call sites of `torch.norm` that might have broken

---

[kurtamohler]: @nikitaved suggested that I try using an iterative approximation of the largest and smallest eigenvalues when `matrix_norm` is called with `p = +/-2`, rather than my current implementation which unnecessarily calculates every eigenvalue with SVD. With Nik's help, and after learning a bit more linear algebra, I wrote a proof-of-concept script to calculate these approximations for a range of matrix sizes and measure the average relative error of the approximation with respect to `torch.svd()`'s output. The script also performs the same experiment with `numpy.linalg.norm`, also calculating relative errors with respect to `torch.svd()`. Here's the script: https://github.com/kurtamohler/pytorch-perf-test-scripts/blob/master/matrix-norm/approx_matrix_2_norm.py

Here are my error measurements:

First of all, `numpy.linalg.norm` is more accurate than both power iterations I tried. I also compared `numpy.linalg.norm` to `numpy.linalg.svd`, and they matched exactly for all input sizes. That led me to suspect that `numpy.linalg.norm` just calls `numpy.linalg.svd`, and indeed it does: https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/19f91f3935069229bbc23bbda4294c36957901a5/numpy/linalg/linalg.py#L2355

Looking at the power iteration's accuracy, it doesn't look especially great. Typically it's off by 1-2% with 20 iterations. One important outlier, though, is that when the matrix is square, the smallest eigenvalue approximation is considerably inaccurate, with a relative error of about 8 +/- 230% at 20 iterations. That's pretty terrible. Perhaps there's a way to fix that special case.

I did try simply running the approximation for more iterations and got much better accuracy. At 1000 iterations, almost all the errors effectively disappear, aside from the case of the smallest eigenvalue of a square matrix.

It's worth noting that my power iteration algorithm could be improved with an adaptive number of iterations. The amount of change per iteration could be tracked, and once it's less than some small threshold, the iteration could stop.

The whole point of doing the power iteration is to gain performance over SVD. But without actually implementing the power iteration in C++, I can't really measure the performance. I don't know, for instance, if 1000 power iterations will take longer than SVD, because at the moment, I don't know how SVD is implemented (I'll skim over the code to see if I can get an understanding of its perf characteristics).

In any case, I think I should prioritize finishing up the functionality changes to `torch.norm`. Performance improvements could be addressed with a second PR.

[nikitaved]: @kurtamohler , could you please also compare against the power method implemented for the Spectral Normalization? In the paper they operate on the right and the left sides of a rectangular matrix, without "squaring" it by multiplying with A or A^T. So, I would speculate that the power method on a matrix after the "squaring" process should be faster (it really depends more on the gap between the largest and the second largest singular value).

---

[kurtamohler]: @nikitaved , unfortunately, it seems like the `torch.nn.utils.spectral_norm` method actually calculates `A / largest_eigenvalue(A)`. Maybe it uses a helper function exposed to `at::` that will just calculate the eigenvalue, but it doesn't seem to be exposed to Python, as far as I can tell. So I had to calculate `A / spectral_norm(A)` to get the eigenvalue back out. This will introduce some amount of error.

```
def approx_spectral_largest(a, iters: int, method: str):
    class MyModule(torch.nn.Module):
        def __init__(self, weight):
            super(MyModule, self).__init__()
            self.weight = torch.nn.Parameter(weight)
    
        def forward(self):
            return self.weight
    
    b = torch.nn.utils.spectral_norm(
        MyModule(a),
        n_power_iterations=iters)()
    
    if method == 'median':
        return (a / b).median()
    elif method == 'mean':
        return (a / b).mean()
    else:
        raise RuntimeError('method "%s" not available' % method)
```

It seemed like `(a / b).median()` would give a more stable result than mean, but I chose to just see what happens with both. Unfortunately, I don't think `spectral_norm` offers any simple way of generating the smallest eigenvalue. I could use the same trick I did before--run it on `a.inverse()`, and then invert the result--but as far as I understand, one of the reasons for your suggestion to use `spectral_norm` is that we'd like to avoid using `torch.inverse()` because we want to find something more stable.

Anyway, I updated my script to compare the largest eigenvalue given by `torch.svd()` to approximation calculated with `spectral_norm`. Here's what I get:

This `spectral_norm` method seems to have pretty similar accuracy to the power iteration that I implemented. However, as I mentioned, since I have to calculate the eigenvalue from `A / spectral_norm(A)`, I'm introducing extra error than might be possible if I could access `spectral_norm`'s internal approximation of the eigenvalue. Let me look and see if that would be simple to do.

[nikitaved]: @kurtamohler, the power method from Spectral Normalization does the following:
```
for k=1, 2,...
v_{k+1} = A u_k / ||A u_k||_2
u_{k+1} = A^T v_k / ||A^T v_k||_2
return ||A||_2 approximated by v_k^T A u_k
```
So you can use your code to test the stability.
It also makes sense to actually try matrices with different spectra.

Maybe, it is easier for now just to implement these things with SVD?

P.S. inverse might not work because the matrix might have very poor condition number. It makes sense to stick to SVD for the smallest value imo...

---

[kurtamohler]: @nikitaved , how does spectral normalization handle non-square matrices though? If `u_1` is size(n) and A is size(m, n), then the first `A u_k` multiply will result in `u_2` size(m). After that, `u_3` couldn't be calculated.

So I assume you'd have to obtain a square matrix from A somehow. One method would be `A_gram = A^T A`. But `A_gram == A_gram^T`, so if we input `A_gram` into the algorithm you posted, `u_k` and `v_k` would eventually converge to the same vector.

---

[nikitaved]: See that the two sequences operate over A and A^T. They are trying to compute the leading right and left singular vectors.

---

[kurtamohler]: I still don't understand how this can be done for a non-square matrix. `u_{k+1} = A u_k / ||A u_k||_2` can only be calculated for one iteration because after that, `u_k.size() == A.size()[0]`, and you can't right-multiply an m-by-n matrix with an m-element vector if `m != n`.

---

[vishwakftw]: The procedure is: `v_{k + 1} = A_{gram} v_{k} / ||A_{gram} v_{k}||_{2}` and `u_{k+1} = A A^{T} u_{k} / ||A A^{T} u_{k}||_{2}`. Note that `A_{gram} != A A^{T}`.

This is true because: `A A^{T} = U \Sigma^{2} U^{T}` and `A_gram = A^{T} A = V \Sigma^{2} V^{T}`, where `A = U\Sigma V^{T}`

---

[nikitaved]: @kurtamohler , you are right! I have noticed that I made a mistake, confused `v_k+1` and `u_k+1`. I updated the message.

---

[nikitaved]: @vishwakftw , this is the method that @kurtamohler has already implemented. 

The spectral normalization method for GANs does not compute "squaring". See https://arxiv.org/abs/1802.05957

---

[kurtamohler]: @nikitaved , ah I see, `u_k` and `v_k` are just swapped every iteration. I've implemented it like this:

```
def approx_spectral_largest_custom(a, iters: int):
    a_t = a.transpose(-1, -2) 
    u = torch.rand(a.size()[-1])
    u /= u.norm()
    v = torch.rand(a_t.size()[-1])
    v /= v.norm()
    
    for _ in range(iters):
        v_new = a.matmul(u)
        v_new /= v_new.norm()
        u_new = a_t.matmul(v)
        u_new /= u_new.norm()
        v = v_new
        u = u_new

    return v.matmul(a).matmul(u).abs()
```

But that seems to give worse results than any of the other methods I've tried so far. Even with 100 iterations, the error can get up to around 10% +/-20%.

[nikitaved]: Cool, it exactly in accordance with my claim, as "squaring" squares singular values, hence has a potential for better convergence (squaring does happen each iteration).

---

[vadimkantorov]: I propose to migrate "spectral normalization" method into torch matrix_norm and have `algorithm` argument to specify if fast and approximate solution is expected. Then spectral normalization can use torch.matrix_norm under the hood

---

[nikitaved]: Makes sense, but I think it is better to leave spectral normalization as is. It avoids the matrix matrix multiply at the cost of slower convergence, but it is faster and cheaper the way it is..

---

[vadimkantorov]: This behavior can also be desirable for similar scenarios. Or do you mean that if it's factored out of spectral normalization, it would have to do those matrix multiplies?

---

[nikitaved]: Yep, you kind of expect norm to give high quality results I guess. And for GANs it is speed first then the quality of norm I presume, but I am not an expert with GANs. Actually, it makes sense to contact the authors and ask whether they tried better approximations.

I will probably run some experiments to see the effect of matrix multiply versus without it. In theory one should be about 2x faster than the other.

---

[vadimkantorov]: Fast inaccurate estimate may still be useful in other contexts too. But yes, it maybe requires some explicit choice of algorithm or some flag to enable it...

title: PyTorch issue 24807
body:
## 🐛 Bug

Pylint returns the error `torch.tensor is not callable`

## To Reproduce

Steps to reproduce the behavior:

```python
import torch

if __name__ == "__main__":
    t = torch.tensor([1, 2, 3], dtype=torch.float64)
```

```
>>> pipenv run pylint test.py
test.py:4:8: E1102: torch.tensor is not callable (not-callable)
```

## Expected behavior

This shall not raise pylint error

## Environment

```
PyTorch version: 1.2.0
Is debug build: No
CUDA used to build PyTorch: 10.0.130

OS: Ubuntu 18.04.2 LTS
GCC version: (Ubuntu 7.4.0-1ubuntu1~18.04.1) 7.4.0
CMake version: version 3.10.2

Python version: 3.7
Is CUDA available: Yes
CUDA runtime version: 10.0.130
GPU models and configuration:
GPU 0: GeForce RTX 2080 Ti
GPU 1: GeForce RTX 2080 Ti

Nvidia driver version: 418.67
cuDNN version: /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libcudnn.so.7.6.1

Versions of relevant libraries:
[pip3] numpy==1.17.0
[pip3] torch==1.2.0
[conda] Could not collect

Pytorch installed in Pipenv Environment
pylint==2.3.1
```

## Additional context

None

title: PyTorch issue 25010
body:
## 🐛 Bug
There seems to be an issue with CPU utilization when using a DataLoader with pin_memory=True and num_workers > 0. 

I revisited some old code that had `pin_memory=True` and two workers that weren't doing all that much. It pinned all of my CPU cores at or near 100%, with 40-50% of the usage in the kernel.  I've had performance issues with pinned memory in the past, but never to this level. Indeed, I cannot reproduce the issue going back to some older environments with 0.4 or 1.0.1. But on 3 machines w/ PyTorch 1.1 and 1.2 I can easily reproduce by running the MNIST main.py from the current examples master.

If I set either num_workers=0 or pin_memory=False the issue goes away and I'm left with the expected one core and change of usage for MNIST (or two cores and change for my original case). 

## To Reproduce

1. Run `main.py` from the MNIST example on master pytorch/examples repository.

## Expected behavior

For MNIST, with 1 worker I expect roughly 1 core's worth of CPU utilization plus some overhead. It consumes 50-100% of all cores on systems with 8-14 physical (16-28 logical) cores. A large % of the CPU usage is in the kernel, appears to be spinning/yielding, possibly due to contention. 

## Environment

I've reproduced on 3 machines.

 - PyTorch Version (e.g., 1.0): 1.1 and 1.2 (no issue on an older 1.0.1 and 0.4.1 environment on one of the machines)
 - OS (e.g., Linux): two Ubuntu 18.04 machines, one Ubuntu 16.04 machine
 - How you installed PyTorch (`conda`, `pip`, source): All conda installs
 - Python version: 3.7.3 and 3.6.8
 - CUDA/cuDNN version: Cuda 10 and CUDNN 7.5.1 and 7.6.2
 - GPU models and configuration: dual Titan RTX one one machine, dual Titan Xp and 1080Ti on other two
 - Any other relevant information:

## Additional context
I've seen at least one very likely, and other potential threads in discussion forum that are likely this issue. 

This one has an htop screenshot from me, and another user:
https://discuss.pytorch.org/t/cpu-usage-extremely-high/52172/5



cc @SsnL

[ssnl]: I propose to `torch.set_num_threads(1)` in memory pinning thread, just like what we do for the workers.

---

[rwightman]: @ssnl The workers are separate processes, pin_memory a thread. Doesn't set_num_threads impact the whole process?  To not impact the parallalization threads of the rest of the process you'd have to restrict the pin memory loop to non parallalized ops no?

---

[ssnl]: The setting is thread local.

---

[rwightman]: Cool, I tried the change locally on two envs, setting the num_threads at the beginning of _pin_memory_loop. It fixed the massive CPU use in both cases. I haven't tried it with any more meaningful workloads besides the MNIST demo yet.

---

[colesbury]: @SsnL so is this because the pinned memory copy is parallelized in 1.2 (and wasn't prior?)

---

[ssnl]: @colesbury I think this is because of the `copy_` indeed. But I am not sure if it was not parallelized before 1.2.

---

[rwightman]: @colesbury @SsnL I flipped through my environments and ran the MNIST example in each. I repro'd in 1.1 and 1.2, could not repro in 1.0.x or 0.4 as mentioned.

title: PyTorch issue 26038
body:
The code snippet works fine in 1.1 but causes an error in 1.2:
> p = F.softmax(x, dim=1)
> m = y != self.ignore_index
> t = F.one_hot((y * m.byte()).long(), num_classes=self.num_classes).byte().permute(0,3,1,2)
> i = (p * (t * m.unsqueeze(1).byte()).float()).sum((0,2,3))
> u = ((p + t.float()) * m.unsqueeze(1).float()).sum((0,2,3)) - i
> v = u.nonzero()
> return -((i[v] / u[v]).mean()).log()

I get the error message below:

> RuntimeError: range.second - range.first == t.size() INTERNAL ASSERT FAILED at /pytorch/torch/csrc/autograd/generated/Functions.cpp:55, please report a bug to PyTorch. inconsistent range for TensorList output

I had to remove the usage of nonzero() like below to make the code work:
> p = F.softmax(x, dim=1)
> m = y != self.ignore_index
> t = F.one_hot((y * m.byte()).long(), num_classes=self.num_classes).byte().permute(0,3,1,2)
> i = (p * (t * m.unsqueeze(1).byte()).float()).sum((0,2,3))
> u = ((p + t.float()) * m.unsqueeze(1).float()).sum((0,2,3)) - i
> return -((i / u).mean()).log() 

I have two questions:
(1) Why does the runtime error happen in 1.2 but not in 1.1?
(2) By not using nonzero to prevent division by zero I have numeric instability in theory. However, in really the number that comes out from a softmax operation should not reach zero because the input can't really reach negative infinity right?

cc @ezyang @gchanan @zou3519 @SsnL @albanD
comments:
[fmassa]: @bwang-delft can you write a self-contained example (with dummy input tensors) that reproduce the behavior?
Without it it is very hard to trace back where the problem comes from.

---

[bwang-delft]: Below is a self-contained example:

    
    import torch
    import torch.nn as nn
    import torch.nn.functional as F

    def loss_func(p, y, ignore_index=255, num_classes=19):
         p = F.softmax(x, dim=1)
         m = y != ignore_index
         t = F.one_hot((y * m.byte()).long(), num_classes=num_classes).byte().permute(0,3,1,2)
         i = (p * (t * m.unsqueeze(1).byte()).float()).sum((0,2,3))
         u = ((p + t.float()) * m.unsqueeze(1).float()).sum((0,2,3)) - i
         v = u.nonzero()
         return -((i[v] / u[v]).mean()).log()

    x = torch.rand(1,19,1024,2048, requires_grad=True).to('cuda').float()
    y = torch.randint(0, 18, (1,1024,2048)).to('cuda').byte()
    y[0][0][0] = 255

    loss = loss_func(x, y)
    loss.backward()

---

[VitalyFedyunin]: Between 1.1.0 and master something was changed in `nonzero` and it now requires grad (it should not). It need to be disabled.

title: PyTorch issue 26302
body:
## 🐛 Bug
Backward passes generate nonzero gradients for the embedding vector at the `padding_idx` index. Only occurs on the GPU, and didn't occur as of version 1.0.1.post2. 

## To Reproduce

```
import torch
dev = torch.device('cuda')
torch.manual_seed(0)

random_ix = torch.randint(high=10, size=(256, 3, 7))
embedding_layer = torch.nn.Embedding(10, 5, padding_idx=0)

embedding_layer.to(dev)
random_ix = random_ix.to(dev)

embeds = embedding_layer(random_ix)
merged = torch.sum(embeds, dim=2)
summed = merged.sum()
summed.backward()

print(embedding_layer.weight.grad[0])
```
which outputs: `tensor([505., 505., 505., 505., 505.], device='cuda:0')`

## Expected behavior
`tensor([0., 0., 0., 0., 0.], device='cuda:0')`

Running on the CPU provides the expected behavior:

```
torch.manual_seed(0)

random_ix = torch.randint(high=10, size=(256, 3, 7))
embedding_layer = torch.nn.Embedding(10, 5, padding_idx=0)

embeds = embedding_layer(random_ix)
merged = torch.sum(embeds, dim=2)
summed = merged.sum()
summed.backward()

print(embedding_layer.weight.grad[0])
```

## Environment

 - PyTorch Version: 1.2
 - OS: Ubuntu 18.04.2 LTS
 - How you installed PyTorch: pipenv
 - Python version: 3.6.7
 - CUDA/cuDNN version: 10.0
 - GPU models and configuration: GeForce RTX 2080 Ti


cc @ezyang @gchanan @zou3519 @SsnL @albanD @gqchen

title: PyTorch issue 26304
body:
Other files may also be susceptible, but the issue reproduces 100% on `aten/src/ATen/core/dispatch/DispatchTable.h`

Steps to reproduce:
1. Build PyTorch `python setup.py develop`
2. Edit the "Didn't find kernel to dispatch to" error message to insert some string. Example diff:

```
diff --git a/aten/src/ATen/core/dispatch/DispatchTable.h b/aten/src/ATen/core/dispatch/DispatchTable.h
index 7b4af315f1..3a5879cbda 100644
--- a/aten/src/ATen/core/dispatch/DispatchTable.h
+++ b/aten/src/ATen/core/dispatch/DispatchTable.h
@@ -263,7 +263,7 @@ private:
       }
 
       const std::string dispatch_key_str = dispatch_key.has_value() ? toString(*dispatch_key) : "None";
-      TORCH_CHECK(false, "Didn't find kernel to dispatch to for operator '", operator_name_,
+      TORCH_CHECK(false, "ARGLEBARGLEFARR Didn't find kernel to dispatch to for operator '", operator_name_,
                "'. Tried to look up kernel for dispatch key '", dispatch_key_str,
                "'. Registered dispatch keys are: ", listAllDispatchKeys());
   }
```

3. Rebuild PyTorch `python setup.py develop`
4. Run `python -c "import torch; torch.cat([], dim=1)`

Expected result: you see ARGLEBARGLEFARR
Actual result: you don't see it

I did an experiment where I did one of these botched recompiles, and then compared the set of files which had ARGLEBARGLEFARR with the set of files that had the normal error message. What I found is that CUDA generated files had not been recompiled, but CPU (and final library) all had the string. The fact that the CUDA files are not recompiling seems problematic; however, I don't understand how this could have caused the problem, as the test I made above is CPU (perhaps some sort of weak symbol situation?)

cc @ezyang @gchanan @zou3519 @smessmer
comments:

The build.ninja entries for `.cpp` sources properly point to dependency files, e.g.:


And `CUSTOM_COMMAND` is defined in `rules.ninja` as might be expected:
```

  command = $COMMAND                                                                                                                                                                                               
  description = $DESC
```

Note: something actually generates a dependency file (in this example `torch_cuda_generated_IndexUtils.cu.o.depend`) but:
* It's not in the Makefile format which ninja could understand. Instead it's just a CMake file that propagates a single variable - `CUDA_NVCC_DEPEND` - with a list of all the header files that are dependencies)
* The `.cu.o` target depends on the dependency file, rather than its contents because the `.cu.o.depend` file is declared as one of the dependencies, instead of being pointed to by the `depfile` variable (see https://ninja-build.org/manual.html#_depfile). So editing the dependency file itself would trigger a rebuild, but editing one of the dependencies does not.

TODO:

- verify that ninja dependency system is in fact used (and is not circumvented by some other dependency system)
- find out what generates the `.cu.o.depend` and `.cu.o.RelWithDebInfo.cmake` files

However, the more I look into this, the more I feel that this is not the right thing to do.

I now suspect that the patch at the bottom of `tools/setup_helpers/cmake.py` should not be there:

```
        # in cmake, .cu compilation involves generating certain intermediates    
        # such as .cu.o and .cu.depend, and these intermediates finally get compiled
        # into the final .so.                                                    
        # Ninja updates build.ninja's timestamp after all dependent files have been built,
        # and re-kicks cmake on incremental builds if any of the dependent files 
        # have a timestamp newer than build.ninja's timestamp.                   
        # There is a cmake bug with the Ninja backend, where the .cu.depend files
        # are still compiling by the time the build.ninja timestamp is updated,  
        # so the .cu.depend file's newer timestamp is screwing with ninja's incremental
        # build detector.                                                        
        # This line works around that bug by manually updating the build.ninja timestamp
        # after the entire build is finished.                                    
        ninja_build_file = os.path.join(self.build_dir, 'build.ninja')           
        if os.path.exists(ninja_build_file):                                     
            os.utime(ninja_build_file, None)
```

When I remove that snipped, there is a lot of unnecessary rebuilding (~2000 files) on the second run of `setup.py develop` but not on subsequent runs. This happens because the list of include dirs in the variable `CUDA_NVCC_INCLUDE_DIRS` changes between the first and second run, which causes a lot of build files to be regenerated, which in turn are dependencies of many object files. I'm hoping that once I fix the change of that `CUDA_NVCC_INCLUDE_DIRS` variable between the first and second build, there will be no unnecessary rebuilds.

Although I still have to verify that there are no other triggers for the unnecessary rebuilds.

title: PyTorch issue 26333
body:
Currently if the the argument of `__rmatmul__` is not a Tensor, PyTorch throws `*** TypeError: matmul(): argument 'other' (position 1) must be Tensor, not Vec`

Python data model asks that it return `NotImplemented` in such case. This allows interpreter to dispatch op to the proper implementation.
(ie see https://docs.python.org/3/library/numbers.html#implementing-the-arithmetic-operations)

Current behavior makes it hard to implement custom types. IE
```
class FactoredMatrix:
  def __init__(self, mat):
    self.mat = mat
  def __matmul__(self, other):
    return 0
  def __rmatmul__(self, other):
    return 1
x = torch.ones((2,2))
print(FactoredMatrix(x) @ x)  # works
print(x @ FactoredMatrix(x))  # fails
```


cc @ezyang @gchanan @zou3519
comments:
[gchanan]: I don't think this can be considered complete, as noted in https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/26507.

Namely:
1) That PR doesn't cover operators that are not explicitly bound
2) It doesn't seem like we should be returning NotImplemented for in-place ops.

---

[peterbell10]: > 1. That PR doesn't cover operators that are not explicitly bound

I'd missed those. I can see that and, or, xor, lshift and rshift are in `native_functions.yaml`; are there any others hidden elsewhere, e.g. I can't see `__eq__` anywhere. @gchanan could you point me towards the code generator for `native_functions.yaml`?

> 2. It doesn't seem like we should be returning NotImplemented for in-place ops.

Can you expand on this? The [python docs] state:
> `NotImplemented`
>     Special value which should be returned by the binary special methods (e.g. `__eq__()`, `__lt__()`, `__add__()`, `__rsub__()`, etc.) to indicate that the operation is not implemented with respect to the other type; **may be returned by the in-place binary special methods (e.g. `__imul__()`, `__iand__()`, etc.)** for the same purpose.


[python docs]: https://docs.python.org/3.7/library/constants.html#NotImplemented

---

[gchanan]: > Can you expand on this? The python docs state:

Ah, good looking out.  I guess I wasn't sure how another type would understand how to do in-place ops on the type, but that isn't really true.

---

[gchanan]: > could you point me towards the code generator for native_functions.yaml?

I think the relevant code is here:
https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/master/tools/autograd/gen_python_functions.py

Essentially you want to look at the generated version of python_variable_methods.
