title: PyTorch issue 54846
body:
## 🐛 Bug
This code:
```
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

input_tensor = torch.ones((1, 32, 128, 128), dtype=torch.float)
cs = nn.ChannelShuffle(groups=2)
cs(input_tensor)
```

produces irrelevant warning:

> /home/user/PyTorch/dist-packages/release/torch/nn/modules/channelshuffle.py:46: UserWarning: Named tensors and all their associated APIs are an experimental feature and subject to change. Please do not use them for anything important until they are released as stable. (Triggered internally at  ../c10/core/TensorImpl.h:973.)
>   return F.channel_shuffle(input, self.groups)

as no named tensors are used.

## Expected behavior

No warnings

## Environment

 - PyTorch Version (e.g., 1.0): current master
 - OS (e.g., Linux): Linux
 - How you installed PyTorch (`conda`, `pip`, source): source

cc @ezyang @gchanan @zou3519 @bdhirsh @jbschlosser @anjali411 @albanD @mruberry
comments: [jbschlosser]: Hey @h6197627, thanks for reporting this! `nn.ChannelShuffle` uses named tensor logic internally, resulting in the warning: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/7d56de18345bfaedf31cbb400389cbd6111b216d/aten/src/ATen/native/ChanelShuffle.cpp#L55 I agree this warning is confusing and will look into options for a fix. --- [zou3519]: Fix should be just to replace ``` return namedinference::propagate_names_if_nonempty( output_tensor, self.names()); ``` with ``` return namedinference::propagate_names_if_nonempty( output_tensor, self.has_names() ? self.names() : {}); ``` --- [h6197627]: @zou3519, changing to `self.has_names() ? self.names() : {}` results into build error: > ../aten/src/ATen/native/ChanelShuffle.cpp:57:41: error: expected primary-expression before ‘{’ token but changing to: `self.has_names() ? self.names() : std::vector()` works. If it is ok I can do PR with fix --- [ezyang]: don't do a std::vector; ArrayRef is good enough --- [BramVanroy]: I encounter this warning as well on `torch==1.9.0` (Windows, Python 3.8, cpu) when using maxpool1d. Specifically, [this line](https://github.com/stanfordnlp/stanza/blob/166e80e00254b1bf97961dc3462d043e0a0590c3/stanza/models/classifiers/cnn_classifier.py#L364): ```python pool_outs = [F.max_pool1d(out, out.shape[2]).squeeze(2) for out in conv_outs] ``` triggers this warning: > site-packages\torch\nn\functional.py:652: UserWarning: Named tensors and all their associated APIs are an experimental feature and subject to change. Please do not use them for anything important until they are released as stable. (Triggered internally at ..\c10/core/TensorImpl.h:1156.) > return torch.max_pool1d(input, kernel_size, stride, padding, dilation, ceil_mode) --- [glenn-jocher]: This warning appears in YOLOv5 now with torch 1.9.0, caused by `torch.max_pool2d()`. If this warning serves no purpose then **I think it should be removed** to not worry users unnecessarily. To reproduce run Setup and Inference cells in YOLOv5 notebook: https://colab.research.google.com/github/ultralytics/yolov5/blob/master/tutorial.ipynb ``` /usr/local/lib/python3.7/dist-packages/torch/nn/functional.py:718: UserWarning: Named tensors and all their associated APIs are an experimental feature and subject to change. Please do not use them for anything important until they are released as stable. (Triggered internally at /pytorch/c10/core/TensorImpl.h:1156.) return torch.max_pool2d(input, kernel_size, stride, padding, dilation, ceil_mode) ``` --- [zou3519]: We've fixed the problems with max_pool{1, 2, 3}d on master with https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/60059. The fix will be in our next release. --- [glenn-jocher]: @zou3519 great, thank you!

```
a = torch.randn((1,))

```
In [3]: a = torch.full((1,), 0.5, device="cuda")
   ...: 
   ...: b = torch.ones((3, 4), device="cuda")
   ...: c = torch.ones((4,), device="cuda")

[ezyang]: Consider porting this to structured to fix the problem! https://github.com/pytorch/rfcs/blob/rfc-0005/RFC-0005-structured-kernel-definitions.md

[kshitij12345]: Following patch fixes the inplace behavior (verified locally),
```patch
--- a/aten/src/ATen/native/Blas.cpp
+++ b/aten/src/ATen/native/Blas.cpp
@@ -89,6 +89,12 @@ Tensor addmv(const Tensor &self, const Tensor &mat, const Tensor &vec, const Sca
 }
 
 Tensor &addmv_(Tensor &self, const Tensor &mat, const Tensor &vec, const Scalar& beta, const Scalar& alpha) {
+  TORCH_CHECK(
+      self.sizes() == IntArrayRef{mat.size(0)},
+      "output with shape ",
+      self.sizes(),
+      " doesn't match the broadcast shape ",
+      IntArrayRef{mat.size(0)})
   return native::addmv_out(self, mat, vec, beta, alpha, self);
 }
 
```

Is it okay if porting to structured is done independently?

[ezyang]: @ngimel has a port in progress at https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/55746 but I'm not opposed to landing just the extra torch check for now.

title: PyTorch issue 55768
body:
## 🐛 Bug

Calling `torch.manual_seed(...)` (or `torch.cuda.manual_seed_all(...)`) leaks memory, quite a bit in fact. I am resetting the seed in my data loader for each sample and over the course of a few hours there are tens of gigabytes that are being leaked.

## To Reproduce

```
for i in range(1000000000): torch.manual_seed(i)
```

And watch the memory consumption of the process slowly creep up.

## Expected behavior

The memory consumption to remain constant, as is the case with `for i in range(1000000000): random.seed(i)`.

## Environment

```
PyTorch version: 1.8.1+cu102
Is debug build: False
CUDA used to build PyTorch: 10.2
ROCM used to build PyTorch: N/A

OS: Ubuntu 20.04.2 LTS (x86_64)
GCC version: (Ubuntu 9.3.0-17ubuntu1~20.04) 9.3.0
Clang version: 10.0.0-4ubuntu1 
CMake version: version 3.19.6

Python version: 3.8 (64-bit runtime)
Is CUDA available: True
CUDA runtime version: 10.1.243
GPU models and configuration: GPU 0: GeForce GTX 1650
Nvidia driver version: 460.39
cuDNN version: Could not collect
HIP runtime version: N/A
MIOpen runtime version: N/A
```


cc @ezyang @gchanan @zou3519 @bdhirsh @jbschlosser @anjali411 @pbelevich
comments:
[ngimel]: High pri to investigate leak.

---

[eqy]: FWIW I think this is not just a bug with `manual_seed`, but also in other generator functions. On upstream I see increasing memory usage with `torch.seed()` as well.

---

[kshitij12345]: Turns out that the problem is with the lazy initialization of cuda.

https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/389380ffccd6afc428189af3869272c15530c933/torch/cuda/__init__.py#L114-L123

If cuda is not initialized, any calls wrapped in `_lazy_call` get appended to `_queued_calls` (in this case, all calls to `manual_seed` were queued).  Memory doesn't grow if we call `torch.cuda.init()` before the loop.

Also many generator related functions besides `manual_seed` as noted by @eqy also call `_lazy_call`.
Ref : https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/master/torch/cuda/random.py

To this end, I think we can choose to fix this in 1 of the 3 ways.
1. Once a threshold for queued calls is reached, just `init` the CUDA. (this maybe bad because maybe they never intended to use cuda)
2. Once a threshold for queued calls is reached, warn the user that this is queuing is happening and they should init cuda (maybe?)
3. Once a threshold for queued calls is reached, raise an error with msg. (raising an error for implementation detail doesn't seem like a good option)

I think second is ok. Because, they maybe unknowingly queuing the calls on CUDA by using `manual_seed` or other similar function, so getting a warning makes sense.

-------------------
(Also we should empty _queued_calls once cuda is initialized)
https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/389380ffccd6afc428189af3869272c15530c933/torch/cuda/__init__.py#L176-L187

---

[sniklaus]: Thanks for looking into this!

But why does `torch.manual_seed()` have anything to do with CUDA? For `torch.cuda.manual_seed()` sure, but not the former.

None of the proposed solutions seem good to me. Consider calling `torch.manual_seed()` repeatedly in the data loader, 1) wouldn't work due to requiring CUDA, 2) wouldn't work since it doesn't fix the issue, and 3) just makes the data loader fail.

---

[kshitij12345]: Agree that none of the proposed solution actually fix the problem. But updating `manual_seed` to not update CUDA's seed would be **BC-Breaking**. However, I agree that logically it makes sense to decouple them.

Also if we decide not to do decouple, the documentation should be updated to reflect this behaviour of setting seed for CUDA generators as well.
https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.manual_seed.html?highlight=manual_seed#torch.manual_seed

EDIT: Look at PR https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/2811 for more idea about the motivation

This behavior of setting seed for CUDA has been there since [0.2](https://pytorch.org/docs/0.2.0/_modules/torch.html#manual_seed)

cc: @ngimel

---

[ezyang]: There is a simple fix: seeding is memoryless, so instead of tracking every modification to seed, track the "latest" CUDA seed only, and apply that on its own at the end. This won't fix leaks for other API calls but it seems more urgent to fix this one first.

@kshitij12345 do you think that's enough detail to prepare a PR?

---

[kshitij12345]: Ah. That makes sense. Thanks @ezyang

title: PyTorch issue 56022
body:
## 🐛 Bug

Per title

Currently, we directly `copy_` the result into the output tensor.
https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/d490e0120f32dcbb8b23e11eebd638b96b4b0898/aten/src/ATen/native/Linear.cpp#L644
If the output tensor can be broadcasted from result, it would generate wrong output.

## To Reproduce

```py
>>> a = torch.randn(5)
>>> b = torch.randn(5)
>>> torch.inner(a, b)
tensor(-4.2595)
>>> c = torch.randn(2)
>>> torch.inner(a, b, out=c)
tensor([-4.2595, -4.2595])
```

## Expected
```py
>>> a = torch.randn(5)
>>> b = torch.randn(5)
>>> torch.inner(a, b)
tensor(-4.2595)
>>> c = torch.randn(2)
>>> torch.inner(a, b, out=c)  # c should be resized to shape([1])
tensor([-4.2595])
```

title: PyTorch issue 56627
body:
## 🐛 Bug

In NumPy `std` and `var` of complex arrays returns a real result, but `torch.std`/`var` return a complex tensor with zero imaginary component.

## To Reproduce

Need to pass dim=0 needed because of #51127
```python
>>> import torch
>>> torch.rand(100, dtype=torch.complex128).var(dim=0).dtype
torch.complex128
>>> torch.rand(100, dtype=torch.complex128).std(dim=0).dtype
torch.complex128
```

Compare to NumPy:
```python
>>> import numpy as np
>>> np.random.rand(100).astype(complex).var().dtype
dtype('float64')
>>> np.random.rand(100).astype(complex).std().dtype
dtype('float64')
```

I've confirmed it's worked like this since at least PyTorch 1.6.

title: PyTorch issue 57273
body:
## 🐛 Bug

When using a `DataLoader` with `num_workers>0` and `pin_memory=True`, warnings trigger about `Leaking Caffe2 thread-pool after fork`. This warning shows multiple times, and populates the screen.
The warning doesn't trigger when either `num_workers=0` or `pin_memory=False`.

## To Reproduce

Steps to reproduce the behavior:

1. Run the following:
   ```python
    from torch.utils.data import DataLoader
    from torchvision.datasets import FakeData
    from torchvision.transforms import ToTensor
    
    
    def main():
        data = FakeData(transform=ToTensor())
        dataloader = DataLoader(data, num_workers=2, pin_memory=True)
        for e in range(1, 6):
            print(f'epoch {e}:')
            for _ in dataloader:
                pass
    
    
    if __name__ == '__main__':
        main()
    ```

### Output:
```
epoch 1:
epoch 2:
[W pthreadpool-cpp.cc:88] Warning: Leaking Caffe2 thread-pool after fork. (function pthreadpool)
[W pthreadpool-cpp.cc:88] Warning: Leaking Caffe2 thread-pool after fork. (function pthreadpool)
epoch 3:
[W pthreadpool-cpp.cc:88] Warning: Leaking Caffe2 thread-pool after fork. (function pthreadpool)
[W pthreadpool-cpp.cc:88] Warning: Leaking Caffe2 thread-pool after fork. (function pthreadpool)
epoch 4:
[W pthreadpool-cpp.cc:88] Warning: Leaking Caffe2 thread-pool after fork. (function pthreadpool)
[W pthreadpool-cpp.cc:88] Warning: Leaking Caffe2 thread-pool after fork. (function pthreadpool)
epoch 5:
[W pthreadpool-cpp.cc:88] Warning: Leaking Caffe2 thread-pool after fork. (function pthreadpool)
[W pthreadpool-cpp.cc:88] Warning: Leaking Caffe2 thread-pool after fork. (function pthreadpool)
```

## Expected behavior

No warnings

## Additional context

It looks like this warning was introduced in https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/54895. I don't quite follow the details there, though.

Perhaps, the warning message can be revised, so that it doesn't cause any confusion among users. --- [xkszltl]: We're seeing the same issue. title: PyTorch issue 57381
body:
Originally raised in https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/50937#discussion_r604346660

The COO to CSR tensor conversion is implemented in
https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/3a777b67926c5f02bc287b25e572c521d6d11fb0/torch/_tensor.py#L928-L940
and it consists of two time-consuming parts:
- coalescing if the COO tensor is uncoaleasced
- row indices compression procedure

Indeed, taking a coalesced COO sample with `size=(1000, 1000)` and `nnz=500`, about 1.5% of the `to_sparse_csr` call time is spend in coalescing and 98% of the time in running the row indices compression loop.

The slowness of the row indices compression loop has two origins:
- it is implemented in Python
- it uses a suboptimal algorithm

Indeed, a sample of row indices with `size[0] == 1000` and `nnz=500`, a pure Python algorithm in
https://github.com/pearu/gcs/blob/b54ba0cba9c853b797274ef26b6c42386f2cafa3/gcs/storage.py#L24-L45
is about 3x faster than used in `to_sparse_csr`, and when applying `numba.njit` to the `compress_indices` function, about 83x speedup is achieved compared to `to_sparse_csr`.

The performance of COO to CSR tensor conversion can be improved by implementing a more efficient row indices compression algorithm (see `compress_indices` referenced above) in C++.

cc @VitalyFedyunin @ngimel @aocsa @nikitaved @pearu @mruberry
comments:
[ngimel]: How about using `torch.searchsorted` for this?

---

[pearu]: Use unsafe CSR constructor when it becomes available: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/57274#discussion_r629580597

---

[pearu]: xref: [..conversion from COO to CSR is ridiculously slow on CUDA device.](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/16187#issuecomment-869735963)

---

[pearu]: Since CSR `crow_indices` are always 1D, use `torch.bucketize` instead of `torch.searchsorted` for efficiency.

---

[cpuhrsch]: cc @rusty1s

---

[rusty1s]: I've done some benchmarking efforts and pushed them to the `torch-sparse` package, see [here](https://github.com/rusty1s/pytorch_sparse/blob/master/benchmark/ptr2ind.py).

I compared the current PyTorch implementation, the `compress_indices` implementation, a vectorized version using `bincount` and `cumsum`, and the dedicated implementation sitting in [`torch-sparse`](https://github.com/rusty1s/pytorch_sparse/blob/master/csrc/cpu/convert_cpu.cpp#L7-L35), using real-world sparse matrices:
```
citationCiteseer.mat: shape=(268495, 268495) nnz=2313294
========================================================
current:            9.76767897605896
compress_indices:   3.5093854665756226
vectorized:         0.0039603453445132535
torch-sparse:       0.001211377943671027

web-Stanford.mat: shape=(281903, 281903) nnz=2312497
====================================================
current:            9.746762037277222
compress_indices:   3.2903430461883545
vectorized:         0.0038891834196740326
torch-sparse:       0.0012816107621381125

StocF-1465.mat: shape=(1465137, 1465137) nnz=21005389
=====================================================
current:            84.82162189483643
compress_indices:   29.867455005645752
vectorized:         0.030471684715964578
torch-sparse:       0.007548295534574068

ldoor.mat: shape=(952203, 952203) nnz=42493817
==============================================
current:            169.77982473373413
compress_indices:   60.181442975997925
vectorized:         0.07296083975529326
torch-sparse:       0.014217141677032818
```

---

[pearu]: @rusty1s thanks for the benchmarks! A couple of notes. The benchmarks compare pure single-thread Python and parallel C++ implementations and the `1 000..10 000`-fold difference is not surprising.

If possible, could you also benchmark
- the different algorithms under the same implementation environment, that is, all in C++ using parallel_for
- using searchsorted/bucketize approach that could provide immediate CUDA backend support to COO->CSR conversion

---

[rusty1s]: The `torch-sparse` C++ implementation already implements the `compress_indices` algorithms mentioned above. For the current COO2CSR PyTorch implementation, I don't think it's possible to make use of multi-threading at all.

I also updated the benchmark script to support both `searchsorted`/`bucketize` functions:
```
citationCiteseer.mat: shape=(268495, 268495) nnz=2313294
========================================================
torch-sparse: 0.00159161581043071
searchsorted: 0.005281335574898322
bucketize:    0.004996270923824101

web-Stanford.mat: shape=(281903, 281903) nnz=2312497
====================================================
torch-sparse: 0.001423873635250778
searchsorted: 0.0050520981201017745
bucketize:    0.00515870925077458

StocF-1465.mat: shape=(1465137, 1465137) nnz=21005389
=====================================================
torch-sparse: 0.011693406884915361
searchsorted: 0.03374433517456055
bucketize:    0.032992157496904075

ldoor.mat: shape=(952203, 952203) nnz=42493817
==============================================
torch-sparse: 0.017606527872488533
searchsorted: 0.030941883722941082
bucketize:    0.02959784530323638
```
Both are about 3 times slower than the current `torch-sparse` function (probably because of the extra `torch.arange` call?).

---

[pearu]: It sounds like torch-sparse C++/parallel_for implementation of the coo->csr operation is a way to go for CPU. Notice that this implementation needs a specialization for int64-COO -> int32-CSR because, in PyTorch, COO tensors use int64 indices while CSR tensors use int32 indices (because PyTorch is tied to LP64 MKL, and on CPU, MKL sparse tools are used for computations).
 
For indices stored on CUDA device, multiple choices are possible:
1. implement torch-sparse/compress_indices algorithm for CUDA
2. use bucketize for CUDA as a first instance when 1. is not available
3. copy indices to CPU, perform coo->csr operation using CPU method, copy crow_indices to CUDA as a first instance when 1. is not available.
4. anything else?

@rusty1s , what do you think?

---

[rusty1s]: I‘m in favor of option (1) or (2), and will work on that with @cpuhrsch.

---

[ngimel]: >For the current COO2CSR PyTorch implementation, I don't think it's possible to make use of multi-threading at all.

searchsorted/bucketize are dispatched to the same implementation, so it's not surprising that they produce similar results.
searchsorted/bucketize uses multithreading - it's doing independent binary searches for each element. Overhead from `arange` should be minimal (can be confirmed), so maybe the best thing is to add sequential implementation to searchsorted, so that all searchsorted usecases can benefit too, not just coo2csr?

title: PyTorch issue 59198
body:
## 🐛 Bug

Output shape of `torch.linalg.norm` diverges from numpy equivalent in edge case.

## To Reproduce

```python
import torch
import numpy as np

t = torch.rand((1, 1, 0))
a = t.numpy()

t_norm = torch.linalg.norm(t, ord=2, dim=(0, 1), keepdim=False)
a_norm = np.linalg.norm(a, ord=2, axis=(0, 1), keepdims=False)

assert t_norm.shape == a_norm.shape
```

- This also fails for `ord=-2` but works for all other orders
- This works fine for `keepdim=True`
- This also works fine for other degenerate input shapes

title: PyTorch issue 59248
body:
## 🐛 Bug

A large bunch of tests in test_unary_ufuncs fails on POWER9 after the vector intrinsic introduction in PyTorch 1.8 they look related hence pasted as a single issue.

## To Reproduce

Steps to reproduce the behavior:

1. run `test_unary_ufuncs.py` on PPC

## Expected behavior

Tests pass

## Environment

 - PyTorch Version (e.g., 1.0): 1.8.1
 - OS (e.g., Linux): Linux
 - How you installed PyTorch (`conda`, `pip`, source): source
 - Build command you used (if compiling from source): `CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release BUILD_TEST=0 PYTORCH_BUILD_VERSION=1.8.1 PYTORCH_BUILD_NUMBER=1 MAX_JOBS=$(nproc) BLAS=Eigen USE_FFMPEG=1 BUILD_CUSTOM_PROTOBUF=0 USE_IBVERBS=1 USE_CUDA=0 USE_METAL=0 python setup.py install`
 - Python version: 3.8.6

## Additional context

I'll try to categorize the tests into failure types and (possible) explanations:

- test_contig_vs_every_other_angle_cpu_complex64: 256 / 513 elements failed `The greatest difference was 3.0628208592534065 (-3.096264600753784 vs. -0.033443741500377655)`: Looks like the VSX version gets bogus values here
-  test_contig_vs_every_other_angle_cpu_float32/float64, test_non_contig_angle_cpu_float32/float64, test_non_contig_expand_angle_cpu_int16/int32/int64/int8: `greatest difference was 3.1415927410125732 (0.0 vs. 3.1415927410125732)`: This is an omission: The VSX implementation does not return PI when the argument is negative: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/afdfd2288ab839ece89e667e44777201536b2d82/aten/src/ATen/cpu/vec/vec256/vsx/vec256_double_vsx.h#L257

title: PyTorch issue 59312
body:
## 🐛 Bug

`torch.utils.data.DataLoader`, when works with a customized `torch.util.data.IterableDataset`, scans the dataset **twice**, as shown in my diff D28508287.

To make it easy to reproduce the problem, Shunting Zhang simplified the above diff into paste P415580779, which reveals that `torch.utils.data.DataLoader` doesn’t stop calling next after the IterableDataset raised StopIteration exception.

## To Reproduce

Please try the following customized IterableDataset with DataLoader.

```python
class CachingIterator:  # depends on CachedIterable so cannot be nested class.
    def __init__(self, ds: CachedIterable):
        self.ds = ds
        self.pos = -1
        self.raise_num = 0

    def __next__(self) -> Any:
        if self.raise_num == 1:
            self.raise_num == 2
            raise StopIteration

        if self.ds.itrt is not None:
            i = next(self.ds.itrt, None)
            if i is not None:
                self.ds.cache.append(i)
                return i
            else:
                print("CachingIterator stopes")
                self.ds.itrt = None  # notify CachedIterable.__iter__ to yield cache.
                self.raise_num += 1
                raise StopIteration
        else:
            if self.pos + 1 < len(self.ds.cache):
                self.pos = self.pos + 1
                return self.ds.cache[self.pos]
            else:
                raise StopIteration
```

## Additional context

@dzhulgakov identified the bug is due to that the fetcher doesn't remember it had encountered StopIteration. For details, please see his comment at https://fb.quip.com/nvyoAJgmzBzZ#PBKACAQVfEj


cc @VitalyFedyunin @ejguan @SsnL
comments: [ejguan]: Nice catch!

title: PyTorch issue 59378
body:
## 🐛 Bug

Thanks to @pmeier, a bug of `IterDataPipe` was found. For each `IterDataPipe` class, we raise `NotImplementedError` in `__len__` in some cases such as `length` is -1 or prior `DataPipe` is not `Sized`.

When we apply `tqdm` to the `DataPipe` instance without explicit length, `len(dp)` will be called and the `NotImplementedError` will emit. So, we can never use `tqdm` for such kind of DataPipe. However,  we are actually expecting DataPipe with `NotImplementedError` as an infinite length. 

And, based on the definition of [`NotImplementedError`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/exceptions.html#NotImplementedError), it's not an appropriate Exception since this exception normally raises from base class to enforce derived class implementing this method.

And, considering the implementation of `tqdm`, we should switch `NotImplementedError` to `TypeError`.
https://github.com/tqdm/tqdm/blob/05ad200df3ccbc21c5c840d064e619ec9838f902/tqdm/std.py#L983-L990

## To Reproduce

```py
>>> import tqdm
>>> class DP(IterDataPipe):
...     def __len__(self):
...         raise NotImplementedError
... 
>>> dp = DP()
>>> pbar = tqdm.tqdm(dp)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/Users/erjia/tools/miniconda3/envs/pt/lib/python3.8/site-packages/tqdm/std.py", line 985, in __init__
    total = len(iterable)
  File "<stdin>", line 3, in __len__
NotImplementedError
```

## Expected behavior

By switching to `TypeError`, DataPipe works with `tqdm` correctly.
BTW, we should not create `__len__` at runtime since this method is attached to `class` object. When we change it, behavior of all instances will change. Any other suggestions to fix it are welcomed.

cc @SsnL @VitalyFedyunin @ejguan
comments:
[ssnl]: see also 
https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/6620d7d688c9482a00b1a9827187f135b72b7862/torch/utils/data/sampler.py#L26-L51

title: PyTorch issue 598
body:
Hi,

there is something strange in the `backward` step (or maybe something I don't understand). If I define a Module that takes 3 inputs, the `grad_input` has to be of size 3, right ? But this is not the case here (from the backward_hook point of view):

```import torch
from torch.autograd import Variable
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

def bh(m,go,gi):
    print("Grad Input")
    print(go)
    print("Grad Output")
    print(gi)

class M(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        super(M,self).__init__()
        self.register_backward_hook(bh)


    def forward(self,x,y,z):
        return (x+y+z)

x=Variable(torch.randn(1,5),requires_grad=True)
y=Variable(torch.randn(1,5),requires_grad=True)
z=Variable(torch.randn(1,5),requires_grad=True)

criterion=nn.MSELoss()
mod=M()
out=mod(x,y,z)
loss=criterion(out,Variable(torch.randn(1,5)))
loss.backward()```

In that case, when I print grad_input throught the hook function, it is just composed of two elements... Could you tell me where am I wrong ? But `x.grad, y.grad and z.grad` seem correctly computed

cc @ezyang @gchanan @zou3519 @SsnL @albanD @gqchen
comments:
[apaszke]: Ok, so the problem is that module hooks are actually registered on the last function that the module has created. In your case `x + y + z` is computed as `((x + y) + z)` so the hook is registered on that `(_ + z)` operation, and this is why you're getting only two grad inputs.

We'll definitely have to resolve this but it will need a large change in the autograd internals. However, right now @colesbury is rewriting them to make it possible to have multiple functions dispatched in parallel, and they would heavily conflict with his work. For now use only Variable hooks (or module hooks, but not on containers). Sorry!

---

[karandwivedi42]: @apaszke Is the refactor you referred to this one: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/1016? Since that is merged, is this bug solvable now?

---

[apaszke]: Yes. We can remove the on hold label, but I don't have any good solutions in mind

---

[shubhamjain0594]: @apaszke Any progress or ideas on this issue, about how to solve. I may write a PR if required.

---

[apaszke]: No progress on this. It's actually a pretty hard problem to solve properly. I think I'd be ok with disallowing registering hooks on modules that have submodules as a partial workaround.

---

[whr94621]: @apaszke  Any progress or ideas on this issue? I also encounter this problem we the output of forward function is the result of ```torch.chunk```.

---

[apaszke]: Not yet. It's really hard to solve, and not such a common pitfall. I think we might just disable the function for now.

---

[t-vi]: So here is a copypaste from the duplicate I filed today.

The [pytorch documentation for `nn.Module.register_backward_hook`](http://pytorch.org/docs/master/nn.html#torch.nn.Module.register_backward_hook) says:

> The grad_input and grad_output may be tuples if the module has multiple inputs or outputs. The hook should not modify its arguments, but it can optionally return a new gradient with respect to input that will be used in place of grad_input in subsequent computation.

This is not quite accurate:
- `grad_output` is indeed the gradient of the loss w.r.t. the layer output. So if you have a layer l and do, say, `y = l(x) ; loss = y.sum(); loss.backward()`, you get the gradient of `loss` w.r.t. `y`. So far so good.
-  What is unexpected to most users is that `grad_input` are the inputs to the last operation in the layer. For linear layers, this is fairly complete, as the last op is `torch.addmm` multiplying the input with the weight and adding the bias. For other layers (e.g. do a Sequential, it’ll be the last op of the last layer, the inputs not even remotely related to the sequential layer’s inputs). You can see what will be used by looking at `y.grad_fn`.

This may be confusing to users, e.g. https://discuss.pytorch.org/t/exact-meaning-of-grad-input-and-grad-output/14186

So improving the docs is one thing, but should we make the hook actually live up to the description?

I see three strategies:
- Documentation only.
- The straightforward way of providing input gradients: collect the grad_ins with variable hooks and call the module hook when we have all of them. We loose the ability to return a different gradient.
- The somewhat convoluted way: If the module has hooks, wrap the module forward in a autograd function - similar to checkpointing. The the variable hook for the output would do the right thing.

---

[apaszke]: I think we should just remove this functionality for now (raise an error when `register_backward_hook` is called). It never worked correctly and is really hard to implement. I don't think the checkpointing way is good in this case, because it can cause certain backward hooks to trigger multiple times, and that's generally not what you want. It also doesn't work with `.grad`.

---

[karandwivedi42]: @apaszke Remove this functionality from all modules or only from Container type modules? If entirely removed, how should we obtain, for example, gradient norm?

---

[marcoancona]: I encountered the same issue. Is there any alternative to replace the gradient of a *sequence* of operations? Similarly to [custom_gradient](https://www.tensorflow.org/api_docs/python/tf/custom_gradient) in Tensorflow 1.8?

What I am trying to do is override the gradient of an entire layer at once. The use-case is to implement some attribution methods as in https://github.com/marcoancona/DeepExplain, by modifying the gradient of the output with respect to the input features.

---

[tete1030]: Could this problem be properly noted in the doc before some solution?  Since it has existed so long and misleads many people including me.

---

[zou3519]: > Could this problem be properly noted in the doc before some solution? Since it has existed so long and misleads many people including me.

Sure, let's do that for now.

---

[cpuhrsch]: The corresponding pull requested https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/1016 mentioned by @apaszke has been marked as merged.

@ludc - is this issue resolved?

---

[apaszke]: No, this has never been fixed, and I think it's a wontfix for the foreseeable future.

---

[t-vi]: I must say I did like @albanD 's approach in #12573 . And think it might be worthwhile even if it has limitations (as long as hooks not being called is the only harm), one might warn if by the end of the forward the inputs have been inplace-modified..

Of course, my ideal PyTorch has inplace hooks for #7313, which might help with the issues that killed #12573.

---

[albanD]: @t-vi This approach lead to other issues with inplace ops in the autograd.
I will have time in the next couple of month to work on them properly and hopefully fix them all.

A note has been added in the documentation though explaining that they are mostly broken at the moment.

---

[gchanan]: added @albanD to assignee list based on the above conversation.

---

[yaroslavvb]: Can someone summarize why this turned out to be hard to implement?

---

[albanD]: Hi,

As many things touching the autograd engine, this is hard because of inplace operations!
We need a way to attach to all the differentiable inputs/outputs and get all the gradients for the input at once.

The current approach simply hooks onto the last Node associated with the first output. So for complex modules, it misses all intermediary results and is fairly broken.

The new approach I tried to introduce above was to add a specific Node into the graph that will be inserted at the input and output and will be able to call the callback with the saved data.
The current blocking (but that will be resolved soon 🤞 ) problem is that the autograd engine does not really allow for these NoOp Nodes. It also does not allow to return views of the input if there is more than one output (because in the case where an inplace operation is performed on this output, the graph need to be rewritten and we can only do that if a Node has a single output).

Not sure if it's really clear. Does that help?

---

[yaroslavvb]: Yes, makes sense. Restricting to single-output modules seems fine since that's the most common use-case.

PS, the motivation for module backward hooks is that the user code becomes simpler. Example -- implement https://arxiv.org/abs/1510.01799 . Currently this requires a module forward hook to save the activations, then backward hook to combine saved activations with backprops. Module backward hook would let you do this in a single function

---

[AlexBinder]: Hi all,

is there any fix intended other than forbidding? I ask because there are people who implement "non-pure-gradient" backward attributions (e.g. LRP) via custom stuff in backward hooks in the context of explaining ML predictions. For all those people it would be helpful, while forbidding backward hooks would cause a bigger problem as far as I can see.


I think I stumbled over something related when implementing a backward attribution and using it on GPU:
nn.Conv2d with bias=True

in short: on the CPU i see the grad inputs which i would expect (input feature map x, conv-weights, conv-bias)
on the GPU i see 2 input shapes to what looks like a bias addition. 

code attached below. apologies for code style like from public restrooms. in this case it creates an inconsistency CPU-GPU

#code starts here

from __future__ import print_function, division
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import numpy as np

def add_ahook(model):
    for module in model.modules():
        module.register_backward_hook(testbackwardhook)

def testbackwardhook(module, relevance_input, relevance_output):

    print( 'types of rel inp, relout', type(relevance_input), type(relevance_output), len(relevance_input), len(relevance_output))

    print( 'out[0].shape', relevance_output[0].shape)
    for k in range(len(relevance_input)):
      if hasattr(relevance_input[k],'shape'):
        print( k, 'relevance_input['+str(k)+'].shape', relevance_input[k].shape)
      else:
        print( k, 'relevance_input['+str(k)+']', relevance_input[k])

def runstuff():
  c=nn.Conv2d(in_channels=5, out_channels=9, kernel_size=3, stride=1,
                     padding=1, groups=1, bias=True, dilation=1)

  add_ahook(c)

  x=torch.ones((1,5,7,7))
  x.requires_grad=True  

  device2=torch.device('cuda:0')
  #device2=torch.device('cpu')

  x=x.to(device2)
  c=c.to(device2)

  z=c(x)
  print('forwardoutput',z.shape)
  torch.sum(z).backward(retain_graph=True)  

if __name__=='__main__':
  runstuff()

title: PyTorch issue 59859
body:
## 🐛 Bug

Build Source code with ninja is officially supported. 
But due to Ninja issue https://github.com/ninja-build/ninja/issues/1976, the incremental build isn't supported on Windows.
So, it rebuilds all code in fact if we rerun ` python setup.py install ` on Windows

So far, we can use sccache to accelerate the rebuild.
With nothing changed, it still takes 7 minutes to rerun` python setup.py install ` for CPU build on SSD 
For HDD, it takes about 20 minutes

## To Reproduce

Steps to reproduce the behavior:
1. clone pytorch source code on windows
2. build it
3. rerun 'python setup.py install' to rebuild.

## Expected behavior
The edit and compile cycle on windows doesn't take so much time.
For small change in leaf node, rebuild should be finished as soon as Linux.

## Additional context
From the exception message in #59688, I think the root cause is that ninja fails to parse Visual C++ compiler option when rebuilding.



cc @malfet @seemethere @walterddr @peterjc123 @mszhanyi @skyline75489 @nbcsm
comments:
[peterjc123]: Looking into the ninja_deps file uploaded by @mszhanyi, isn't it just because Ninja fails to detect the dependencies for the objects generated by the RC compiler since it doesn't support `/showIncludes`? So, the possible actions for this problem are:
1. Provide the option `/showIncludes` for the RC compiler at the MSFT side
2. Ignore RC file dep generation at the Ninja side
3. Filter unintended options in the dep file before rebuild at the PyTorch side

---

[peterjc123]: I figured out that by using `set(CMAKE_NINJA_CMCLDEPS_RC OFF)` in CMake, it defers the RC stuffs to the RC compiler instead of writing them into the ninja rules, thus should fix the problem.

---

[mszhanyi]: I just want to know more details. [grpc](https://github.com/grpc/grpc) has protobuf submodule too, it uses cmake+ninja too.
Do you know why it can rebuild on Windows without  `set(CMAKE_NINJA_CMCLDEPS_RC OFF)`

---

[peterjc123]: > I just want to know more details. [grpc](https://github.com/grpc/grpc) has protobuf submodule too, it uses cmake+ninja too.
> Do you know why it can rebuild on Windows without `set(CMAKE_NINJA_CMCLDEPS_RC OFF)`

I will have a try on grpc when I have time.

title: PyTorch issue 60187
body:
## 🐛 Bug

On windows, tensorboard_trace_handler use the separador \  in the file path instead of / or \\\ breaking the json file.


## To Reproduce

Steps to reproduce the behavior:

1. Setup the Profiler. Example:
`with torch.profiler.profile(
    activities=[
        torch.profiler.ProfilerActivity.CPU,
        torch.profiler.ProfilerActivity.CUDA],
    schedule=torch.profiler.schedule(
        wait=2,
        warmup=3,
        active=6),
    on_trace_ready=torch.profiler.tensorboard_trace_handler('./result', worker_name='worker0'),
    record_shapes=True,
    profile_memory=True,
    with_stack=True
  ) as p:`

title: PyTorch issue 60892
body:
## 🐛 Bug

`torch.linalg.eigh` fails on the gpu if device not set beforehand (thanks @ngimel for the fix). 

## To Reproduce

```python
import torch
torch.__version__
# nightly 1.10

# fix is next line
# torch.cuda.set_device("cuda:1")
device = torch.device("cuda:1")

a = torch.randn(20, 50)
b = a @ a.t() + 1e-3 * torch.eye(20)
b = b.to(device)
torch.linalg.eigh(b)
```
results in 
```
RuntimeError: cusolver error: CUSOLVER_STATUS_INTERNAL_ERROR, when calling `cusolverDnXsyevd( handle, params, jobz, uplo, n, CUDA_R_32F, reinterpret_cast<void*>(A), lda, CUDA_R_32F, reinterpret_cast<void*>(W), CUDA_R_32F, reinterpret_cast<void*>(bufferOnDevice), workspaceInBytesOnDevice, reinterpret_cast<void*>(bufferOnHost), workspaceInBytesOnHost, info)`

```


## Expected behavior

this should work, and works when device is set.

## Environment
```

Is debug build: False
CUDA used to build PyTorch: 11.1
ROCM used to build PyTorch: N/A

OS: Ubuntu 18.04.4 LTS (x86_64)

Python platform: Linux-4.15.0-99-generic-x86_64-with-glibc2.17
Is CUDA available: True
CUDA runtime version: 11.3.58

cuDNN version: Could not collect

Versions of relevant libraries:

## Additional context

seemingly breaks some gpytorch linear algebra. 


cc @ezyang @gchanan @zou3519 @bdhirsh @jbschlosser @anjali411 @ngimel @jianyuh @nikitaved @pearu @mruberry @heitorschueroff @walterddr @IvanYashchuk @xwang233 @Lezcano
comments:
[ngimel]: Hi-pri as it is a crash for all practical purposes, it nukes cuda context so any operations after this are not possible.

---

[xwang233]: I've tried that adding a `CUDAGuard` may solve the issue

```diff
diff --git a/aten/src/ATen/native/cuda/BatchLinearAlgebraLib.cu b/aten/src/ATen/native/cuda/BatchLinearAlgebraLib.cu
index 0b10b34833..2a788c9578 100644
--- a/aten/src/ATen/native/cuda/BatchLinearAlgebraLib.cu
+++ b/aten/src/ATen/native/cuda/BatchLinearAlgebraLib.cu
@@ -1030,6 +1030,8 @@ template <typename scalar_t>
 static void apply_syevd(Tensor& values, Tensor& vectors, Tensor& infos, bool upper, bool compute_eigenvectors) {
   using value_t = typename c10::scalar_value_type<scalar_t>::type;
 
+  ::at::cuda::CUDAGuard guard(values.device());
+
   cublasFillMode_t uplo = upper ? CUBLAS_FILL_MODE_UPPER : CUBLAS_FILL_MODE_LOWER;
   cusolverEigMode_t jobz = compute_eigenvectors ? CUSOLVER_EIG_MODE_VECTOR : CUSOLVER_EIG_MODE_NOVECTOR;
 
```

but I'm not sure if this is the right approach. I didn't see many `CUDAGuard`s exist in the codebase. Is there any dispatch logic that guarantees this, otherwise, wouldn't many operators have this problem as well?

---

[ngimel]: DeviceGuards are usually codegened to the first tensor in the list of arguments, and recently automatic codegened tests that guarantee that all inputs are on the same device have been added, so it's unclear why the correct behavior doesn't happen automatically. cc @bdhirsh for codegen questions.

---

[ngimel]: It looks like all non-structured CompositeExplicitAutograd functions have device checks and device guards omitted:
```
::std::tuple<at::Tensor,at::Tensor> wrapper__linalg_eigh(const at::Tensor & self, c10::string_view UPLO) {
    // No device check


  // DeviceGuard omitted
  return at::native::linalg_eigh(self, UPLO);
}
```
(from generated RegisterCompositeExplicitAutograd.cpp)

---

[ngimel]: I guess this (and other linalg ops) should not be registered to CompositeExplicitAutograd, because they use Dispatchstub inside, #40564 seems to be related.

---

[bdhirsh]: @ngimel that looks like the right diagnosis to me: `at::linalg_eigh()` is definitely not backend-agnostic, since it has separate cpu and cuda implementations using dispatch stub.

So updating native_functions.yaml entry to point CPU/CUDA to `linalg_eigh` should correct the op (so it correctly errors if we try to call it with another backend), and also add the missing device guard logic for cuda.

---

[ngimel]: @ezyang, @bdhirsh do we have some tests to identify incorrect dispatch keys in the native_functions.yaml? I'm pretty sure this is the case for a lot of linalg (and maybe some other) functions. cc @IvanYashchuk

---

[IvanYashchuk]: cuSOLVER fails as its handle and temporary work buffers are initialized on a different device than input tensors. Unfortunately,  it was overlooked for some functions, when introducing the dispatch stubs, that the keys in native_functions.yaml need to be changed as well. I'll submit a patch fixing that.

I've noticed other interesting behavior
```python
In [1]: import torch

In [2]: device = torch.device("cuda:1")

In [3]: torch.linalg.eigh(torch.randn(3, 3, device='cuda:0'))
Device is 0
Device is 0
Out[3]: 
torch.return_types.linalg_eigh(
eigenvalues=tensor([-1.8241,  2.3956,  2.9491], device='cuda:0'),
eigenvectors=tensor([[-0.2985,  0.6476,  0.7010],
        [ 0.4553, -0.5489,  0.7010],
        [ 0.8388,  0.5285, -0.1310]], device='cuda:0'))

In [4]: torch.lu_solve(torch.randn(3, 1, device=device), torch.randn(3, 3, device=device), torch.arange(1, 4, device=device, dtype=torch.int))
   ...: 
Out[4]: 
tensor([[ 1.4775],
        [-2.1323],
        [-4.3082]], device='cuda:1')

In [5]: torch.linalg.eigh(torch.randn(3, 3, device='cuda:0'))
Device is 1
Device is 1
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
RuntimeError                              Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-5-f46d4d323bc3> in <module>
----> 1 torch.linalg.eigh(torch.randn(3, 3, device='cuda:0'))

RuntimeError: cusolver error: CUSOLVER_STATUS_INTERNAL_ERROR, when calling `cusolverDnSsyevd(handle, jobz, uplo, n, A, lda, W, work, lwork, info)`
```

`lu_solve` also uses (incorrectly) CompositeExplicitAutograd key, it should use CPU, CUDA keys because dispatch stubs are used in the implementation. For this input, size MAGMA is used internally and it seems that it sets the device somewhere. Using "CPU, CUDA" keys for `lu_solve` removes this behavior.

---

[bdhirsh]: I made a little test that looks for ops that map to `CompositeExplicitAutograd`, but use DispatchStub. It's a little fragile, so idk if it's worth landing - especially because a lot of ops follow a weird pattern that turn up false positives - e.g. `at::cumprod` is `CompositeExplicitAutograd`, because it has a derivatives entry, but it calls into `at::_cumprod` for all of the real work.

But the ops that came up on the list are:

```
eig
lu_solve
linalg_eigh
```

@IvanYashchuk  has a fix for `lu_solve` and `linalg_eigh` in his PR.

`eig.e` also looks wrong, since it's CompositeExplicitAutograd but has separate DispatchStub kernels for cpu and cuda. The `eig` (functional) op looks fine though, since it redispatches to `eig.e`, but also has its own derivatives formula.

---

[ezyang]: i say land it, at the very least its failure will make people think twice, even if it is a false positive

---

[bdhirsh]: PR for it here: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/61134. Let me know if you still think it's worth landing after taking a look at it 😛

title: PyTorch issue 61251
body:
## 🐛 Bug
This is a followup to https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/37499. As it was decided to handle NaNs in `x.eig()` for a tensor `x` there, I suppose they should also be handled for `torch.linalg.eig(x)`.

### To reproduce
```
import torch
import numpy as np
A = np.nan * torch.ones((3,3))
torch.linalg.eig(A)
```
This currently gives:
```
Intel MKL ERROR: Parameter 3 was incorrect on entry to SGEBAL.
Process finished with exit code 139 (interrupted by signal 11: SIGSEGV)
```
### Environment
 - PyTorch Version: 1.9.0+cu102
 - OS (e.g., Linux): Ubuntu 20.04
 - How you installed PyTorch (`conda`, `pip`, source): pip
 - Python version: 3.7.7
 - CUDA/cuDNN version: 10.2


cc @ezyang @gchanan @zou3519 @bdhirsh @jbschlosser @anjali411 @jianyuh @nikitaved @pearu @mruberry @heitorschueroff @walterddr @IvanYashchuk @xwang233 @Lezcano
comments:
[jamaliki]: I would love to help here as my first contribution if I can get some guidance.

---

[malfet]: I could not reproduce the crash on Mac, even if code is compiled without MKL:
```
% python -c "import torch;print(torch.__version__);print(torch.linalg.eig(float('nan') * torch.ones(3, 3)))" 
1.10.0a0+git1f03965
** On entry to SGEBAL, parameter number  3 had an illegal value
** On entry to SGEHRD, parameter number  2 had an illegal value
** On entry to SORGHR, parameter number  2 had an illegal value
** On entry to SHSEQR, parameter number  4 had an illegal value
** On entry to SGEBAK, parameter number  4 had an illegal value
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<string>", line 1, in <module>
RuntimeError: torch.linalg.eig: Argument 4 has illegal value
%%  /usr/bin/python3 -c "import torch;print(torch.__version__);print(torch.linalg.eig(float('nan') * torch.ones(3, 3)))"
1.9.0
** On entry to SGEBAL, parameter number  3 had an illegal value
** On entry to SGEHRD, parameter number  2 had an illegal value
** On entry to SORGHR, parameter number  2 had an illegal value
** On entry to SHSEQR, parameter number  4 had an illegal value
** On entry to SGEBAK, parameter number  4 had an illegal value
Python(31967,0x102a97d40) malloc: Incorrect checksum for freed object 0x13af07e18: probably modified after being freed.
Corrupt value: 0xffc000007fc00000
Python(31967,0x102a97d40) malloc: *** set a breakpoint in malloc_error_break to debug
zsh: abort      /usr/bin/python3 -c 
```

---

[IvanYashchuk]: [Balancing routines in LAPACK](https://www.netlib.org/lapack/lug/node51.html) (used inside eigenvalues solvers) do not work well with NaNs or Infs.
Reference LAPACK implementation has checks for NaN and _may_ exit correctly
https://github.com/Reference-LAPACK/lapack/blob/77a0ceb6c9a3c757e7039027f1f324e2811e6ee0/SRC/sgebal.f#L345-L351
But I compiled PyTorch with lapack=netlib and lapack=openblas (both use reference lapack implementation) from conda-forge and the program just hangs on invalid inputs.
PyTorch compiled with MKL segfaults.

[MKL recommends](https://software.intel.com/sites/products/documentation/doclib/mkl_sa/11/mkl_lapack_examples/lapackro2.htm) testing input for NaN and Inf:
> The standard LAPACK functions do not check the input data (matrices) for IEEE 754 floating point INFs or NANS. INFs and NANs will propagate through the computations and may cause unexpected results or instabilities. It is the user's responsibility to ensure that the input data do not contain INFs nor NaNs.
The LAPACKE (C interfaces to LAPACK) functions do check for NaNs in the input data (matrices) , and an error code is returned if a NAN is found.

eig, eigvals are the only problematic functions causing crash, other functions from torch.linalg either propagate nan, or raise an error from LAPACK.

SciPy's linalg functions have a flag `check_finite=True` that by default checks inputs for NaNs or Infs and raises an error.  With `check_finite=False` no check is performed.

To avoid crashes or freezes immediate possible fixes:

1. Add a check for NaNs and Infs in the beginning of `linalg_eig_out`, `linalg_eigvals_out` in `aten/src/ATen/native/BatchLinearAlgebra.cpp`.
  It would raise an error if at least one element of any matrix in the batched input is not finite.
  This behavior would be similar to other linalg functions that raise an error.
2. In `apply_linalg_eig` from `aten/src/ATen/native/BatchLinearAlgebraKernel.cpp` check for NaNs or Infs per each matrix in the batch. If NaN or Inf element is present in the matrix skip calling `lapackEig` in the for loop for that matrix and fill the output with NaN/Inf instead.
  Do the same for `apply_linalg_eig` from `aten/src/ATen/native/cuda/BatchLinearAlgebra.cu`.
  This would enable correct computations for matrices in the batch that have finite elements.

@mruberry, what do you think is a better option? I lean towards propagating NaNs or Infs without raising an error.

---

[IvanYashchuk]: @jamaliki, thank you for your interest in contibuting to PyTorch! Do have experience building PyTorch from source? Here are the instructions https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch#from-source

For fixing this problem I think CPU only build is sufficient and it is faster to build. If you prefer to use conda for development work, you can use [this environment file](https://github.com/Quansight/pearu-sandbox/blob/master/conda-envs/pytorch-cuda-dev.yaml). Then compile PyTorch with `DEBUG=1 USE_CUDA=0 python setup.py develop`. LAPACK should be picked up automatically from conda environment, but please check the logs in the beginning of the compilation for the following lines:
```
--   USE_BLAS              : 1
--     BLAS                : mkl
--   USE_LAPACK            : 1
--     LAPACK              : mkl
```
In addition to proposed fixes in my previous message, the pull request would need a test with a link to this issue. The test should be added to `test/test_linalg.py` file.

---

[jamaliki]: Hi, @IvanYashchuk. Thank you so much, I will start looking through the compilation instructions. For what it's worth, I think option 2 sounds better too because it seems more in line with the rest of torch's behaviour. But, I will wait for your decision for that part.

---

[malfet]: Both MKL and OpenBLAS implement [LAPACKE_get_nancheck](https://www.netlib.org/lapack/explore-html/d2/db1/lapacke__nancheck_8c_a8e1f1fb0ed6d571c26e80242572be8e2.html) but it still causes hard crash when MKL is used and seems to be missing in MacOS LAPACK implementation

---

[jamaliki]: @malfet so then it would make sense to handle this on the torch side right

---

[malfet]: @jamaliki yes, essentially it would be great to introduce a context manager, that could be used to enable/disable nanchecks, i.e. to have something like:
```
with torch.linalg.nancheck(Flase):
  # This will run faster but might crash
   torch.linalg.eig(torch.linalg.eig(float('nan') * torch.ones(3, 3)))
```

---

[jamaliki]: @malfet Interesting, is there an example of doing this through the C++ API? I'm assuming the C++ code will see a flag set or something and only check for NaNs then?

---

[malfet]: Sample below reproduces that crash if MKL-2020 is used as LAPACK backend even when nancheck is set to true:
```
// Compile me as
// gcc foo.c -lmkl_lapack95_lp64 -lmkl_blas95_lp64 -lmkl_intel_lp64 -lmkl_sequential -lmkl_core -lm -L/home/conda/miniconda3/lib/
#include <stdio.h>
#include <math.h>

void sgeev_(char *jobvl, char *jobvr, int *n, float *a, int *lda, float *wr, float *wi, float* vl, int *ldvl, float *vr, int *ldvr, float *work, int *lwork, int *info);

int main(void) {
  char jobvl = 'N';
  char jobvr = 'V';
  int n = 3;
  float a[9];
  int lda = 3;
  float w[6];
  float* vl = 0;
  int ldvl = 1;
  float vr[9];
  int ldvr = 3;
  float work[102];
  int lwork = 102;
  int info[3];

  float *wr = w;
  float *wi = w + n;

  for(int i = 0; i < 9; ++i) {
      a[i] = NAN;
  }
  sgeev_(&jobvl, &jobvr, &n, a, &lda, wr, wi, vl, &ldvl, vr, &ldvr, work, &lwork, info);
  printf("info[0]=%d\n", info[0]);
  return 0;
}
```

```
$ LD_LIBRARY_PATH=~/miniconda3/lib lldb ./a.out 
(lldb) target create "./a.out"
Current executable set to '/home/conda/a.out' (x86_64).
(lldb) r
Process 3057295 launched: '/home/conda/a.out' (x86_64)

Intel MKL ERROR: Parameter 3 was incorrect on entry to SGEBAL.
Process 3057295 stopped
* thread #1, name = 'a.out', stop reason = signal SIGSEGV: invalid address (fault address: 0x7ffffffff000)
    frame #0: 0x00007ffff1b92932 libmkl_core.so`mkl_lapack_sgehrd + 386
libmkl_core.so`mkl_lapack_sgehrd:
->  0x7ffff1b92932 <+386>: movups %xmm0, 0x10(%rbp,%rcx,4)
    0x7ffff1b92937 <+391>: addq   $0x8, %rcx
    0x7ffff1b9293b <+395>: cmpq   %rdi, %rcx
    0x7ffff1b9293e <+398>: jb     0x7ffff1b9292d            ; <+381>
(lldb) p (int)LAPACKE_get_nancheck()
(int) $0 = 1

```

---

[jamaliki]: @malfet Oh I see, what I was asking was if there is any example in the pytorch codebase of passing the status of a python context manager like `torch.linalg.nancheck(False)` to, for example, the C++ code in `aten/src/ATen/native/BatchLinearAlgebraKernel.cpp` such that if `nancheck` is true, I can add a check in `aten/src/ATen/native/BatchLinearAlgebraKernel.cpp` to fill with nans, otherwise just run normally.

---

[IvanYashchuk]: > Both MKL and OpenBLAS implement [LAPACKE_get_nancheck](https://www.netlib.org/lapack/explore-html/d2/db1/lapacke__nancheck_8c_a8e1f1fb0ed6d571c26e80242572be8e2.html) but it still causes hard crash when MKL is used and seems to be missing in MacOS LAPACK implementation

@malfet, yes there are nan checks in LAPACKE (C wrapper of Fortran LAPACK). But we don't use that wrapper, we use Fortran LAPACK library and in your snippet also Fortran LAPACK is used (`sgeev_` vs `LAPACKE_sgeev`).

---

[v0dro]: I can take up this issue.

---

[v0dro]: After discussion with @IvanYashchuk , the following approaches were discussed for tacking this issue:
1. Introduce a context manager `nancheck` globally. This would require using introducing global changes into pytorch and much more discussion and deliberation that warranted by this issue.
2. Introduce a keyword argument `nancheck` to `eig()` to toggle whether the `NaN` checking should happen before the `eig()` routine is called. This is also non-trivial due to policies on forward and backward compatibility. 
3. Loop over the data, check for NaNs and propagate them to the result. The looping cost is supposed to be much lesser than the LAPACK function cost. Implementing and benchmarking this should provide a more informed answer.

Option 3 seems like the fastest way to implement this feature.

---

[lezcano]: Re the context manager. This looks related to the work that @ezyang was doing on `FiniteTensor`, but now he's on leave, so not sure who to contact about that. cc @mruberry for pointers.

---

[xwang233]: It's nice to have the `nancheck` functionality for linalg functions. This will definitely avoid many unexpected errors from linalg backend library functions.

I suggest that we have the `nancheck` functionality **disabled** by default. Doing reduction is not a simple task and will cause device-host sync if executed on GPU. This may significantly decrease linalg performance on GPU.

---

[IvanYashchuk]: `nancheck=False` guarantees that your program would halt with a segfault. `torch.linalg.eig` is the only function in linalg that segfaults on input with nan, all other functions either propagate nan or raise an error.
I proposed two options here for handling nans https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/61251#issuecomment-878910619 either check the input upfront before the computation and raise an error or do it per matrix in the batch and fill with nans.
`torch.linalg.eig` is also special in that even with inputs on CUDA it would internally do transfers to CPU, compute the result, and send it back to CUDA device. There are already lots of device-host syncs and nothing can be done about it; there's no non-symmetric eigenvalue decomposition in cuSOLVER and MAGMA doesn't have pure GPU function for this operation.

---

[lezcano]: I like the option of simply checking the whole input for nans and raising an error. I think that this is a good compromise, since most of the time, once you find a `NaN`, chances are your program is going nowhere. Now, if someone wants a finer grain control, they could do the check themselves before calling the function and filter the matrices that have `NaN`s from their batch.

---

[IvanYashchuk]: Alright, if nobody else votes for propagating the `NaN`s, let's go with the first option:
>Add a check for NaNs and Infs in the beginning of linalg_eig_out, linalg_eigvals_out in aten/src/ATen/native/BatchLinearAlgebra.cpp.
It would raise an error if at least one element of any matrix in the batched input is not finite.
This behavior would be similar to other linalg functions that raise an error.

title: PyTorch issue 61656
body:
## 🐛 Bug

Calling `torch.median()` on an empty tensor causes segfault.

## To Reproduce

```python
import torch
t = torch.tensor([])
t.median()
```

## Expected behavior

Similar to other ops like `torch.mean()` or `torch.sum()` - attempting to find median of an empty tensor should cause a `RuntimeError` or otherwise produce a stack trace, rather than causing segfault.

## Environment

```shell

cc @ezyang @gchanan @zou3519 @bdhirsh @jbschlosser @anjali411 @heitorschueroff
comments:
[malfet]: Seems easy to fix, but I guess we need a better audit for those types of errors

---

[malfet]: Though, I guess the question is, what it should return? `nan` as `torch.mean` or exception, as `torch.quantile`

---

[heitorschueroff]: > Though, I guess the question is, what it should return? `nan` as `torch.mean` or exception, as `torch.quantile`

That is an interesting question. I think a more general question is what should we do with reductions over empty slices for operations that don't have an identity. For `mean` and `median` NumPy returns `nan` because these have no identity and the return type is float. For `max` and `min` NumPy raises an error saying these have no identity. For `quantile` NumPy also raises an error but it's not a descriptive one. Finally, `sum` and `prod` have identities so NumPy just returns 0 and 1 respectively. @mruberry  @rgommers do you think we should follow NumPy or always raise an exception for reductions over empty slices without identity? Is this something the Python Array API Standard specifies, or should specify?

---

[rgommers]: > Is this something the Python Array API Standard specifies, or should specify?

It's not specified (yet). I'd like the test suite to cover it. It may be useful to put it in the spec, but I'm not 100% sure - it may be a lot of boilerplate. Perhaps it could be a general design rule somewhere.


> @rgommers do you think we should follow NumPy or always raise an exception for reductions over empty slices without identity

I have the feeling we discussed this somewhere else recently, and the outcome was that an exception should only be raised if no sensible answer is possible. Which is consistent with what NumPy does now.

---

[Jup11ter]: It seems like there is an underlying implementation bug, `torch.nanmedian` also returns `Segmentation fault (core dumped)` in 1.9.0 versions when running the following codes.

In 1.10.0, by contrast, it can run without crashing. I think the reason is that [pr #61698](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/61698) also fixes the bug on `torch.nanmedian`.

```python
import torch

func_cls=torch.nanmedian
t = torch.tensor([])

def test():
	func_cls(t)
test()
```

title: PyTorch issue 62967
body:
## 🐛 Bug

SyncBatchNorm layers in torch 1.10.0 give different outputs on 2 gpus vs the equivalent BatchNorm layer on a single gpu. This wasn't a problem in torch 1.8.0

## To Reproduce
This code is based on the [PyTorch DDP demo](https://pytorch.org/tutorials/intermediate/ddp_tutorial.html) but the model consists only of a `BatchNorm2d` layer.

```python
import os
import torch
import torch.distributed as dist
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.multiprocessing as mp

from torch.nn.parallel import DistributedDataParallel as DDP
import argparse


def setup(rank, world_size):
    torch.manual_seed(0)
    os.environ["MASTER_ADDR"] = "localhost"
    os.environ["MASTER_PORT"] = "12355"

    # iniialize the process group
    dist.init_process_group("gloo", rank=rank, world_size=world_size)


def cleanup():
    dist.destroy_process_group()


class ToyModel(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, num_features=10):
        super().__init__()
        self.bn = nn.BatchNorm2d(num_features=num_features)

    def forward(self, x):
        x = x.permute(0, 2, 1, 3)
        x = self.bn(x)
        x = x.permute(0, 2, 1, 3)
        return x


def demo_basic(rank, world_size, convert_sync_batchnorm):
    print(f"Running basic DDP example on rank {rank}.")
    setup(rank, world_size)

    # create model and move it to GPU with id rank
    model = ToyModel(num_features=10)
    # create model and move it to GPU with id rank
    if world_size > 1 and convert_sync_batchnorm:
        print("Converting BatchNorm to SyncBatchNorm")
        model = torch.nn.SyncBatchNorm.convert_sync_batchnorm(model)

    model = model.to(rank)
    if world_size > 1:
        ddp_model = DDP(model, device_ids=[rank])
        model = ddp_model

    inputs = torch.randn(2, 2, 10, 1).to(rank)
    print(f"{rank=}, {inputs[0, 0].squeeze()=}")
    outputs = model(inputs)
    print(f"{rank=}, {outputs[0, 0].squeeze()=}")

    cleanup()


def run_demo(demo_fn, world_size, convert_bn):
    if world_size > 1:
        mp.spawn(demo_fn, args=(world_size, convert_bn), nprocs=world_size, join=True)
    else:
        demo_fn(0, world_size, convert_bn)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument("--n_gpus", default=2, type=int, help="Number of gpus")
    parser.add_argument(
        "--convert_bn",
        action="store_true",
        default=False,
        help="Whether to convert BatchNorm layers to SyncBatchNorm",
    )

    args = parser.parse_args()

    print(f"{torch.__version__=}")
    run_demo(demo_basic, args.n_gpus, args.convert_bn)
```

### Torch 1.10.0a0+git63955b3

#### 1 - 2 gpus, convert to `SyncBatchNorm`
```
python3 ddp_demo.py --n_gpus 2 --convert_bn
torch.__version__='1.10.0a0+git63955b3'
Running basic DDP example on rank 0.

#### Updated code
```python 
import os
import torch
import torch.distributed as dist
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.multiprocessing as mp

from torch.nn.parallel import DistributedDataParallel as DDP
import argparse


def setup(rank, world_size):
    torch.manual_seed(0)
    os.environ["MASTER_ADDR"] = "localhost"
    os.environ["MASTER_PORT"] = "12355"

    # iniialize the process group
    dist.init_process_group("gloo", rank=rank, world_size=world_size)


def cleanup():
    dist.destroy_process_group()


class ToyModel(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, num_features=10):
        super().__init__()
        self.bn = nn.BatchNorm2d(num_features=num_features)

    def forward(self, x):
        x = self.bn(x)
        return x


def demo_basic(rank, world_size, convert_sync_batchnorm, permute):
    print(f"Running basic DDP example on rank {rank}.")
    setup(rank, world_size)

    # create model and move it to GPU with id rank
    model = ToyModel(num_features=3)
    # create model and move it to GPU with id rank
    if world_size > 1 and convert_sync_batchnorm:
        print("Converting BatchNorm to SyncBatchNorm")
        model = torch.nn.SyncBatchNorm.convert_sync_batchnorm(model)

    model = model.to(rank)
    if world_size > 1:
        ddp_model = DDP(model, device_ids=[rank])
        model = ddp_model

    inputs = torch.randn(2, 3, 3, 1).to(rank)
    if permute:
        inputs = inputs.permute(0,2,1,3)
    print(f"{rank=}, {inputs[0].squeeze()=}")
    outputs = model(inputs)
    print(f"{rank=}, {outputs.squeeze()=}")

    cleanup()


def run_demo(demo_fn, world_size, convert_bn, permute):
    if world_size > 1:
        mp.spawn(demo_fn, args=(world_size, convert_bn, permute), nprocs=world_size, join=True)
    else:
        demo_fn(0, world_size, convert_bn, permute)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument("--n_gpus", default=2, type=int, help="Number of gpus")
    parser.add_argument(
        "--convert_bn",
        action="store_true",
        default=False,
        help="Whether to convert BatchNorm layers to SyncBatchNorm",
    )
    parser.add_argument(
        "--permute",
        action="store_true",
        default=False,
        help="Whether to convert BatchNorm layers to SyncBatchNorm",
    )
    args = parser.parse_args()

    print(f"{torch.__version__=}")
    run_demo(demo_basic, args.n_gpus, args.convert_bn, args.permute)
```

[erees1]: Thanks for the reply @ngimel - I have rebuilt off the latest commit 1.10.0a0+git3df4870 and am finding the discrepancy from this example has been fixed. I am still doing some digging as I am finding using `SyncBatchNorm` vs `BatchNorm2D` slightly degrades the performance of our models wheras in in torch 1.8.0 it slightly improved performance (same data, same models).

---

[ngimel]: cc @peterbell10. Implementation of syncBatchNorm was slightly changed in 1.9 as a part of generally improving speed of BatchNorm1d that shares the same kernels, but the difference should be within fp limits. If you have data to suggest otherwise, please share.

[peterbell10]: I have confirmed this is fixed in master, and that #62839 specifically was the fix.

The issue is that the input tensor has `shape=[2, 10, 2, 1]` and `strides=[20, 1, 10, 1]`. This doesn't strictly have the strides of a channels-last tensor (because of the last dimension) and so `suggest_memory_format` returns contiguous and a contiguous output is allocated. However,  `is_contiguous(MemoryFormat::ChannelsLast)` which is used to select the implementation did accept the tensor because it ignores the strides of length-1 dimensions. The end result is the channels last kernel being called with an NHCW contiguous output and so it's assuming the wrong strides.

So, the correctness bug is fixed but it's now using the slower generic kernel. I'll work on a PR to fix that.

title: PyTorch issue 66946
body:
## 🐛 Bug

`TransformedDistribution.icdf` should perform validation _after_ applying the inverse transformation rather than before.

## To Reproduce

Running the following snippet
```python
from math import log
import torch

class TorchLogUniform(torch.distributions.TransformedDistribution):
    def __init__(self, lb, ub):
        super(TorchLogUniform, self).__init__(
            torch.distributions.Uniform(lb.log(), ub.log()),
            torch.distributions.ExpTransform(),
        )

lu = TorchLogUniform(torch.tensor(1e8), torch.tensor(1e10))
lu.icdf(torch.tensor(0.1))
```
...produces this error:
```python
...
~/.pyenv/versions/3.9.2/envs/lens-3.9.2/lib/python3.9/site-packages/torch/distributions/distribution.py in _validate_sample(self, value)
    275         assert support is not None
    276         if not support.check(value).all():
--> 277             raise ValueError('The value argument must be within the support')
    278 
    279     def _get_checked_instance(self, cls, _instance=None):

ValueError: The value argument must be within the support
```

## Expected behavior

`icdf` should work for inputs between zero and 1.

The fix is simple: [this validation check](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/c371542efc31b1abfe6f388042aa3ab0cef935f2/torch/distributions/transformed_distribution.py#L184) should follow the `for` loop. I'd be happy to submit a PR.

cc @fritzo @neerajprad @alicanb @nikitaved
comments: [neerajprad]: Thanks for catching this. I don't think we need to validate here, but if we move it after the for loop, I think the check should be against the transformed distribution rather than the base distribution, `self._validate_sample(value)`. cc. @fritzo.

[ngimel]: Now it would make sense to check if AdaptiveMaxPool itself is producing correct results on the gpu, and if it does, how do subsequent conv/bn call change.

---

[xwang233]: ~~Let the backward of `adaptive_max_pool2d_backward` use contiguous path restores accuracy.~~

`adaptive_max_pool2d_backward` doesn't have a channels-last path on GPU. Set output of `adaptive_max_pool2d_backward` to contiguous restores accuracy.

~~From the provided code snippet, there is only a linear layer (which matters) after adaptive_max_pool2d https://gist.github.com/andrewilyas/ac844f3368c97e6d55e40209d2070557#file-train-py-L70-L73~~

```diff
diff --git a/aten/src/ATen/native/AdaptiveMaxPooling2d.cpp b/aten/src/ATen/native/AdaptiveMaxPooling2d.cpp
index bc9bc60b9d..cdf2613381 100644
--- a/aten/src/ATen/native/AdaptiveMaxPooling2d.cpp
+++ b/aten/src/ATen/native/AdaptiveMaxPooling2d.cpp
@@ -61,7 +61,7 @@ TORCH_META_FUNC(adaptive_max_pool2d_backward)
   TORCH_CHECK(input.dtype() == grad_output.dtype(),
     "expected dtype ", input.dtype(), " for `grad_output` but got dtype ", grad_output.dtype());
 
-  set_output(0, input.sizes(), input.options().memory_format(input.suggest_memory_format()));
+  set_output(0, input.sizes(), input.options().memory_format(at::MemoryFormat::Contiguous));
 }
 } // namespace meta

```

[xwang233]: For `AdaptiveMaxPool2d` layer with output size of `(1, 1)`, is it possible to add a shortcut to use 
```
input.max({-1, -2}, keepdim=True)
```
to improve the performance?

---

[ngimel]: Yes, we can use `max` to improve performance (we are using `mean` for adaptive average pooling in this case). 
If max pool backward itself produces correct result with channels last, the accuracy shouldn't change, there's a bug lurking somewhere, and we shouldn't hide it by making max pool contiguous. 
Edit: so do you mean that, since adaptive_max_pool2d_backward doesn't support channels last on the gpu, it just produces wrong result, reinterpreting output as contiguous? I wonder why disabling cudnn also helps accuracy then. 
But in this case we should fix adaptiv_max_pool2d on the gpu, probably by copying contiguous output to the channels-last output supplied by structured kernel, and add tests for it.

---

[xwang233]: Both `adaptive_max_pool2d` forward and backward implementations are likely wrong on CUDA.

Explain:
https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/8eb85b50277234e792d32f8f141f02d3b7c3042c/aten/src/ATen/native/cuda/AdaptiveMaxPooling2d.cu#L267-L271

The `adaptive_max_pool2d` CUDA kernel uses raw pointer instead of TensorAccessor. The kernel was probably written for output tensors of contiguous instead of channels_last, so passing a channels_last formatted pointer could yield wrong output.

https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/8eb85b50277234e792d32f8f141f02d3b7c3042c/aten/src/ATen/native/cuda/AdaptiveMaxPooling2d.cu#L56-L59

In the forward output kernel, the `input` pointer takes the advantages of the strides, but the `output` pointer assumes tensor is formatted as NCHW-contiguous.

Similar issues exist for backward impl and backward kernel.

Example:
```python

from torch.testing._core import _compare_tensors_internal

x = torch.randn(2, 3, 6, 6, device='cuda', dtype=torch.float)\
        .to(memory_format=torch.channels_last)

x_cpu = x.cpu()

out = torch.nn.functional.adaptive_max_pool2d(x, (2, 2))
out_cpu = torch.nn.functional.adaptive_max_pool2d(x_cpu, (2, 2))

_a, _b = _compare_tensors_internal(out.cpu(), out_cpu, rtol=1e-5, atol=1e-5, equal_nan=False)
if _a:
    print('good')
else:
    raise RuntimeError(_b)
```

RuntimeError: With rtol=1e-05 and atol=1e-05, found 20 element(s) (out of 24) whose difference(s) exceeded the margin of error (including 0 nan comparisons). The greatest difference was 2.4265955686569214 (3.1810925006866455 vs. 0.7544969320297241), which occurred at index (0, 0, 1, 0).

title: PyTorch issue 67626
body:
RRefProxy (`rref.rpc_async`, `rref.rpc_sync`, `rref.remote`) currently uses a blocking RPC call to the owner 


https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/885a8e53bab50b1ac53e6c140cc0a9953e7e288e/torch/distributed/rpc/rref_proxy.py#L15-L19

https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/885a8e53bab50b1ac53e6c140cc0a9953e7e288e/torch/distributed/rpc/api.py#L409-L419

This violates the promise of `rpc_async` and `remote` being non-blocking. Instead, we should use the async call, and chain the remaining code in `_invoke_rpc` as a callback.

title: PyTorch issue 71063
body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

After upgrading pytorch version from 1.10.1 to the master branch (commit [d697bb4](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/commit/d697bb4220ff5ea8b46591cc082583bbf1a294a4)), `torch.utils.data._utils.collate.default_collate` raises the below noisy warning repeatedly:

```
UserWarning: An output with one or more elements was resized since it had shape [10], which does not
match the required output shape [2, 5].This behavior is deprecated, and in a future PyTorch release outputs will
not be resized unless they have zero elements. You can explicitly reuse an out tensor t by resizing it, inplace,
to zero elements with t.resize_(0).
```
The warning is original from [Resize.cpp](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/master/aten/src/ATen/native/Resize.cpp#L11-L27) and triggered by [these lines](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/master/torch/utils/data/_utils/collate.py#L130-L138) of `default_colloate`:

```
    elem = batch[0]
    elem_type = type(elem)
    if isinstance(elem, torch.Tensor):
        out = None
        if torch.utils.data.get_worker_info() is not None:
            # If we're in a background process, concatenate directly into a
            # shared memory tensor to avoid an extra copy
            numel = sum(x.numel() for x in batch)
            storage = elem.storage()._new_shared(numel)
            out = elem.new(storage)
        return torch.stack(batch, 0, out=out)
```

In case `default_collate` runs in background/worker process (torch.utils.data.get_worker_info() returns something), `out` is always a 1D tensor, but `batch` may has higher rank, thus a resize operation happens inside the `torch.stack` operation, which triggers the warning.

### Versions

Master branch (commit [d697bb4](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/commit/d697bb4220ff5ea8b46591cc082583bbf1a294a4))

# To Reproduce
Use this code snippet to reproduce the problem:

```
import torch

a, b = torch.randn([5]), torch.randn([5])
batch = [a, b]
elem = batch[0]

# In case torch.utils.data.get_worker_info() is not None
numel = sum(x.numel() for x in batch)
storage = elem.storage()._new_shared(numel)
out = elem.new(storage)

torch.stack(batch, 0, out=out)
```

# Expected behavior
No warning raises.

# Workaround

```
    elem = batch[0]
    elem_type = type(elem)
    if isinstance(elem, torch.Tensor):
        out = None
        if torch.utils.data.get_worker_info() is not None:
            # If we're in a background process, concatenate directly into a
            # shared memory tensor to avoid an extra copy
            numel = sum(x.numel() for x in batch)
            storage = elem.storage()._new_shared(numel)

            # reshape `out` before passing to torch.stack
            out = elem.new(storage).view(-1, *list(elem.size()))  

        return torch.stack(batch, 0, out=out)
```

Test code:

```
import torch

a, b = torch.randn([5]), torch.randn([5])
batch = [a, b]
elem = batch[0]

# In case torch.utils.data.get_worker_info() is not None
numel = sum(x.numel() for x in batch)
storage = elem.storage()._new_shared(numel)
out = elem.new(storage).view(-1, *list(elem.size()))

torch.stack(batch, 0, out=out)
```

cc @VitalyFedyunin @ejguan @NivekT

title: PyTorch issue 71089
body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

`torch.unique(torch.tensor([float("nan")]), dim=0)` yields a segfault on PyTorch 1.10 while working fine on 1.9.

### To reproduce the issue

```python
# segfaults in torch 1.10, works as expected in torch 1.9:
tensor_nan = torch.tensor([float("nan")])
assert torch.unique(tensor_nan, dim=0).numel() == 1
assert torch.isnan(torch.unique(tensor_nan, dim=0)[0])
```

### Additional comments

Note that when leaving `dim` to its default (`None`), the segfault does not occur, i.e.:
```python
# works as expected in both torch 1.10 and 1.9:
tensor_nan = torch.tensor([float("nan")])
assert torch.unique(tensor_nan, dim=None).numel() == 1
assert torch.isnan(torch.unique(tensor_nan, dim=None)[0])
```

The problem does not occur for `float("inf")`, i.e.:
```python
# works as expected in both torch 1.10 and 1.9:
tensor_inf = torch.tensor([float("inf")])
assert torch.unique(tensor_inf, dim=0).numel() == 1
assert torch.isinf(torch.unique(tensor_inf, dim=0)[0])
```
