title: PyTorch issue 71280
body:
### 🐛 Describe the bug

In pytorch/torch_version.py, we do `from pkg_resources import packaging`.

https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/83b45fe1666fd8ecc697078ca99ff46dabe0286b/torch/torch_version.py#L3

This causes two problems:
- We have an undeclared runtime dependency on setuptools. Currently pytorch only has declared dependencies on typing_extensions and dataclasses: https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/83b45fe1666fd8ecc697078ca99ff46dabe0286b/setup.py#L409
- pkg_resources is a really slow import. It takes a full 1s on my laptop, sometimes longer depending on disk cache. Moreover, we're not actually using pkg_resources itself, we're using pkg_resource's vendored version of https://github.com/pypa/packaging

Possible fixes:
- Declare a dependency on `packaging` and replace `from pkg_resources import packaging` with `import packaging.version`. This makes torch imports much faster! :-)
- Don't declare a dependency on `packaging`, but try to import it, and fallback to `from pkg_resources import packaging`. This doesn't fix the undeclared dependency, but it does make `import torch` faster.
Another variation on this is to try to steal vendored `packaging` from a package that imports faster, e.g. `pip._vendor.packaging.version`.  We could combine that with fallback to `pkg_resources` as well.
- Vendor the appropriate code from `packaging.version`

### Versions

1.10.1

cc @malfet @seemethere
comments:
[malfet]: Thank you very much for writing a detailed issue.

> * pkg_resources is a really slow import. It takes a full 1s on my laptop, sometimes longer depending on disk cache. 

Can you please try running something like `time python3 -c "print('Hello')"; time python3 -c "from pkg_resources import packaging; print('Hello')` and share the output?
On my laptop, running python3 takes 0.03 sec and running python that imports pkg_resources takes 0.17 sec, which is indeed pretty bad, but nowhere close to 1 sec

---

[malfet]: Adding `triage review` to discuss, which label should be used for such cases, as `module: build` as well as `module: binaries` feels wrong in this case

---

[hauntsaninja]: It varies by a) how many packages you have in your environment, b) state of disk cache

`pkg_resources` calls https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/blob/4f9ebdbe81cb81a75c94a93cc53551c558dfc5e7/pkg_resources/__init__.py#L3269 at import time and it basically goes through everything in your venv (e.g. see the contents of `pkg_resources.working_set.__dict__`).

This is quite commonly a problem, e.g. see https://github.com/pypa/setuptools/issues/510 (which also reports numbers in the range 1s-1.5s)

I tried your command in the virtual environment of the large project I work on, this is the first result (0.7s):
```
λ time python3 -c "print('Hello')"; time python3 -c "from pkg_resources import packaging; print('Hello')"
Hello
python3 -c "print('Hello')"  0.03s user 0.01s system 91% cpu 0.047 total
Hello
python3 -c "from pkg_resources import packaging; print('Hello')"  0.25s user 0.15s system 58% cpu 0.690 total
```

---

[hauntsaninja]: I'm also happy to provide a PR if one of those options sounds good to you :-)

---

[albanD]: > Vendor the appropriate code from packaging.version

Given that we use these classes for isinstance check. I don't think this would work.

For the solution I think that trying to import packaging and fallback to the slower import is ok to do. We already do this kind of things for our yaml loader for example [here](https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/88012c7daf019d0f6a11a744c03fb63231b84830/tools/codegen/utils.py#L12-L16). @malfet would you be happy with this solution?

---

[malfet]: @albanD it would solve rougue dependency, but would not solve a perf penalty everyone have to pay for something very rarely used

---

[albanD]: Ho that can be solved by lazy loading the package (or just inline the import inside the methods). The import only creates an instance of our class which doesn't require this package at all. We just import it whenever the user tries to do any comparison. I verified locally that `import torch` indeed never does that.

---

[malfet]: @albanD yeah, thats the idea, but I'm debating how to do that without rendering the code too ugly. https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/71316 is somewhat borderline

---

[seemethere]: > Ho that can be solved by lazy loading the package (or just inline the import inside the methods). The import only creates an instance of our class which doesn't require this package at all. We just import it whenever the user tries to do any comparison. I verified locally that `import torch` indeed never does that.

Lazy load might work but since `torch/torch_version.py` gets imported in the root as part of `import torch` it'll just load it every time anyways.

---

[hauntsaninja]: > Given that we use these classes for isinstance check. I don't think this would work.

But the class used is pkg_resources vendored packaging, not packaging itself. So I think this is mostly an implementation detail; users aren't going to be using the class from pkg_resourced vendored packaging. As long as `_convert_to_version` returns the same kind of class, we should be fine.

```
In [1]: import torch

In [2]: torch.__version__
Out[2]: '1.10.1'

In [3]: type(torch.__version__)
Out[3]: torch.torch_version.TorchVersion

In [4]: import packaging.version

In [5]: packaging.version.Version("1.10.2") > torch.__version__
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
TypeError                                 Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-5-067714c3f7be> in <module>
----> 1 packaging.version.Version("1.10.2") > torch.__version__

TypeError: '>' not supported between instances of 'Version' and 'TorchVersion'

In [6]: from pkg_resources import packaging

In [7]: packaging.version.Version("1.10.2") > torch.__version__
Out[7]: True
```

---

[albanD]: > Lazy load might work but since torch/torch_version.py gets imported in the root as part of import torch it'll just load it every time anyways.

We don't actually use any of these functions on import. So a lazy load would not trigger during the import (I checked by removing the import and importing torch, that works fine).

> @albanD yeah, thats the idea, but I'm debating how to do that without rendering the code too ugly. #71316 is somewhat borderline

It is borderline as is indeed. I like the `_get_pandas()` idea though as it removes a lot of copy pasted error checking in the actual code.
You can even avoid the function call by making it an object instance with a custom getattr that does the lazy init.

---

[hauntsaninja]: Thanks for the fix!

Using `pkg_resources` will still be slow if you do `torch.__version__`. I think there'd still be value in doing:
```
diff --git a/torch/torch_version.py b/torch/torch_version.py
index a7aa6764b5..667c17980b 100644
--- a/torch/torch_version.py
+++ b/torch/torch_version.py
@@ -18,7 +18,10 @@ class _LazyImport:
         self._cls_name = cls_name
 
     def get_cls(self):
-        from pkg_resources import packaging  # type: ignore[attr-defined]
+        try:
+            import packaging.version
+        except ImportError:
+            from pkg_resources import packaging  # type: ignore[attr-defined]
         return getattr(packaging.version, self._cls_name)
 
     def __call__(self, *args, **kwargs):
```

Above you'd mentioned reluctance to do this since we use `isinstance`. But if anything, users will use `Version` from `packaging`, not from the secret vendored version of `packaging` in `pkg_resources`. For instance, see:

```
In [1]: import torch

In [2]: import packaging.version

In [3]: torch.__version__
Out[3]: '1.10.1'

In [4]: type(torch.__version__)
Out[4]: torch.torch_version.TorchVersion

In [5]: torch.__version__ > packaging.version.Version("1.10.0")
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
TypeError                                 Traceback (most recent call last)
Input In [5], in <module>
----> 1 torch.__version__ > packaging.version.Version("1.10.0")

TypeError: '>' not supported between instances of 'TorchVersion' and 'Version'
```

---

[malfet]: @hauntsaninja this sounds good to me, please do not hesitate to propose a fix like that and I will gladly review it

[ssnl]: This is valid. I added synchronization to the script and got the following timings:
```
.argmax()              : 36.8158 ms
.cpu().argmax()        : 6.53611 ms 
.cpu().numpy().argmax(): 2.87929 ms
```

script: https://gist.github.com/SsnL/6898c240d22faa91da16fc41359756a2

---

[ailzhang]: Talked to @soumith , will delay this until @colesbury 's changes are merged.

---

[tbenst]: @colesbury any update on this? Appreciate the work!

---

[tbenst]: @ailzhang do you know if @colesbury 's changes were merged by chance? Thanks for your attention!

---

[fmassa]: @tbenst Sam's changes have been merged, via the TensorIterator reductions. But argmax hasn't been touched since then

---

[prasunanand]: Got a blocker here. `filters = torch.rand(8,300,300,2).cuda()` in reproducer code doesn't work with CUDA10.1 

Already reported in issue:
https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/15296

---

[prasunanand]: Switching to CUDA 9.

---

[prasunanand]: This issue has been fixed already. 

Running @SsnL 's script I get the following.
```sh
(base) prasun@prasun-xps:~/dev$ python argmax_bench2.py 
27.332282066345215
2.1524667739868164
2.2240757942199707
```

---

[ssnl]: @prasunanand The CPU perf seems to be addressed. But CUDA perf is still pretty bad :(

---

[prasunanand]: OK, I am looking into it

---

[prasunanand]: I think it's a deadlock issue. 
`THCudaTensor_max(globalContext().getTHCState(), max_, max_indices_, self_, dim, keepdim)`;
If I comment out this line, the code gets fast.

This line of code is in `build/aten/src/ATen/LegacyTHfunctionsCUDA.cpp` and its a legacy function.

Might be related to https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/issues/9784

cc: @ezyang

---

[ssnl]: @prasunanand if you comment out that line the function does literally nothing because that line calls the implementation kernels.

---

[prasunanand]: Yeah, but is it possible that `globalContext().getTHCState()`  might be causing a slow down.

---

[prasunanand]: `idx = conv.reshape(-1).max()` runs really fast compared to `idx = conv.argmax()`.

---

[ssnl]: You can try to benchmark that by breaking it into two lines and using some cpp benchmarking. But I highly doubt that it is the issue because it exists in so many cuda function calls.

---

[ssnl]: That is because they call different kernels (tensor.max vs tensor.argmax). The method .max doesn’t compute the index.

---

[prasunanand]: If you look at [link]( https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/f87a4cc23fae6b1561d35103149c4ce5ccdabe76/aten/src/ATen/native/TensorCompare.cpp#L230) , this is what's going on internally.

---

[fmassa]: @prasunanand `torch.max` has two overloads:
- when you pass a dimension, it computes and returns the indices of the argmax as well
- when you don't pass a dimension, it just performs `max` globally over the tensor, without storing a dimension.

Compare the following:
```python
a = torch.rand(10000, device='cuda')

%timeit a.max()
34.3 µs ± 514 ns per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10000 loops each)

%timeit a.max(0)
122 µs ± 0.751 ns per loop (mean ± std. dev. of 7 runs, 10000 loops each)
```

---

[prasunanand]: argmax calls `self.reshape({-1}).max()`[0]

The max step is very slow. Here the kernel that is called is `THC_transformReduceInnermostDimIndex`[1]
`THCTensor_(max)`[2] calls `THC_reduceDimIndex`[3] which calls `THC_transformReduceInnermostDimIndex`
The issue with `THC_reduceDimIndex` `THC_transformReduceInnermostDimIndex`
is that the loop that runs is pretty slow. The threads are scheduled in a manner that
memory access is not coalesced. The jump along grid access makes the loop really slow.

And in the code in `THC_reduceDimIndex` we see that num_rows is explicitly set to 1
before calling the kernel function and when the kernel is called, the loop checks
for stop condition as `block_row < num_rows`.

I am not sure how to go ahead with this.

[0]. https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/dc870a3761ecf15d3ad6ba7a1628b37b8a0be116/aten/src/ATen/native/TensorCompare.cpp#L233
[1]. https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/5bf299b1408356a4339580524aaab5ad435fccb8/aten/src/THC/THCTensorMathReduce.cuh#L494
[2]. https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/dc870a3761ecf15d3ad6ba7a1628b37b8a0be116/aten/src/THC/generic/THCTensorMathReduce.cu#L20
[3]. https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/5bf299b1408356a4339580524aaab5ad435fccb8/aten/src/THC/THCTensorMathReduce.cuh#L526

---

[ezyang]: cc @ngimel @colesbury if you have any thoughts on ways forward (or if we should just not care about this)

---

[ngimel]: All the reductions should be moved to TensorIterator, see also https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/pull/21295#issuecomment-499241569, that should fix performance issues. Not caring is also an option, but i don't know how big of a bottleneck this is.

---

[fmassa]: Moving to TensorIterator is definitely a better strategy, but it will require reworking how Tensoriterator works to support the argmax (and sort, topk, etc) cases, which are not just reductions but requires to keep track of indices and values

---

[ngimel]: Hopefully changes can be localized around here https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/a0b13b4fa55f4a42fc934e1dde52989a40b2c87b/aten/src/ATen/native/cuda/Reduce.cuh#L366-L404 where the data is loaded. `values` array is hardcoded to be `scalar_t` type, but for argmin/argmax reduction it would have to hold both index and value. All higher level reductions apply `combine` operation to arguments of `arg_t` type and that should be able to handle argmin/argmax reduction if `arg_t` is (index, value) pair.

title: PyTorch issue 973
body:

comments:
[apaszke]: My guess would be that one of the workers quit before the main process has managed to receive some tensors form it.

---

[jfsantos]: Yes, that could be the case, since I'm doing some expensive operations inside that loop. Is there any way of specifying a timeout for the workers?

---

[apaszke]: No, there's not, but it should be unnecessary. They should be stopped only once the main process receives and returns all batches. What arguments did you pass in to the DataLoader constructor (other than the dataset)?

---

[jfsantos]: I'm using `batch_size=1, collate_fn=MyDataset.collate_samples, num_workers=1, shuffle=False`. The `collate_samples` function is only used to concatenate things that are not tensors.

---

[apaszke]: It would be great if you could try to get us a small script that would reproduce it. I'm only afraid that it might be caused by some specific case of interleaving subprocess execution, that will be different on our hardware. But should be worth trying anyway 😕

---

[jfsantos]: I kind of worked around this by setting `num_workers=0` until now since I was only having the issue during test time, but now I started seeing this during validation, which is pretty bad. I will try to reproduce it and will let you know if I manage to do it.

[skaae]: I have the same problem. I can get rid of the problem by having my collate_fn return numpy arrays instead of Torch tensors. I.e. commenting the `from_numpy` lines removes the problem. I also get the problem if i create the tensor with `torch.FloatTensor(some_np_arr)`. 

```Python

    def collate_fn(batch):
        org_audio_len_lst, audio_lst, spec_linear_lst, spec_mel_lst, speaker_class_lst, sample_info_lst = zip(*batch)
        audio_arr = np.stack(audio_lst,0).astype('float32')

        audio_arr = torch.from_numpy(audio_arr)
```

[apaszke]: That's because numpy arrays are pickled and unpickled instead of using shared memory. There are probably very tight limits on the shared memory size in your systems, or the workers quit in a weird way. Does it happen at the end of an epoch?

In my setup I load 512 samples to memory at the beginning of training. The samples are stored in list. 
The 512 samples in memory are periodically replaced randomly with new samples loaded from disk. 

Is that the problem and do you have workaround ? :)

```python
import torch

def create_one(batch_size):

pool = torch.multiprocessing.Pool(torch.multiprocessing.cpu_count())
data = pool.map(create_one, torch.LongTensor(1000).fill_(100))
```

```
pool = torch.multiprocessing.Pool(torch.multiprocessing.cpu_count(), maxtasksperchild=1)
```

---

[sampathweb]: Increasing the `ulimit` solved the issue.  Details (https://github.com/fastai/fastai/issues/23).  When using Docker, the `ulimit` is set very high so you may never see the issue, but Ubuntu instance by default had 1024 (file descriptor limit).  Increasing it to 2048 or 4096 or higher solves the issue.  I don't know why that many file descriptors are required, but glad that it had a simple fix.

---

[sampathweb]: If the core devs want to see the error, just reduce the `ulimit` to 1024 and run the code of @kamo-naoyuki above and you might see the same problem.

---

[jph00]: This does seem to be an issue in pytorch as best as we can tell - FDs aren't being released when they should be. As @sampathweb mentioned, it's reproducible using the above code. The workaround for now is:

```
import resource
rlimit = resource.getrlimit(resource.RLIMIT_NOFILE)
resource.setrlimit(resource.RLIMIT_NOFILE, (2048, rlimit[1]))
```
(You may need to set 2048 to a higher number.)

---

[apaszke]: And if you can't change the limits because you don't have super user access, you can always switch to [file name based IPC](http://pytorch.org/docs/master/multiprocessing.html#file-system-file-system).

---

[jph00]: BTW we fixed this problem (and got a good speedup, and also fixed some occassional multiprocessing hangs) in fastai by switching to ThreadPool. Requires libs that release the GIL of course, but opencv does that. :)

---

[apaszke]: Yeah I saw that. I don't think we will want to do this in general, as some datasets might be more GIL-heavy and this would have a huge perf hit, but an option that makes the data loader use threads instead of subprocesses should be quite easy to implement.

---

[sunhs]: I'm faced with this problem too. In my code I did:

```
from multiprocessing import pool
p = pool.Pool()
p.map(preprocess, images)
```

where `images` are PIL Image objects and `preprocess` is a function to transform the PIL Image into a FloatTensor. This yields the same exception as above. When I change the ulimit or replace the `pool.map` with a normal `for loop` everything is good.

I'm just curious about why the combination of `p.map` and `torch.FloatTensor` would have something to do with the file descriptor, while I'm not explicitly doing anything about shared memory.

---

[apaszke]: Tensors are always passed through shared memory. Try switching to the file name based sharing, or increase the limit of file descriptors per process using `ulimit`

[Avsecz]: @ivanprado great catch. In my case, this occurred during test-time since I was keeping track of the target values from all the batches (hence many shared objects had to be tracked). I solved the problem by copying the objects using `copy.deepcopy` and removing the shared objects. This doesn't happen during training time since the targets get discarded right after the SGD update (hence only a few shared objects need to be tracked).

Here is my solution

```python
from copy import deepcopy

pred_list = []
target_list = []
# long version
for inputs, targets in DataLoader(dataset, num_workers=6, batch_size=64):
    pred_list.append(model.predict_on_batch(inputs))  # make model prediction
    targets_copy = deepcopy(targets)
    target_list.append(targets_copy)
    del inputs
    del targets

# short version. interpreter invokes `del` automatically for inputs and targets
for inputs, targets in DataLoader(dataset, num_workers=6, batch_size=64):
    pred_list.append(model.predict_on_batch(inputs))  # make model prediction
    target_list.append(deepcopy(targets))  # append targets copy. `targets` removed automatically
```

Doing so, I was able to run the code without `torch.multiprocessing.set_sharing_strategy('file_system')`.

[Baranowski]: I reproduced the `received 0 items of ancdata` error but couldn't reproduce the FD leak that IvanPrado diagnosed (except the expected scenario where references to the SHM tensors are kept alive outside the DataLoader loop). I think it's fair to ignore the latter part of this issue unless it's observed in the wild again. My rationale:

* It's been almost 2 years so even if there was a bug causing leaks in PyTorch, it might have been fixed since.
* It's possible that the user's code was keeping the SHM tensors alive longer than necessary by maintaining reference to them outside the DataLoader loop. (To be fair, I didn't find any such mistake reading his code, or any other proof of that, but it's possible that we have both missed something.)

If we agree to disregard the report of FD leaks, this issue is reduced to the obscure `RuntimeError: received 0 items of ancdata` exception that the users see.

(**Suggestion 1**) IMO the best improvement would be for the parent process to report the stderr of the crashed workers (or to wrap the worker in a try...catch that gracefully kills the worker and somehow reports the exception back to the parent).

(**Suggestion 2**) It might be also useful to warn the user when a SHM tensor outlives the DataLoader's itearator's iteration. I'm not sure how doable that is with the Python's GC though..

The obscure error `RuntimeError: received 0 items of ancdata` is not a good user experience but it's not terrible since typing it into Google leads to a lot of pages with the workaround that's easily applied. As much as I would like to work on Suggestion 1, perhaps the `high priority` label should be reconsidered at a triage meeting.

@ezyang or @apaszke, any opinions on this? TL;DR: I suggest we disregard the report of FD leaks and improve the user's experience with the `RuntimeError: received 0 items of ancdata` error by having the parent process report the reason for a crash of a worker.

---

[ezyang]: In general it's good for making sure we can accurately report crashed workers (although I wonder why we are suppressing the child stderr stuff to begin with). Do you know if it's just a situation where the child process is dying due to a signal or something? The "check if shm tensors leak out of dataloader" is a nifty idea and is technically possible to do (e.g., loop over all objects known to Python GC) but I'm not sure if we should actually try to offer this by default.

In any case, I'm happy to let you keep working on this; this has affected a lot of people and it would be good to nail it.

---

[Baranowski]: Sorry for the late response. I didn't know the answer to your question.

As I mentioned in the PR above, turns out the worker is not crashing - the socket is silently stripping a FD from a message when passing it would exceed the open file limit. The worker is oblivious to this.

title: PyTorch issue 9922
body:
## Issue description
Are there any methods to get the conv module input params from the ‘grad_fn’ in the newest version of pytorch?

in pytorch 0.2, we can get convolution parameters, such as kernel_size, padding, and stride from grad_fn, this way is very useful to convert the trained model to caffe prototxt.
but, in pytorch 0.4, the ThnnConv2DBackward and CudnnConvolutionBackward  do not support this way.

for example,in pytorch version 0.2,

## Code example
modle = nn.Conv2d(in_channels=3 out_channels=32, kernel_size=3,stride=2, padding=1, dilation=1))
y = modle(x)

Then, we can get the input param’s values like kernel_size、stride、padding value use y.grad_fn.kernel_size，y.grad_fn.padding,……

but now, in pytorch 0.4, it report that 
"AttributeError: 'CudnnConvolutionBackward' object has no attribute 'kernel_size'"

cc @ezyang @SsnL @albanD @zou3519 @gqchen
comments:
[zou3519]: cc @apaszke what do you think?

---

[Weyne168]: I want to convert the parameters trained by PyTorch 0.4.0 to prototxt of caffe and caffemodel

---

[daodao316]: Have you solved this problem?

---

[blueardour]: +1

---

[zym1119]: i find the same problem as well
when print dir(out.grad_fn)
in 0.2.0 you will get 
```
['__call__', '__class__', '__delattr__', '__doc__', '__format__', '__getattribute__', '__hash__', '__init__', '__new__', '__reduce__', '__reduce_ex__', '__repr__', '__setattr__', '__sizeof__', '__str__', '__subclasshook__', '_register_hook_dict', 'dilation', 'groups', 'next_functions', 'output_padding', 'padding', 'register_hook', 'stride', 'transposed'] 
```
which has many parameters in convolution

in 0.4.0 you will get
```
['__call__', '__class__', '__delattr__', '__dir__', '__doc__', '__eq__', '__format__', '__ge__', '__getattribute__', '__gt__', '__hash__', '__init__', '__le__', '__lt__', '__ne__', '__new__', '__reduce__', '__reduce_ex__', '__repr__', '__setattr__', '__sizeof__', '__str__', '__subclasshook__', '_register_hook_dict', 'metadata', 'next_functions', 'register_hook', 'requires_grad']
```
i can only get weights and bias from next_functions

---

[albanD]: This behavior is due to the fact that the conv Function was moved from python code to cpp code for efficiency.
So all the parameters are now passed directly to the cpp Functionsand are not accessible from python.

---

[blueardour]: Hi, @albanD  Thank you for pointing out the reason. I wonder if it is still possible to export the kernel size, stride... parameters from the cpp code for conv or pooling layer?  Would the export impact the elegance of the code?  I'm not quite familiar with whole pytorch code currently, even though I'm struggling to read it. Could you talk a bit more about why the parameters are not likely to be available after moving into cpp code?

---

[yxchng]: Any updates on this?

---

[zou3519]: I don't think we have a plan to expose grad_fn internal in Python. Given the amount of support on this issue, though, we are open to reconsidering.

---

[blueardour]: Hi, I original focused on the issue because I want to convert pytorch model to caffe. I noticed some other people (for example @Weyne168) in this thread also had the same request.

As the thread was not updated for a while, I later found another solution to finish the task. The hint is to convert pytorch to onnx first and then convert onnx to caffe.  Thus, from my aspect, I might not require the feature anymore. 
Maybe still there's  demand for the feature from other people.  Just bless~     ^_^

[taoari]: For those who are still interested. One of the possible solutions:

```python

import torch
import torch.nn as nn

def hook(module, inputs, output):
    if torch.is_tensor(output):
        if 'module' not in output.grad_fn.metadata:
            output.grad_fn.metadata['module'] = module
        if 'output' not in output.grad_fn.metadata:
            output.grad_fn.metadata['output'] = output
            
def register_forward_hooks(model, hook):
    for name, mod in model.named_modules():
        if not mod._modules: # is a leaf module
            mod.register_forward_hook(hook)    
        
model = nn.Conv2d(10, 10, 3)
register_forward_hooks(model, hook)
inputs = (torch.randn(2,10,32,32),)
output = model(*inputs)
print(output.grad_fn.metadata['module'].kernel_size)
print(output.grad_fn.metadata['output'].shape)
```

Really hope that there is an official way to trace back module and output shape info from grad_fn, e.g. `grad_fn.module` to retrieve all the module information.
